Prompt: Generate a Level 1 terminal training scenario for San Francisco International Airport (SFO). The scenario should begin at 1030 UTC and focus on evolving weather conditions: low ceilings filling in within a few hours and a wind shift to the southeast (190@10G15) between 14-15Z. The simulation must include a disagreement during the Ground Delay Program (GDP) telcon: ZOA and the TRACON should advocate for an early 14Z start at a 28 rate to avoid holding, while United Airlines (UAL) should push for a 15Z start based on their own weather forecast. Provide specific TAF/METAR sequences and scripted dialogue for the instructor and facility representatives.
Scenario:
Training script:
The scenario will start with the student assuming the position for training at 1030UTC. They will be given the TAF/METAR for the Terminal West Position Airports and have access to the FSM and TSD.
After the student evaluates issues at their position the instructor will ask…
Instructor: What airports do you expect will see issues for the shift today?
The only airport needing attention will be SFO.
The weather Specialist delivers the 11Z weather sheets and gives you the following briefing.
Weather Specialist: Low ceilings will begin to fill in over SFO sometime in the next couple of hours. Currently, winds are calm, but expect the winds to pick up from the SE between 14 -15Z. Winds are expected to be 190@10G15. Low ceilings may scatter out around 20Z when the winds are forecasted to shift and blow out of the west. (TAF should still show cloud layers through 23Z) Rain is forecasted through 19Z and we should get to VFR by 23 – 00Z.

Here is what the weather sequence for today:

     FM171300 14009KT P6SM -SHRA BKN020 OVC035
       TEMPO 1713/1717 4SM -RA BKN015 OVC025
     FM171800 23014G20KT VCSH SCT015 BKN025 OVC035
     FM172300 26012KT P6SM VCSH P6SM FEW025 SCT035
     FM180600 25016G22KT P6SM -SHRA BKN025 OVC035
 

Student should be given opportunity to use all TM tools available to determine a course of action.
Instructor: What are your ideas regarding SFO?
Options: Wait or SFO will need a Ground Delay Program.
		
•	If GDP  
o	Instructor: Can you model an SFO GDP based on the weather sequence provided by DCC weather?
Instructor: That’s a good starting point. What are you going to do next?
Options:  Wait, Talk to UAL Weather, or hold a SFO GDP Telcon. 
Here is the Updated SFO WX from DCC WX Unit:
KSFO 171148Z 1712/1818 19009KT P6SM VCSH BKN025 OVC040
     FM171300 14009KT P6SM -RA BKN015 OVC025
     FM171800 23014G20KT VCSH SCT015 BKN025 OVC035
     FM172300 26012KT P6SM VCSH P6SM FEW025 SCT035
     FM180600 25016G22KT P6SM -SHRA BKN025 OVC035

Some trainers will teach to hold a telcon with FAA facilities only first, and some will teach to hold the full telcon. Here is what each element will say regardless of when they speak to them. 
ZOA WX – Low ceilings will be in place by 1430 – 1445Z winds will be out of the SE through 20 – 21Z, then expect a shift to westerly winds. Rain showers are expected through 20Z. Clouds will decrease by 20Z, but don’t expect VFR conditions until 2330 or possibly 00Z. 
UAL WX – We don’t see the ceilings coming in until 1500Z. We do see the wind shift occurring around 20Z and the possibility of VFR ceilings at that time. 
SFO – Right now we are in a West Configuration. 28 Left will be back by 14Z. We expect to be in a SE configuration, arriving 19L and departing both 10L/R as the winds start to blow out of the SE. If the winds do shift to the West as forecasted, we will be able to move to a 28/10 operation. 
NCT – Right now we are at a 40 rate. When we shift to a SE configuration, we will be at a 28 rate and will stay there until we are able to get to a West configuration.  When in West configuration, we will be at a 36 rate until we get to VFR conditions and can raise the rate to 40. I’m concerned with the timing of the IFR ceilings.  When this happens we will need to shift operations and go to a 28 rate. I suggest we be cautious and plan to be at the 28 rate by 14Z to avoid holding. It will be better for the operation if we get to a 19/10 Operation prior to the start of the GDP rather than during. 
ZOA – We agree with the TRACON. We would like to see the program start at 14Z at a 28 rate to avoid a holding and possible Ground Stop.
UAL – According to my weather people, the ceilings will not be an issue until 15Z at least. How confident are you about that?  
UAL WX – The ceilings are moving west at 20 kts. I have medium confidence on the forecast. If the winds pick up earlier, it could move the ceilings in sooner.  
UAL – I think we could wait to start any program until 15Z but understand starting at 1400Z. 
DAL – We will defer to UAL on this. 
ASA – We disagree. We do not want to see any holding, Ground Stops or Metering. We think we should start the program at 14Z at the 28 rate.  
Instructor – Why do you think ASA feels this way? (Most of ASA fleet comes from SFO to the first tier, so they have the most to lose if SFO goes into a Ground Stop or has to meter on top of the GDP.)

The student will need to take all this information into account and decide how to precede. 
Options: Go with facilities recommendation or UAL’s recommendation. 
If student asks DCC weather for an updated forecast, DCC weather will support the ZOA WX’s forecast. 
Student will then model their SFO GDP. (Remember, this will be a long GDP and the student should expect some cancelations. They should run the GDP with a stack to account for cancelations and reduce the Average Delay.) After they have finished modeling their program, Instructor should ask the student the Pros and Cons of each option and what led to their decision. 
Student will then send out the SFO GDP Proposal. 
The student will hold the GDP telcon when stated on the proposal. We want to see if the student can explain and defend their position on why they decided to propose the GDP they did. We want to object as the opposite side to the proposal. If the student sides with the facilities, UAL should say they are taking unnecessary delay by starting the program an hour early. If the student goes with UAL’s proposal, then TRACON and Center, as well as Alaska, should chime in saying that the risk of a Ground Stop and revision is too great to start the GDP when UAL wants. 
After the telcon, student will send out the GDP and make the correct calls to notify the Centers.

Include a conflict where ZAU expresses concern about gate blockages while C90 wants to stay 'wide open' as long as possible. Script the telcon to include airline requests for lower rates (e.g., 56) to limit diversions.

Prompt: Generate a Level 3 terminal scenario for LaGuardia (LGA) starting at 1000 UTC. Ultimately, a low rate (20-rate capacity during the runway change) will be required. Include coordination scripts for N90 and instructions for managing impacts on Teterboro (TEB) arrivals.
Scenario:
Training script:

The scenario will start with the student assuming the position for training at 1000 UTC. They will be given the TAF/METAR for the Terminal East Position Airports and have access to the FSM and TSD.

Low ceilings with gusty winds and thunderstorms forecasted for LGA today. Currently, the winds are calm but expect winds to pick up as the day goes on. Strong winds are forecast from 2000 – 5000 feet. Winds will shift to a Southeast flow {use data} later in the day.

***LGA will be the airport that needs attention.*** 
The issues at LGA are as follows:
1.	Gusty winds and compression beginning at 12Z.
2.	PROB30 thunderstorms forecasted between 17 and 19Z.
3.	Runway change to RY13 Operation forecasted from 22 – 01Z.
***Student should be given a few minutes to develop/model any initiatives he/she feels might be necessary for LGA.***
After given enough time to work on a plan, the student receives a call from N90. 
Good Morning, looks like LGA is in for a very busy day according to the forecast. Right now things are okay, but we are looking at some gusty wind with compression issues beginning in 90 minutes or so followed by thunderstorms and then possibly a wind shift to a SE configuration. The compression will affect our arrivals for sure. Have you looked into anything yet? We are thinking a 32 rate for the compression. If these thunderstorms materialize, they will affect arrivals/departures on the West gates first and then maybe shut down the field. Do you want to work something up and get back to me?
***Student may have something worked up or will call N90 back after setting up any initiatives they might deem necessary.***
Options: (Path A) Model a GDP at rates they deem correct. They could change the rates to reflect the thunderstorms as well as the SE flow, or set a rate that will address the compression and wait to see if the storms and the SE flow develops.
(Path B) Since this is a weather forecast, they could wait and Ground Stop into a GDP if/when the winds materialize. 
***Both paths lead to a telcon with the facilities and users.*** 
***Some trainers will teach to hold a telcon with FAA facilities only first (I taught this way), and some will teach to hold the full telcon. Here is what each element will say regardless of when they speak to them.*** 
N90 – As we discussed earlier, the forecast calls for gusty winds, compression and thunderstorms. We think we should be at a 32 rate for the compression and lower for the thunderstorms if they impact the arrival/departure gates. We could be shut down if the storms go over the field. 
LGA – I’m concerned about the surface. I was briefed that compression will be a major factor and I don’t want to see the airport back up and go into possible gridlock. TRACON, are you sure a 32 rate is low enough? Something you did not mention, I’m very concerned about going into an SE operation later this evening. 
DAL – I see that compression could be an issue but I feel we should try to land as many as we can before the winds and compression take effect. I think we should not do anything until the compression becomes an issue. I do not feel that a 32 rate is necessary. If we need a GDP, I think the lowest it should be is a 34 rate. 
AAL – I see Delta’s point. Although I hate to see a Ground Stop with it being a wind issue, it may not arrive at the time it is forecasted. I can support either plan.
UAL or any other Airline called – We defer to what the major airlines would like to do. 
Student will need to decide if they issue a GDP and, if so, at what rate (with DALs objection and their request it be noted) or wait and GS into a GDP when the compression becomes a factor.  
***For either path, the winds do arrive as forecasted at 12Z. If the student choses Path A, the instructor should make sure the GDP was issued correctly, DAL’s objection was noted, and all calls to facilities were completed.***

The development has begun in central PA and will be moving quickly NE, just missing the N90 airports, but will still affect N90 airspace. The storms will be most impactful from 18 – 20Z before dying out.

There may be a time when we will need to stop traffic originating from ZBW. We would like to see a GDP revision that steps the rate down with the low hours of 18 – 20Z, then steps it back up. I know you may get some push back but we need to use our “51%” on this one!  
Student will need to model the GDP requested by the Supervisor before holding a telcon. They will need to decide if a GS is needed prior to implementation. (The student may be able to do a revision beginning with a step down at 16Z or 17Z if they can get it out quickly).

16Z  32

***After the Proposal is sent, the student will need to hold a telcon. Here is what the participants will say.*** 
LGA – I agree with these rates. We are about to go +30 D/Ds and it will be climbing. N90 has increased the MIT on West Gate departures.
N90 – We have increased the MIT because the center has increased the MIT to us.  I know we are expecting this weather to really impact the West gates. We were not thinking we need to go to as low as a 28 rate, but we can certainly support the rate. We know ZDC will have trouble getting into the flow if the traffic is rerouted through them, so the lower rate might help them get internals into the flow. We also still have the compression issue and that is not going to go away any time soon. 
DAL – We don’t agree with the rates. We feel they are way too low. We do not need to go to a 28 rate. We would like to see a 32 rate.  
AAL – We would like to stay out of rolling Ground Stops and surface issues. We think a lower rate is necessary but is a 28 rate too low?  
LGA – We are struggling to land 32 right now. With the increase in the MIT on departures, we will have surface issues if we continue to favor arrivals at a rate that is too high for the conditions. 

Before the telcon, the Instructor should discuss how a telcon like this should be conducted. It is a tough process, empathizing with everyone’s concerns but still letting them know the reasons why we need to run the rates we are going to run without looking like we have not considered all options. This is one of the toughest telcons to conduct because the stakeholders usually come away feeling DCC did not collaborate with them. 
Student will then initiate the Ground Stop into GDP revision or just the GDP revision. 
Instructor will make sure the GDP is issued correctly and all calls are made to inform facilities of the changes.

Prompt: Generate a Level 1 terminal training scenario for San Francisco International Airport (SFO). The scenario should begin at 1030 UTC and focus on evolving weather conditions: low ceilings filling in within a few hours and a wind shift to the southeast (190@10G15) between 14-15Z. The simulation must include a disagreement during the Ground Delay Program (GDP) telcon: ZOA and the TRACON should advocate for an early 14Z start at a 28 rate to avoid holding, while United Airlines (UAL) should push for a 15Z start based on their own weather forecast. Provide specific TAF/METAR sequences and scripted dialogue for the instructor and facility representatives.
Scenario:
Level 1 Terminal Training Scenario — SFO (Start: 1030 UTC)

Background / Scenario Setup (1030Z)
You are the National Traffic Management Specialist (Terminal West) assuming position at 1030Z with responsibility for Bay Area initiatives. You have access to TSD, FSM, SWIM/ASDE-X surface indicators (read-only), and AFP/GDP modeling tools.

SFO is currently landing in a normal west-flow configuration, but marine stratus is expected to fill in and a wind shift to southeast (190@10G15) is forecast between 14Z–15Z, with a period of MVFR/IFR driving reduced acceptance rates and potential runway configuration impacts.

Current observation:
KSFO 261030Z 28006KT 10SM FEW008 SCT020 12/10 A3008 RMK AO2

Terminal forecast sequence provided to the student at plug-in:
KSFO 261030Z 2610/2712 28006KT P6SM FEW008 SCT020
FM261200 26008KT 5SM BR BKN008
FM261330 24010KT 3SM BR OVC006
FM261430 19010G15KT 2SM -DZ BR OVC004
FM261700 19010G15KT 4SM BR OVC008
FM262100 22008KT 6SM OVC015
FM270200 28006KT P6SM SCT020

Instructor note (do not read): Keep SFO demand steady through 13Z, then rising. Use ceiling/vis impacts to justify a reduced arrival rate and coordination friction.

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “Based on the current KSFO METAR and the TAF trend, what operational impacts do you expect at SFO between now and 16Z, and when do you anticipate SFO will hit a sustained reduced arrival acceptance rate?”

Expected trainee answer elements:
Stratus filling in by ~12Z with BKN008 then OVC006 (IFR risk).
Degraded arrival rate likely needed by ~13Z–14Z as ceilings drop.
Wind shift to 19010G15 between 14Z–15Z increases runway/config uncertainty and may increase spacing/complexity.
Risk: holding/diversions if GDP start is too late.

Timeline and Event Injects

1035Z — Initial Facility Check-In
ZOA TMU (phone): “ZOA to Command Center—FYI marine layer is coming in earlier than yesterday. We’re seeing tops lowering west of the Bay. Expect SFO to start feeling it by 12Z.”

Trainee expected actions:
Pull SFO FSM and current arrival demand.
Check neighboring constraints (OAK/SJC) for alternates/diversions.
Start a draft GDP model with a potential start window of 14Z (facility-preferred) vs 15Z (airline-preferred).

1115Z — Weather Trend Confirmation
New METAR arrives (provided by instructor):
KSFO 261115Z 27007KT 6SM BR BKN012 12/11 A3006 RMK AO2

TRACON (NorCal) coordinator (landline):  
“NCT to Command Center—ceilings are coming down faster than forecast. We can run normal rates for now, but if we get OVC008 or below, we’ll need compression and larger in-trail. Heads-up: if the wind backs into the south, we’ll need time to stabilize.”

Trainee expected actions:
Begin coordination notes for a GDP telcon.
Identify decision point: issue early GDP or monitor and hold until a worse observation.

1210Z — Compression Begins
METAR update:
KSFO 261210Z 26008KT 5SM BR BKN008 12/11 A3004 RMK AO2

NorCal TRACON: “Now we’re at eight hundred broken. If it goes OVC006 we’ll need a lower rate. I’d rather meter on the ground than hold in the terminal area.”

ZOA TMU: “We’re starting to see minor arrival compression. If this fills in and the wind shift hits, we’ll be stacked.”

Trainee decision point:  
Option A: Start GDP planning now for a 14Z start at a reduced rate.  
Option B: Wait for confirmation of IFR and/or the wind shift, then start closer to 15Z.

GDP Planning Telcon (Primary Training Objective) — 1255Z

Telcon Setup / Controlling the Call
Instructor (as Command Center facilitator prompt):  
“You’re running the telcon. Keep it structured: confirm weather, confirm operational rate, confirm proposed program, solicit objections, then decide.”

Trainee technique targets:
Roll call → weather summary → proposed plan → facility impacts → airline impacts → decision and next steps.
Use short, decisive phrasing: “I’m proposing…” / “Any operational issues with…” / “Objections noted…”

Telcon Participants
Command Center (Trainee)
ZOA TMU
NorCal TRACON (NCT)
SFO Tower (Local)
UAL Dispatch
(Optional) OAK Tower/Tracon coordinator for diversion/overflow awareness

Telcon Dialogue (Scripted Prompt/Response Pairs)

Trainee (opening): “Command Center initiating SFO weather telcon. Roll call—ZOA, NCT, SFO Tower, UAL on?”

ZOA: “ZOA up.”

NCT: “NorCal TRACON up.”

SFO Tower: “SFO Tower up.”

UAL: “United Dispatch up.”

Weather / Capacity Picture
Trainee: “Current KSFO is 261210Z 26008KT 5SM BR BKN008. TAF trends to OVC006 by 1330Z and wind shift 19010G15 between 14Z and 15Z with OVC004. NCT—what’s your expected arrival rate if we go OVC006 and then into the southeast wind?”

NCT: “At OVC006 we’re looking at significant compression; we’ll need more spacing on final. If the southeast wind sets in, we need time to stabilize—plan for 28 arrivals per hour to avoid holding.”

SFO Tower: “Agree. The surface backs up fast in low ceilings. If we wait until it’s ugly, we’ll be playing catch-up.”

Facility Position: Early Start
ZOA: “We recommend a GDP starting 14Z at 28. We can keep the pipeline manageable and avoid terminal-area holds.”

NCT: “Same. Early program protects us. Start at 14Z, rate 28, then reassess once winds settle.”

Airline Pushback: Later Start
UAL: “United’s view is the southeast wind won’t establish until closer to 15Z. Our internal forecast keeps SFO from going hard IFR until after 15Z. We’d prefer a 15Z start to avoid unnecessary EDCTs and mis-metering.”

Trainee (required follow-up): “UAL—are you expecting ceilings to remain at or above BKN008 through 15Z, or are you forecasting improvement in visibility/ceiling despite the marine push?”

UAL: “We think it holds around BKN008 to OVC008 until near 15Z, then drops. The bigger hit is after 15Z.”

Decision Point (Instructor-Controlled)
Instructor prompt to trainee: “You have ZOA and NCT requesting 14Z/28. UAL wants 15Z. What do you do, and what’s your justification using the METAR/TAF trend?”

Expected trainee decision (target):
Implement GDP start 14Z at 28 based on observed deterioration already at BKN008 and the forecast to OVC006/OVC004 plus wind shift risk.
Note airline objection, but prioritize avoiding holding and unstable recovery.

Trainee (model decision language):  
“Based on current observation trending down and the forecast wind shift window, we’re going to plan a GDP for SFO starting 14Z at 28 AAR. UAL objection for a 15Z start is noted. We’ll build a review time at 15Z for potential adjustment if the wind shift delays.”

Implementation Tasks (Student Checklist)
Student must complete:
Model GDP: Start 14Z, rate 28 (initial phase).  
Coordinate with ZOA/NCT/SFO Tower on expected compliance and staffing.  
Publish program and execute required notifications.  
Set a reassessment trigger: first wind shift indication (METAR wind 170–210 with gusts) or ceiling OVC006 or lower.  
Monitor diversions/alternates (OAK/SJC) and advise NCT if overflow begins.

Follow-On Injects (Outcome Validation)

1335Z — IFR Confirmed
METAR:
KSFO 261335Z 24010KT 3SM BR OVC006 12/11 A3001 RMK AO2

NCT (call): “We’re at six hundred overcast now. Good call getting the program out. If we didn’t have it, we’d be holding.”

1445Z — Wind Shift Hits (As Forecast)
METAR:
KSFO 261445Z 19010G15KT 2SM -DZ BR OVC004 12/12 A2998 RMK AO2

SFO Tower: “We’ve got the southerly wind with gusts now. This is exactly where we needed to be metering.”

ZOA: “We’re seeing improved manageability in the stream. Keep the 28 for now.”

UAL (secondary comment): “We see the shift now. Understood on the early start. If we can get a review for improvement later, we’d appreciate it.”

Instructor Wrap-Up / Debrief Prompts
Instructor:  
“What specific observation/forecast detail justified the 14Z start versus 15Z?”  
“How did you document and communicate UAL’s objection while still making the decision?”  
“What triggers would you use to revise the rate up or extend the program?”  
“If the wind shift had not occurred by 15Z, what revision would you have proposed?”

End of Scenario.

Scenario:
ORD Level 3 Terminal Training Scenario — Thunderstorms NW / Gate Impacts by 17Z

Background / Scenario Setup (Start 1030Z)
Position/Role: Student is the Traffic Management Coordinator (TMC) working terminal constraints for ORD with coordination responsibilities with C90 (Chicago TRACON) and ZAU (Chicago Center).  
Shift Start: 1030 UTC  
Tools Available: TSD/FSM, ATCSCC advisories, CDMnet (as applicable), C90/NWSTG coordination lines, playbook reference (regional), EDCT monitoring.

Operational Context at 1030Z:  
ORD is in a high-throughput morning push, operating normally with arrival acceptance rate (AAR) 114. Surface is stable and demand is heavy inbound from ZMP/ZWU/ZDV/ZKC and departures pushing eastbound banks.

Weather Theme: Thunderstorms are forecast to form northwest of ORD and move east, with arrival/departure gate impacts expected by ~17Z. Confidence is not absolute: some guidance suggests storms stay north and become a “gate nuisance,” while other guidance suggests an organized line that blocks multiple arrival fixes and eventually shuts down the airport.

Assessment Question (1035Z)
Instructor Prompt:  
“Based on the current forecast and morning throughput, which facility/airspace elements will hit capacity first as the storms develop, and what is your initial TMI posture?”

Expected Trainee Focus:  
ORD is the primary constraint; impacts will first show as gate restrictions (NW gates first), then arrival metering/holding demand, then airport acceptance collapse (ground stop / zero-rate periods) if the line crosses the terminal area.  
Identify coordination triggers with C90 (arrival config/rate changes) and ZAU (gate closures, holding, MIT, reroutes).

Scenario Objectives (Instructor Notes)
Maintain situational awareness while ORD remains at AAR 114 early.  
Recognize uncertainty and build conditional plans: wait-and-see vs preemptive GDP/GS.  
Conduct a contentious telcon: ZAU wants earlier constraints due gate blockages; C90 wants to stay “wide open.”  
Implement a measured TMI sequence that ultimately transitions from 114 to low rates and then a storm shutdown (airport effectively unavailable for a period).  
Respond to airline push for lower rates (e.g., 56 AAR) to limit diversions and protect hub integrity.

Timeline & Injects

Phase 1 — Normal Ops with Growing Concern (1030Z–1400Z)
Condition: VFR, gusts building. ORD continues AAR 114.

Instructor Inject (1115Z):  
C90 TMU messages: “We’re watching NW development beyond 40 miles. No impacts yet. Keep us posted on any Center gate plans.”

Meteorologist Brief (Instructor voice, 1130Z):  
“Convection is expected to initiate northwest of ORD. Confidence is moderate on timing; highest likelihood of impacts begins 16:30–17:30Z. If cells organize, expect multiple gate penetrations rather than single-fix deviations.”

Trainee Decision Point A (1200Z):  
Options to brief instructor:
Option 1: No TMI yet; continue monitoring, prep telcon, request updated mesoscale guidance.  
Option 2: Begin planning GDP with a stepped rate reduction starting 16Z.  
Option 3: Pre-position a short-duration GS trigger (16:30Z) if gates collapse rapidly.

Instructor Question:  
“What’s your trigger to move from monitoring to action, and how are you coordinating that with C90 and ZAU?”

Phase 2 — Facility Conflict Emerges (1400Z–1600Z)
Condition: Winds gusty; storms visible on radar NW, building eastbound.

Instructor Inject (1445Z):  
ZAU calls requesting early planning:
ZAU Arrival Coordinator: “We’re already seeing early deviation requests out northwest. If this becomes a line, we’ll lose the NW arrival streams and start stacking. We need a plan now.”

Instructor Inject (1450Z):  
C90 counters:
C90 TMU: “ORD is running great. We want to stay wide open as long as possible. Don’t drag the rate down early—we can work gaps.”

Instructor Prompt to Trainee (1455Z):  
“You have opposing positions. What’s your plan to structure the discussion and force a decision timeline?”

Expected Trainee Actions:  
Establish a planning telcon with clear agenda: forecast, gate impacts, demand, proposed rates, triggers, decision time.  
Offer a conditional plan: remain high until X time, then step down; define shutdown criteria (e.g., CB tops crossing final, multiple fixes blocked, lightning within ramp threshold).

Planning Telcon Script (1515Z) — “Hold the Line vs Protect the Hub”
Participants: ATCSCC (Student), C90 TMU, ZAU TMU, ORD Tower (as needed), Airline Dispatch (UAL/AAL/DAL/SWA).

Opening (Student)
Student (Expected):  
“Chicago telcon, this is Command Center ORD desk. Purpose is to align on timing and triggers for convective impacts expected by 17Z. We’ll go weather, then C90/ZAU constraints, then we’ll decide on either a stepped-rate GDP or a wait posture with a GS trigger. Please hold questions until after each round.”

Weather Summary (Instructor as Weather Unit)
DCC Weather:  
“Cells are now initiating NW of ORD and moving east. Confidence is medium that impacts reach the arrival/departure gates by 17Z. Uncertainty is whether it becomes a broken cluster with gaps or a more continuous line. Highest risk window for gate impacts is 17–20Z.”

ZAU Position — Gate Blockage Concern
ZAU TMU:  
“We are concerned about gate blockages. If this consolidates, the NW streams will compress quickly and we’ll be holding. We want demand managed before we’re forced into holding stacks. We recommend we start a program early and be ready to step down sharply if the NW gates close.”

C90 Position — “Stay Wide Open”
C90 TMU:  
“We can work it tactically. ORD is stable, and we’ll take as much as we can for as long as we can. We do not want to advertise low rates too early; it causes unnecessary delay and cancels that we can’t recover from.”

Airline Inputs — Request for Lower Rates to Prevent Diversions
UAL Dispatch:  
“We need predictability. If storms hit at 17Z, a late reaction causes airborne holding and diversions. We’d rather accept a lower planned rate earlier—56 if necessary—than see 30 airborne holding and a diversion wave.”

AAL Dispatch:  
“Agree. Our primary concern is diversions to outstations and crew legality. If you think this becomes a line, we want a conservative plan.”

DAL Dispatch:  
“We can support planned compression, but we want a defined trigger and a path to recover. If you go to 56, we need to know the recovery plan.”

Decision Framing (Student)
Student (Expected):  
“Understood. Here are two executable paths:  
1) GDP with stepped rates starting 16Z, with a contingency for a zero-rate period if the line crosses the airport.  
2) Wait posture through 16:30Z, then Ground Stop into GDP if we lose two or more primary arrival gates or C90 declares arrival acceptance below threshold.”

Instructor Cue (force decision)
Instructor (as ATCSCC Supervisor):  
“Decision needed now. What are you proposing, what rates, and what triggers will cause a revision or a GS?”

TMI Decision Paths (Student Choice)

Path 1 — Preemptive GDP (Preferred for training if student identifies uncertainty + demand)
Expected Student Proposal (example structure):
GDP effective 16Z, stepping down as convective risk increases:
16Z–17Z: 90 (protect some throughput; posture change)  
17Z–18Z: 56 (airline-requested conservative rate)  
18Z–20Z: 0–20 as required if airport stops accepting due TS over field  
20Z–22Z: 44 then 72 as recovery begins (as conditions allow)

Facility Reaction (scripted)
ZAU: “Support. If we wait, we’ll be holding.”  
C90: “We still think 56 at 17Z is too low if gaps exist, but we can support if you build in a rapid recovery revision capability.”  
UAL: “Support 56. Request EDCT stability and avoid rolling GS.”  

Instructor Notes:  
Have the student publish proposal, complete calls, and prepare for revision when the 1505Z TAF amendment hits.

Path 2 — Wait-and-See, then Ground Stop into GDP (Acceptable alternate)
Wait Posture: No program issued before 16Z; student sets explicit triggers.

Trigger Inject (1635Z):  
Radar indicates strengthening cells. ZAU reports:
ZAU TMU: “We’re now losing the NW arrival routing intermittently; aircraft are deviating into adjacent flows. Holding is starting to form.”

Trigger Inject (1655Z):  
C90 calls:
C90 TMU: “We’re getting VCTS and we’re starting to lose departure holes. If this crosses the final, we’ll have to stop arrivals.”

Expected Student Action:  
Issue a 1st-tier Ground Stop for ORD immediately, then transition into a GDP with stepped rates, to avoid uncontrolled airborne holding and diversions.

Airline Reaction (scripted):
UAL: “We needed the 56 earlier. Please lock a conservative rate and reduce airborne exposure.”  
AAL: “Support GS now, but we need a fast conversion to GDP.”

Storm Shutdown Event (Mandatory Outcome in the Sim)
At 171830Z METAR (inject): +TSRA, 1SM, BKN008CB OVC020.  
Instructor Inject:  
ORD Tower reports ramp restrictions and lightning; arrival acceptance collapses.

ORD Tower (as needed):  
“Ramp is stopped due lightning. Arrival acceptance is severely limited. Expect arrival holds and potential diversions.”

C90 TMU:  
“We can’t take them right now—field is effectively shut down. Keep arrivals from launching. We need a hard stop while this core passes.”

Expected Trainee Response:  
Confirm Ground Stop status/tiers.  
Coordinate with ZAU for holding containment (ensure minimal airborne exposure).  
Plan a restart posture and rate recovery sequence once TS intensity drops (post-20Z trend).

TEB/Secondary Impacts (Instructor Optional Inject)
(Use only if workload needs escalation)
ZAU: “MDW and MKE are seeing spillover. Watch diversions and alternates.”  
Student Expectation: Keep focus on ORD constraint; note alternates and diversion risk in telcon updates.

Managing the Conflict (Instructor Technique Focus)
Instructor Prompt (mid-telcon or follow-up):  
“Demonstrate how you control the conference when C90 and ZAU disagree.”

Expected Student Techniques (verbatim-style examples):
“I have both positions; we’re going to decide based on triggers and demand. ZAU, confirm expected gate loss window. C90, confirm lowest sustainable AAR before you declare a stop.”  
“I’m setting the decision point at 1600Z for the initial posture, with automatic revision authority at 1700Z if we lose gates.”  
“We’re not debating whether storms exist—we’re deciding how to minimize holding and diversions.”

End-State / Evaluation Criteria
Successful Performance Indicators:
Student identifies uncertainty and sets clear triggers.  
Student conducts telcon with structure: weather → constraints → demand → proposal → objections logged → decision.  
Student selects and executes a coherent TMI plan (GDP or GS→GDP) that transitions from AAR 114 to reduced rates (including 56 requested) and ultimately handles a shutdown period without uncontrolled airborne holding.  
Student keeps ZAU/C90 aligned through revisions and communicates impacts to airlines with professional, concise phraseology.

End of Simulation (Target ~2300Z)
At 2300Z, METAR improves to P6SM BKN035 OVC060 with diminishing showers. Student briefs recovery posture and outlines next steps for rate normalization and EDCT cleanup.

Scenario:

1700–1859Z: Convection uncertainty window (PROB30): risk of rapid AAR drop / temporary stoppage

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “Based on the weather sequence and the mandatory runway change late day, what time windows do you expect LGA to face capacity issues, and what is your initial plan to protect the operation—including TEB?”

  1) Low ceilings now, worsening by 12Z with gusty winds → compression / reduced acceptance

Decision Point 1 — Morning Compression (1000Z–1200Z)
Instructor Setup
Traffic demand is normal-to-high. Early indicators show ceilings already OVC006 and forecast dropping to OVC004 by 12Z with gusts 28–30 kt.

Instructor: “Do you want to do anything now, or wait for N90 to declare a rate?”

Student Options (presented):
No TMI yet; monitor and pre-coordinate with N90, prepare GDP shell if needed.  
Preemptive GDP proposal beginning 12Z to protect against compression and downstream TS/RY13 impacts.  
EDCT/slot management only via minor rate reduction with no formal GDP (instructor should allow, but stress it is fragile with later constraints).

Expected Good Practice: If not starting a GDP immediately, the student should at least:  
Call N90 TMU for expected AAR changes  
Flag 22Z RY13 AAR 20 as a “known hard constraint”  
Establish triggers for GDP vs. GS: ceiling thresholds, missed approaches, wind/gusts, first TS impacts, gate closures, or departure meter saturation.

Stakeholder Dialogue Block 1 — Coordination with N90 (around 1030Z)
Agent: N90 TMU  
N90: “LGA’s already sliding. We’re seeing OVC006 now and trending down. By 12Z with gusts, we’re anticipating compression. We can probably hang onto high-20s briefly, but if the missed approaches start, we’ll be closer to mid-20s.”

“Confirm your planned AAR by hour 11Z–15Z based on ceiling and gusts.”  
“Any expectation of config changes before the 22Z mandatory RY13?”  
“What’s your missed approach rate and when do you start restricting final?”  
“Any TEB constraints you need protected (arrival windows, IFR training, spacing programs)?”  
“Do you foresee needing a GS trigger if TS hits 17–19Z?”

“Convective window is uncertain—PROB30 means we might get nothing, or we might get a 30–60 minute disruption.”  
“TEB: IFR arrivals get ugly when LGA clamps down; we need you to avoid creating a situation where we’re holding everyone and losing the satellite.”

Decision Point 2 — Convection Uncertainty vs. Planning for RY13 (1100Z–1400Z)
Instructor Inject (1100Z)
A fresh update comes in showing persistent low ceilings. Wind gusts increase on the next METAR.

METAR Update (LGA):  
KLGAB 261100Z 10015G24KT 2SM -RA BR OVC005 16/15 A2990

Instructor: “Given you know you’ll be at AAR 20 during the 22–01Z RY13 operation, do you build a long-duration GDP now, or do you wait and manage tactically until convection is clearer?”

Student Options:
Path 1 (Proactive GDP): Build a GDP that addresses:  
12Z compression reduction  
17–19Z PROB30 TS risk (rate or planning buffer)  
Hard constraint at 22–01Z AAR 20  
Path 2 (Wait/See): No GDP yet; publish an internal plan and triggers; prepare for GS into GDP if TS materializes or if AAR collapses earlier than expected.

Stakeholder Dialogue Block 2 — Facility Telcon (Instructor-Led, 1130Z)
Instructor (as ATCSCC Telcon Lead): “Let’s get LGA on the line: N90, LGA Tower, airline reps, and TEB operations. We’re discussing whether to implement a GDP and how we protect the 22Z runway change.”

Facility Inputs (scripted)
Agent: LGA Tower
“We’re running reduced in the soup. With gusts increasing, we’ll be in compression. The 22Z runway change to 13 is mandatory—construction and equipment. Once we swap, we’re not coming back until after 01Z.”

Agent: N90 TMU
“Agree. We can’t carry normal demand into that 13 window. If we try to stay wide open all day, we’ll pay for it late with holding and diversions. Also, once TS starts popping, we may need to stop arrivals to stabilize.”

Agent: TEB Operations / N90 Satellite Desk
“If you pull LGA down hard without a plan, our holds stack immediately. We need a heads-up and some protection for TEB arrival pushes. When LGA clamps, we lose maneuvering room.”

Agent: Airline Dispatch (generic major carrier)
“We’d rather see a planned program than diversions. If storms are possible 17–19Z, we want conservative planning—don’t flood the airspace. We can accept a lower rate if it reduces airborne holding.”

Instructor Prompt (to trainee): “Take control. Summarize what you heard and propose your plan—start time, end time, and the key hourly rates, including the 20-rate window.”

Expected Trainee Response (template):
“We have confirmed compression beginning by 12Z, convection risk 17–19Z, and a hard 22–01Z AAR 20 due to RY13. I propose [GDP now / wait with triggers]. If GDP: begin no later than [time] to meter demand into the 22Z constraint and reduce late-day holding. We will coordinate TEB protection with N90 to avoid saturating the satellite.”

Instructor Branching Guidance (How the scenario plays)
If Student Chooses Proactive GDP
Instructor: “Model it. Make sure the program explicitly captures the 22–01Z AAR 20 and doesn’t assume an unrealistic recovery during that window.”
Expected modeling elements (not rates dictated except end-state):
Step-down starting 12Z reflecting compression  
Conservative planning for 17–19Z PROB30 (buffer / reduced rate or planning for a potential stop)  
Mandatory 22–01Z at 20 AAR  
Recovery after 01Z only if ceilings support

Coordination requirement: Call N90 and TEB desk with the plan and confirm satellite impacts.

If Student Chooses Wait / GS into GDP
Instructor: “You’re choosing to accept risk. Define triggers and pre-coordinate the play so you can execute quickly.”
Required trigger examples:
LGA ceiling falls to OVC004 with increasing missed approaches  
N90 declares AAR ≤24 sustained  
First TS impacts in LGA/N90 airspace during 17–19Z  
Surface saturation / departure queue exceeds agreed thresholds  
TEB holds reach unacceptable levels due to LGA constraints

Weather Materialization Inject — Midday Compression Verified (1200Z)
METAR Update (LGA):  
KLGAB 261200Z 11018G28KT 1SM -RA BR OVC004 16/15 A2989

Instructor: “Compression is now real. N90 says they’re running AAR 24. If you didn’t start a GDP, you’re behind demand.”

Agent: N90 TMU (call)
“We’re at 24 and not trending up. If this keeps up into the afternoon and you carry too much demand, we’ll be looking at holding.”

Instructor Prompt: “What do you do right now?”

Expected Good Actions:
If no GDP yet: initiate a GDP proposal immediately, or at minimum implement aggressive metering and prepare GS authority if TS hits.  
If GDP already in place: assess whether revision is needed earlier than planned.

Decision Point 3 — PROB30 TS Window (17–19Z) Uncertainty
Instructor Inject (1630Z)
Weather remains IFR but stable; convection is still uncertain. Then N90 reports developing cells west/northwest.

Agent: N90 TMU
“We’re starting to see buildups. Nothing closed yet, but deviations are increasing. If this becomes a line, we’ll lose arrival routes fast.”

Instructor: “Do you reduce the rate proactively, or hold and be ready for a Ground Stop?”

Student Options:
GDP revision: reduce 17–19Z rates to limit airborne risk/diversions.  
Wait and see: keep rates until impacts occur, then GS into GDP revision.  
Hybrid: short-duration GS (tiered) to reset the system, then GDP out.

Airline Pressure (scripted)
Airline Dispatch: “Request lower rates now—something like 20–24 temporarily, or we’ll be diverting. If you wait until it breaks, it’s too late for us.”

Mandatory Runway Change — RY13 (22Z–01Z) Hard Constraint
Instructor Inject (2130Z)
Facilities confirm runway change is still on time and mandatory.

Agent: LGA Tower
“No slip. RY13 at 22Z. Once we change, we’re capped. Plan on AAR 20 through 01Z.”

Agent: N90 TMU
“We need you to meter for that. If you don’t, we’ll hold—and TEB will get crushed with the spillover.”

Instructor Prompt: “What is your final posture for 22–01Z, and what coordination must occur?”

Expected Trainee Answer:
Confirm AAR 20 for the runway change window.  
Ensure GDP (or revision) reflects the 20-rate.  
Coordinate with N90 on:  
Arrival list management and holding avoidance  
Departure release / surface management  
TEB protection plan (see next section)

TEB Impacts — Required Management Actions (Embedded Objectives)
Instructor Requirements
At least once during the scenario, force the student to address TEB.

Trigger Inject (anytime after 12Z, recommended 21Z–23Z):  
N90 reports TEB is stacking due to LGA constraints.

Agent: N90 Satellite Desk
“TEB arrival bank is getting hammered. We’ve got multiple IFR arrivals holding because the LGA constraints are consuming our airspace. We need a plan: either protect a TEB window or coordinate a controlled slowdown.”

Instructor Prompt: “What do you tell N90, and what do you tell the users?”

Expected Student Actions:
Confirm with N90 a TEB protection plan, such as:  
A defined arrival window (e.g., short protected push) when feasible  
Explicit acknowledgment that LGA constraints will constrain maneuvering room; avoid flooding N90 with unmanaged demand  
Communicate to users:  
TEB delays are a consequence of shared airspace + LGA compression; advise planning for holding/EDCT impacts  
Ensure the plan is consistent with the LGA AAR 20 window (don’t promise TEB relief that contradicts the hard LGA constraint).

Telcon Script — Conflict and Control Technique (Student Practice)
Instructor (sets the scene): “You are leading the telcon. N90 wants predictability; airlines want lower rates to avoid diversions; Tower is locked into the runway change.”

Scripted Telcon (student must control)
Student (required technique):  
“This is [Name], TMU. Goal of this call: confirm the operational rate plan through 01Z, including the 22Z runway change, and align on actions to prevent holding/diversions. I’ll go facility-by-facility, then users, then I’ll restate the plan and next update time.”

Agent: LGA Tower
“Runway change is mandatory at 22Z. Our acceptance will be 20.”

Agent: N90 TMU
“We need the program to reflect that early enough to prevent airborne holding. Also, we need to manage TEB impacts.”

Agent: Airline Dispatch
“We want you to keep the rate low earlier so we don’t divert during TS risk. If you keep pushing, we’ll get diversions.”

Student (expected “controlling the conference” closure):
“Understood. The plan is AAR 20 from 22–01Z for RY13. We will [initiate/revise] the GDP to meter demand into that constraint and coordinate TEB protection with N90. Next update at [time] unless N90 declares a further rate drop or convective impacts force a stop. Any objections to the 20-rate window or the proposed trigger-based adjustments prior to 22Z?”

End-State / Instructor Evaluation Criteria
Instructor should evaluate whether the student:
Recognizes and plans around the hard 22–01Z AAR 20 constraint early enough to prevent late-day holding/diversions.  
Makes a defensible choice between:  
GDP modeling with revisions, or  
Wait/see with defined triggers and rapid GS into GDP capability.  
Coordinates effectively with N90 and includes TEB in the operational picture.  
Uses structured, professional telcon control: agenda, facility inputs first, user inputs, summarize, decisions, next update time.  
Adjusts strategy as METAR/TAF conditions verify (compression at 12Z, TS uncertainty 17–19Z).

End of Simulation (after program set for the 22–01Z window)
Instructor: “Brief the next shift: current LGA rate plan, status of TS impacts, and the runway change constraints, including how we’re protecting TEB.”

Prompt: Develop a severe weather scenario for Dallas/Fort Worth (DFW) focusing on convective development in the west at 2030 UTC. The weather should impact the VKTRY and JOVEM arrival gates, requiring the student to evaluate fix balancing and move traffic to the SOCKK arrival (southwest gate). The student will be given multiple options for action, spanning from no assistance, to tactical moves, and lastly, a national playbook implementation. The expected outcome is the implementation of the DFW_VKTRY playbook. Include specific meteorologist briefings regarding 'medium coverage' confidence and scripted facility feedback from ZAB and ZLA during the implementation conference.
Scenario:
Severe Weather Training Scenario: DFW VKTRY/JOVEM Gate Impacts → SOCKK Fix Balancing  
Level: Severe Weather / En Route–Terminal Integration (High-Fidelity)  
Shift Window: 1900Z–2230Z (primary disruption begins 2030Z)  
Student Role: ATCSCC Severe Weather Desk / Strategic Planning Specialist supporting DFW arrival management  
Primary Training Objectives:  
Recognize convective impacts to DFW arrival gates (VKTRY/JOVEM) and translate weather into flow constraints  
Compare/choose among: no assistance vs tactical moves vs national playbook implementation  
Execute fix balancing to protect delivery while preserving DFW throughput as long as feasible  
Coordinate effectively with ZFW, ZAB, ZLA, D10 (as needed), airline SOCs  
Implement and defend the DFW_VKTRY playbook with clear start/stop triggers and demand accounting  

1) Background / Scenario Setup (Student On Position 1900Z)

You are on position at 1900Z. DFW is operating normal mid-day west flow with stable demand. No active GDP/AFP for DFW at shift start. The Command Center weather unit notes increasing convective potential west of the Metroplex later this afternoon.

DFW operational picture (1900Z–2000Z):  
DFW arrival demand is steady; banks will build again late afternoon into evening push.  
Primary DFW arrival gates expected to be in use: VKTRY and JOVEM (west/northwest arrivals), with SOCKK available as a southwest arrival gate for balancing.  
ZFW indicates they can remain “normal ops” until convective development matures and begins blocking key arrival streams.  

Student Tools: TSD, FSM, IDP demand, SWIM weather layers, TCF/CCFP (for reference), reroute monitor, advisory database.

2) Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)

Instructor: “Based on the current setup and the forecast for west-side convection, which DFW arrival gates or flows are most likely to lose efficiency first, and what would be your first indicator that you need to intervene?”

Expected trainee answer (target elements):  
VKTRY and JOVEM are most vulnerable if storms build west/northwest and move east  
Intervention trigger = sustained deviation/closure requests from ZFW/adjacent Centers, loss of gate usability, rapidly increasing MIT/holding, inability to balance demand across fixes  
First action might be tactical balancing toward SOCKK while assessing volume and downstream impacts; prepare playbook if medium coverage becomes established  

3) Weather Timeline & Briefings (Evolving Uncertainty)

1900Z Weather Specialist (Initial Heads-Up)
ATCSCC Meteorologist (1900Z brief):  
“Convection is expected to initiate west of DFW this afternoon with eastward motion. Confidence is moderate on timing; the leading edge could affect west-side arrival corridors after 2030Z. I’m watching for rapid intensification and clustering that could reach medium coverage criteria. I’ll update you at 2000Z and again closer to 2030Z.”

Instructor Note: At this point, do not force a route. Let the student monitor and prepare options.

2000Z Weather Brief (Trend Increasing)
ATCSCC Meteorologist (2000Z brief):  
“Storm initiation has begun west of the Metroplex. Cells are discrete but developing along a boundary. I have medium confidence that coverage will consolidate into medium coverage between 2030Z and 2130Z, mainly affecting the west-to-east arrival corridors into DFW. Eastward movement is steady. If the cluster fills in, VKTRY and JOVEM will see increasing deviation and potential temporary unusability.”

Instructor Technique Prompt: Ask the student to restate what “medium confidence in medium coverage” means operationally and to propose triggers.

Expected trainee response:  
Medium confidence means enough probability to prepare structured mitigation now (park playbook, pre-coordinate), but avoid premature nationwide disruption unless indicators confirm (e.g., facility requests, increasing MIT, gate closures, tops/coverage trending up).  

2030Z Weather Brief (Primary Disruption Begins)
At 2030Z, convection increases in areal extent west of DFW, with more continuous reflectivity and deviations increasing.

ATCSCC Meteorologist (2030Z update):  
“The convective line west of DFW is now organizing. I’m upgrading to high confidence that we reach medium coverage impacting the primary west-side arrival flows. Expect sustained deviation and potential gate impacts for VKTRY and JOVEM beginning now through at least the next 60–90 minutes. The most workable arrival option is likely shifting demand toward the SOCKK gate where gaps are more consistent.”

4) Decision Point: Student Options Menu (Instructor-Driven)

Instructor: “You have three broad options. Talk me through your choice, triggers, and how you’ll communicate it.”

Option 1 — No Assistance / Monitor Only
Continue monitoring; respond only if ZFW requests immediate help  
Risk: gate failure leads to holding/diversions, reactive ground stops, late structure

Option 2 — Tactical Moves (Fix Balancing Without National Playbook)
Coordinate with ZFW/adjacent Centers to move portions of demand from VKTRY/JOVEM to SOCKK  
Use local reroutes, minor MIT adjustments, and quick-turn advisories  
Risk: volume imbalance or inconsistent compliance, requires heavy tactical coordination

Option 3 — Implement National Playbook: DFW_VKTRY (Expected Outcome)
Use structured routing to protect the arrival stream and create predictable distribution  
Explicitly brief start/stop triggers and ensure ZAB/ZLA acceptance  
Benefit: standardization, earlier customer prep, reduced holding/diversions

Expected outcome path: Option 3 (DFW_VKTRY) after confirming medium coverage and facility impacts.

5) Trigger Event: Facility Call (ZFW Raises the Flag)

2040Z — ZFW Arrival Management Request
ZFW TMU (2040Z):  
“Command Center, ZFW. We’re seeing increasing deviations on the west side. VKTRY and JOVEM arrival feeds are getting squeezed and we’re starting to vector aggressively. We need help balancing to SOCKK or we’re going to be in holding shortly.”

Instructor to student: “What do you need from ZFW before you act?”

Expected trainee questions to ask ZFW (prompt checklist):  
“Are VKTRY and JOVEM still usable at all, or do you want them effectively closed?”  
“What’s your target distribution—how many per hour can you take on SOCKK?”  
“Any internal DFW runway/config constraints changing?”  
“What’s the current MIT/holding trend and where are you holding?”  
“Are departures also deviating and impacting crossing/merge points?”  

6) Fix Balancing Task (SOCKK Shift)

Instructor: “Build a plan to move arrival demand away from VKTRY/JOVEM and into SOCKK. Explain how you’ll prevent SOCKK overload and how you’ll handle markets that can’t reasonably use that gate.”

Expected trainee elements:  
Identify impacted demand set by entry fixes/streams feeding VKTRY/JOVEM  
Determine how much can be shifted to SOCKK without saturating downstream sectors  
Consider market group segmentation (ZAB and ZLA flows)  
Determine whether to implement DFW_VKTRY playbook broadly or partially (start time, end time, exceptions)  

7) Implementation Conference (Playbook Adoption) — Scripted Dialogue  
Conference Title: “DFW Arrivals – VKTRY/JOVEM Gate Impacts – Fix Balancing / DFW_VKTRY”  
Time: 2050Z  
Participants: ATCSCC SW Desk (student), ATCSCC Meteo, ZFW TMU, ZAB TMU, ZLA TMU, Airline SOCs (AAL, UAL, SWA), ATCSCC Traffic Management Officer (TMO)

7.1 Controlling the Conference (Student Techniques to Practice)
Student (opening control statement):  
“Good afternoon, this is [Name] at Command Center Severe Weather. Purpose of this call: confirm impacts to DFW arrival gates, agree on a single plan for the next 90 minutes, and establish triggers for escalation or cancellation. I’ll go weather first, then ZFW constraints, then proposed routing/fix balancing, then we’ll take concerns by facility.”

Student (timeboxing):  
“We have five minutes. I’m going to keep it concise—if you have an objection, state it with the operational impact and an alternative.”

Student (decision framing):  
“Given the medium coverage confidence and current deviation trend, the baseline plan is DFW_VKTRY playbook implementation with explicit balancing to SOCKK. If we cannot agree, we risk holding and diversions within the next hour.”

7.2 Weather Brief Within the Telcon
ATCSCC Meteorologist:  
“We’re in high confidence medium coverage west of DFW now. This is not isolated; it’s organizing and moving east. Expect VKTRY/JOVEM arrival corridors to remain impacted through at least 2200Z with periodic gaps but not reliable for continuous high-volume. SOCKK corridor has better spacing and more consistent gaps at the moment.”

Student (weather translation to ops):  
“Copy. Operationally: assume VKTRY/JOVEM are intermittently unusable, and plan for sustained deviations. We’ll structure to SOCKK to reduce tactical vectoring and holding.”

7.3 ZFW Operational Impacts
ZFW TMU:  
“Arrivals are starting to stack. We can’t keep running normal feeds on VKTRY/JOVEM. We need you to shift a chunk of demand southwest to SOCKK. If the push continues without structure, we’ll be forced into holding and possibly a stop-and-go arrival posture.”

7.4 Student Proposal (Expected: DFW_VKTRY Playbook)
Student:  
“Proposal: implement DFW_VKTRY playbook from 2110Z to 2215Z, with the primary objective to reduce VKTRY/JOVEM dependence and rebalance arrivals toward SOCKK. We will monitor and adjust if ZFW reports VKTRY/JOVEM reopening with consistent gaps. ZFW, confirm you can accept the increased SOCKK feed and what your maximum sustainable delivery is on that gate.”

ZFW TMU (supporting):  
“Yes—shift as much as practical to SOCKK. We’ll manage the merge, but we need predictability.”

7.5 ZAB Facility Feedback (Required)
ZAB TMU (concern):  
“ZAB here. We can support the play, but we’re concerned about compression at the handoff points if everyone gets shoved southwest at once. Also, some of our traffic already built to the north side—late changes will increase airborne holding.”

Student (resolution technique):  
“Understood. Two-part mitigation:  
1) We’ll implement with a forward start time to catch departures still on the ground; airborne flights will be managed tactically case-by-case.  
2) We’ll cap the shift volume to avoid a single-sector overload—ZFW will advise if SOCKK becomes saturated.  
ZAB, what specific handoff/fix is your limiting factor so we can monitor it on TSD?”

ZAB TMU (answer):  
“Our limiting factor is the southwest feed into ZFW—once deviations increase, spacing and reroutes collide. If SOCKK becomes the only path, we need you to meter the conversion.”

7.6 ZLA Facility Feedback (Required)
ZLA TMU (concern):  
“ZLA. We can run DFW_VKTRY, but be aware we’ve got multiple westbound departure deviations and we’re using extra miles. If we add more constraint, we’ll have trouble keeping departures moving and could see sector complexity spikes.”

Student (structured acknowledgment + ask):  
“Copy. For ZLA flows, we’ll keep the playbook scope specific to DFW arrival markets only—no unnecessary impact to your westbound departure routes. ZLA, do you need a defined exception set for specific city pairs that cannot take the SOCKK shift efficiently?”

ZLA TMU:  
“Negative on exceptions right now. Just keep it tight and don’t expand scope beyond what’s required.”

7.7 Airline Requests (Pressure to Reduce Diversions)
AAL SOC:  
“We need structure now to avoid diversions. If you keep VKTRY/JOVEM in play without a firm plan, we’ll see aircraft arrive with insufficient fuel margins. We support the playbook.”

UAL SOC:  
“Agree. Our preference is earlier implementation; tactical-only will create uncertainty.”

SWA SOC:  
“Support. We need clarity on expected delay and whether this is a single revision or rolling.”

Student (control statement + commitment):  
“Intent is one clean implementation window with a defined reassessment point at 2145Z. If the weather degrades beyond medium coverage or moves directly over the field, we’ll reconvene for additional actions.”

8) Student Action Items (Execution)

Instructor: “Proceed to implement. Narrate your steps.”

Expected student execution checklist:  
Park/share DFW_VKTRY routing on TSD (or required collaboration tool)  
Issue advisory (route implementation) with start/stop times and scope  
Coordinate with ZFW, ZAB, ZLA for compliance monitoring and exception handling  
Monitor:  
Arrival counts and gate utilization  
MIT changes  
Holding indicators and diversion risk  
SOCKK saturation and downstream sector complexity  
Set reassessment timer at 2145Z  

9) Mid-Course Update / Uncertainty Inject

2145Z Weather/Facility Update (Instructor inject)
Weather remains disruptive; there are intermittent gaps, but the cluster persists.

ATCSCC Meteorologist (2145Z):  
“Medium coverage remains intact. The leading edge is still feeding new development. I cannot recommend returning to VKTRY/JOVEM-heavy operations yet.”

ZFW TMU (2145Z):  
“SOCKK feed is working better than the west gates. Keep the playbook in place through 2215Z. If you pull it early, we’ll be right back into vectors and holding.”

Instructor Prompt: “Do you extend, revise scope, or hold steady?”

Expected trainee response:  
Extend the playbook window if needed, keeping scope limited to necessary markets  
Communicate clearly that extension is driven by continued medium coverage and operational reports  
Avoid over-expanding the reroute footprint  

10) Termination / Recovery

2215Z Recovery Trigger
ATCSCC Meteorologist (2215Z):  
“Coverage is starting to thin west of DFW. Confidence is increasing that the intensity will begin trending down. VKTRY/JOVEM may become intermittently usable with tactical deviations.”

ZFW TMU:  
“We can begin blending some traffic back west if gaps hold, but keep SOCKK as primary until we confirm reliability.”

Student (closeout plan):  
Begin controlled drawdown: maintain SOCKK preference, allow gradual reintroduction of VKTRY/JOVEM as ZFW requests  
Cancel/expire playbook when ZFW confirms stable routing without sustained deviation and holding trend is resolved  
Provide shift log update: start/stop times, primary rationale, key coordination notes with ZAB/ZLA  

11) Instructor Grading Guide (Observable Behaviors)

Technical:  
Correctly identifies VKTRY/JOVEM vulnerability and the need for SOCKK balancing  
Builds a coherent scope for DFW_VKTRY and avoids unnecessary expansion  
Uses explicit triggers and reassessment points tied to medium coverage confidence and facility impacts  

Coordination & Communication:  
Controls the telcon: purpose, order, timebox, decision framing  
Elicits actionable constraints from ZFW, specific bottlenecks from ZAB, and scope sensitivities from ZLA  
Documents objections and mitigations; converts concerns into measurable monitoring items  

Strategic Judgment:  
Does not wait until holding/diversions force a reactive Ground Stop posture  
Does not overreact by imposing overly broad restrictions without evidence  
Provides a credible recovery strategy and avoids “whiplash” revisions  

END OF SCENARIO

Prompt: Generate a severe weather training scenario for ZAU/ORD arrivals involving a conflict between meteorologist forecasts. ZAU weather should forecast area growth along the Great Lakes, while the DCC meteorologist predicts diminishing coverage. The scenario should offer two paths: a tactical approach if deviations are minimal, or the implementation of an ORD regional playbook (e.g., CAN-KEMPA-WEST-2 or ORD_EAST_3) if the weather intensifies. Include scripted 'controlling the conference' techniques for the student to practice. Throughout the script, there should be guidelines of the types of questions the student should ask or information they should provide at each key part of the scenario, including engagements with stakeholders to convey weather information and convey plans of action.
Scenario:
`
ZAU/ORD Severe Weather Training Scenario — Conflicting Meteorologist Forecasts (Level 3 En Route/Terminal Interface)

Background / Scenario Setup (Start 1030Z)
Role: Student is the TMU/DCC weather impact specialist responsible for ZAU impacts to ORD arrival flows and coordination with C90/ORD, ZAU TMU, and users.

Operational Picture at 1030Z:
ORD is in normal west flow, running high arrival demand with no TMIs currently in place.
Multiple morning push banks are airborne/launching in the next 60–120 minutes from: ZDV/ZMP/ZKC/ZID/ZHU, with later West Coast departures needing stable routing decisions before they file.

Current ORD METAR (for context at shift start):  
KORD 171020Z 22012KT P6SM SCT040 BKN080 21/14 A2992

TAF Trend (ORD):  
KORD 171100Z 1712/1818 23012KT P6SM SCT040 BKN080  
TEMPO 1716/1720 4SM TSRA BKN030CB  
FM172000 24015G22KT P6SM VCSH SCT035 BKN060  
PROB30 1721/1801 2SM TSRA BKN020CB  
FM180200 28010KT P6SM SCT040

Instructor Note (what the student “sees” on tools):
NEXRAD shows early scattered convection well west/northwest of ZAU, currently not blocking ORD flows.
CIWS/GTG show building tops 25–30kft in IA/WI with a corridor along the Great Lakes that could expand.
Traffic demand is set up to saturate ORD arrival banks if reroutes/holds begin.

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “Based on what you see at 1030Z, what’s your initial risk call for ORD—minimal impact with tactical deviations, or a credible threat requiring structured routing? What specific triggers would move you from one posture to the other?”

Expected Trainee Answer (Elements):
Identify ORD/ZAU as the primary risk area; early impact likely arrival gate reliability and MIT increases before field impact.
Provide measurable triggers:
Gate closure probability (e.g., west/northwest arrival gates) based on cell growth/echo tops.
ZAU meter list growth / holding onset.
C90 request for MIT, SWAP, or route amendments.
CIWS: sustained ≥40 dBZ line segment or tops ≥35–40kft crossing key gate corridors.
State two paths:
Path A: tactical-only if deviations remain small and gates stay usable.
Path B: implement an ORD regional playbook (e.g., CAN-KEMPA-WEST-2 or ORD_EAST_3) if Great Lakes growth blocks primary feeds.

Key Training Objective
Force the student to:
Reconcile conflicting meteorologist forecasts (ZAU WX vs DCC WX).
Decide when to stay tactical vs when to structure flows using an ORD playbook.
Practice controlling the conference: agenda, time boxing, decision framing, summarizing, and documenting dissent.

Meteorologist Conflict Inject (1100Z)
ZAU Center Weather Brief (via ZAU TMU)
ZAU TMU: “ZAU weather is calling area growth along the Great Lakes. They’re concerned the corridor from eastern WI into lower MI expands south and blocks primary arrival streams. They expect it to worsen between 15–18Z, with multiple clusters merging.”

ZAU Weather (scripted):  
“We’re seeing a growth signal along the Great Lakes—instability and lake-breeze boundary interaction. Confidence is medium-high that coverage expands, not just isolated cells. Expect more widespread deviations and possible gate impacts as early as 15Z, worst 16–18Z.”

DCC Meteorologist Brief (conflicting)
DCC WX (scripted):  
“I disagree with the growth magnitude. Models show weakening after 15Z as forcing shifts east and early convection stabilizes the column. Confidence is medium that coverage diminishes and becomes more scattered—still tactical deviation potential, but not a sustained gate closure event.”

Instructor Prompt: “You have two credible forecasts pointing in opposite directions. What questions do you ask—right now—to turn this into an operational decision?”

Expected Trainee Questions (must be voiced):
“What is the timing window for peak intensity and what’s the earliest credible gate impact time?”
“What’s the expected motion (bearing/speed) and how quickly does the corridor translate?”
“What are the expected tops and are they strong enough to create hard deviations vs soft?”
“Do you expect line segments or discrete cells?”
“Which specific areas/gates are at risk: northwest vs north vs west arrival streams?”
“What’s the confidence level and what would change your forecast in the next 60 minutes?”

Decision Point #1 — Pre-Telcon Posture (1130Z)
Instructor: “Do you initiate an early facilities-only coordination call, or wait for more evidence?”

Acceptable Student Options:
Facilities-only coordination now (ZAU + C90 + ORD) to establish triggers and a pre-coordinated playbook posture.
Wait-and-watch with a defined update time (e.g., 1230Z) and criteria to escalate.

Instructor Expectation: Student selects a posture and states the reason, tied to demand and lead time for airlines.

Controlled Coordination Telcon (Facilities-Only) — Scripted (1200Z)

Conference Control Techniques (Student Must Use)
Student (opening):
“This is the ORD Weather/Flow coordination line. Purpose: align on expected gate impacts and decide whether to remain tactical or prep an ORD playbook. We’ll go ZAU WX → ZAU TMU → C90 → ORD Tower/ATCT → DCC WX. I’ll summarize and assign next steps. Please keep comments to 30 seconds.”

Instructor evaluates:
Uses a structured order.
Time-boxes.
Prevents cross-talk.
Summarizes and states decision points.

Stakeholder Dialogue (Prompt / Expected Answer Pairs)

ZAU Weather
ZAU WX: “We still expect Great Lakes area growth and more contiguous coverage. Concern is blocking north/northwest feeds into ORD by 16Z.”

Student (expected):

Two-Path Scenario Design

PATH A — Tactical Approach (Deviations Minimal)

C90 maintains near-normal acceptance with manageable MIT.
No sustained holding observed.

Prepare a “ready-to-launch” playbook briefing but do not implement.

I’ll send a heads-up note to users that structure may be required if Great Lakes growth consolidates.”

PATH B — Intensification and Playbook Implementation

Student (expected):
“Understood. For planning we need the most disruptive credible case. If we implement now, what’s your earliest ‘improvement window’ you would plan for—18Z or later?”

ZAU TMU
ZAU TMU: “We need to protect the system. Without structure we’re going to build holding and lose predictability.”

C90 TRACON (wants wide open)

ORD ATCT

AAL Dispatch: “Agree—predictability matters. We can support structured reroutes if they reduce airborne holding.”

Student Decision & Conference Close (Required)
Student (expected “control + decision framing”):

We will use [CAN-KEMPA-WEST-2 or ORD_EAST_3] based on the blocked corridors and ZAU’s delivery constraints. This is a 60–90 minute posture with reassessment at 1630Z. DCC WX dissent is noted; we’ll step down early if improvement materializes.”

State start time and expected duration.
State next update time.

“Airlines: confirm acceptance of reroute filing instructions.”

Instructor-Driven “Controlling the Conference” Techniques (Practice Items)
At least once during a telcon, the instructor will force cross-talk. Student must respond with:

Technique 1 — Reset the floor
“Stop. One at a time. I’m going back to ZAU TMU, then C90, then users.”

Technique 2 — Clarify decision point
“We have two choices: stay tactical and accept holding risk, or implement structure and accept longer routes. I need each facility’s recommendation in one sentence.”

Technique 3 — Time-box disagreement
“I’m noting dissent. We’ll revisit at 1630Z with updated observed trends. For now, we’re executing.”

Technique 4 — Summarize and confirm
“To confirm: ZAU expects continued gate constraint through 17Z; C90 accepts structured delivery; users request stable lower posture to prevent diversions.”

Student Guidelines: What to Ask / What to Provide (By Phase)

Phase 1 (1030–1200Z): Situation Awareness
Ask:
“Which gates are most vulnerable and when?”
“What’s the confidence and what would change it?”
Provide:

Phase 2 (1200–1400Z): Trigger Alignment

Phase 3 (1400–1530Z): Decision Under Uncertainty

Two-path plan and how you’ll pivot quickly.

Phase 4 (1530–End): Implementation & Reassessment

“Are reroutes causing secondary volume problems elsewhere?”
Provide:
Reassessment time, dissent tracking, and rollback plan.

End State / Instructor Wrap
Success criteria (either path):
Student maintains professional control of telcons, prevents cross-talk, and documents dissent.
Student uses measurable triggers to justify tactical vs structured posture.
If Path B occurs, student implements and coordinates an ORD regional playbook (CAN-KEMPA-WEST-2 or ORD_EAST_3) and sets a reassessment window aligned to forecast uncertainty.
