Prompt: Create a comprehensive 'Day in the NAS' severe weather scenario for a full 0800Z–1600Z shift. The simulation should revolve around three progressive TCF runs (07z, 09z, and 15z) showing weather development from northern Texas to the Tennessee/West Virginia boundary. The student must develop a changing strategy throughout the day: initially requiring no TMIs, then considering TRANSCON routes, and finally implementing a suite of regional and Transcon plays (e.g., CAN_NOTAP_EAST_1, PXV_PARTIAL) by 15Z to accommodate West Coast departures. Provide a checklist of shift-start tasks and meteorologist briefing scripts for each TCF run.
Scenario:
Severe Weather “Day in the NAS” — Full Shift Scenario (0800Z–1600Z)  
Level: Severe Weather / National TM Strategy (Progressive Planning)  
Shift Window: 0800Z–1600Z  
Student Role: National Severe Weather/Tactical TMU (Command Center)  
Primary Training Focus: Evolving strategy across three TCF runs (07Z / 09Z / 15Z):  
1) Early: Monitor only, no TMIs  
2) Mid: Consider TRANSCON routes as confidence/coverage increases  
3) Late: Implement coordinated regional + TRANSCON plays (expected by 15Z), including CAN_NOTAP_EAST_1 and PXV_PARTIAL, to protect major flows (especially West Coast departures and long-haul transcon)

1) Background / Scenario Setup (0800Z On Position)  
You assume the position at 0800Z. Overnight convection has been disorganized but models indicate an organizing corridor later today. Current operational posture is normal with elevated monitoring.

Current Weather Snapshot (verbal, as briefed):  
A broad convective environment is expected to evolve from northern Texas northeastward toward the Tennessee/West Virginia boundary through the shift.  
At 0800Z, impacts are not yet constraining primary routes, but the environment supports later growth and organization into lines and embedded clusters.

Tools Available: TCF, TSD/FSM, ETMS/TFMS demand views, Collaborative Routing tools, Playbook library, NOPS/Advisory issuance tools.

2) Shift-Start Checklist (Student Must Complete by 0815Z)  
Instructor Prompt (verbatim): “Before you start making any decisions, walk me through your shift-start tasks.”

Expected Student Actions (checklist):  
Pull current traffic demand (next 6–10 hours): major city pairs, peak banks, departure push windows.  
Open and review the 07Z TCF and compare to current radar/satellite and CWSU chat trends.  
Identify at-risk markets/flows if weather grows as depicted:  
DFW/IAH north/east flows  
MEM/BNA/ATL/ZTL corridors  
Ohio Valley / Appalachians (ZID/ZOB/ZDC interface)  
Check current constraints: any existing AFPs, SWAP, miles-in-trail, special use airspace, or runway constraints.  
Establish coordination posture: know which Centers would be primary for early collaboration (ZFW, ZME, ZTL, ZID, ZDC).  
Set decision points tied to the next TCF runs (09Z and 15Z): what would trigger “consider TRANSCON” vs “implement plays.”  
Build a running log: key times, forecasts, facility calls, and planned conferences.

3) Assessment Question (At 0830Z)  
Instructor Question:  
“Based on what you’ve seen so far, what parts of the NAS are most likely to face capacity or routing issues later this shift, and what are your decision triggers to move from monitoring to action?”

Expected Answer Elements:  
At 0830Z: no immediate NAS-wide constraint; primary watch area is the corridor from north TX toward TN/WV boundary.  
Decision triggers: increasing coverage and continuity along the corridor, expected impacts to major jet routes, loss of preferred routing options, rising deviation rates, or Center gate restrictions.  
Plan to reassess at 09Z TCF and again at 15Z for West Coast departure impacts.

4) TCF RUN #1 — 07Z (Used as Initial Planning Baseline)  
Meteorologist Briefing Script (07Z TCF) — Delivered at 0840Z  
DCC Meteorologist:  
“Here’s what the 07Z TCF is indicating. We have initial convective development expected from northern Texas extending northeast in a broken corridor toward the Tennessee/West Virginia boundary later in the period. At this time, confidence is low to medium on continuity; I expect gaps and mixed intensity early. The key point: at 08–10Z, this is largely a monitoring posture—not a strong basis for structured reroutes yet. Your best near-term strategy is to keep the system under observation and watch for organization in the ZFW into ZME interface.”  

Student Decision Point #1 (No TMIs Yet)  
Instructor Prompt: “What’s your operational strategy for the next 60–120 minutes?”  

Expected Student Strategy:  
No TMIs; coordinate awareness calls only.  
Identify which flows would be first affected if growth increases (e.g., north TX departures/arrivals feeding northeast; early transcon staging).  
Prepare for a 09Z update: outline what you would change if coverage increases.

5) Evolving Conditions & First Stakeholder Engagements (0900Z–1030Z)  
Simulated Facility Call (0930Z): ZFW TMU  
ZFW TMU:  
“We’re not constrained yet, but we’re watching buildups north and northeast of the Metroplex. If this starts linking up later, it’ll start pinching our northeast departures and some overflights. For now, we’re fine—just keep us in the loop on any national routing posture.”

Student Technique Expectation (controlling the call):  
Confirm: “Are you seeing any gate restrictions yet?”  
Confirm: “Any deviation rates increasing?”  
Provide: “At this time we’re monitoring; next decision point is 09Z/15Z TCF. I’ll call back if we see organization.”  
Close: “Do you have any questions or concerns with the monitoring posture?”

6) TCF RUN #2 — 09Z (Strategy Shifts to ‘Consider TRANSCON’)  
Meteorologist Briefing Script (09Z TCF) — Delivered at 1010Z  
DCC Meteorologist:  
“The 09Z TCF is trending more organized than the 07Z run. We’re still looking at development from northern Texas northeastward, but the corridor is showing improved continuity. Confidence is medium that we’ll see pockets of higher coverage developing and connecting through the late morning. This is not yet a guaranteed line, but it’s enough that I recommend you start evaluating TRANSCON options—especially routes that would normally cross the central corridor as it pushes toward the Tennessee/West Virginia boundary. You may be able to wait on implementation, but you should be building candidate routes now.”  

Student Decision Point #2 (Build but Don’t Necessarily Implement)  
Instructor Prompt:  
“What TMIs are you considering now, and what information do you still need before you implement?”

Student Options (must choose and defend one):  
Option A — Continue monitoring; build parked routes only (no advisories yet).  
Option B — Limited pre-coordination: start a facilities-only planning conference (no published TMIs) to align expectations.  
Option C — Early TRANSCON advisory language (heads-up only), with implementation held pending 15Z.

Expected Student Outcome at this stage:  
Considering TRANSCON (not necessarily implementing yet).  
Begin evaluating impacts to long-haul flows that will be airborne by 15Z (West Coast departures).

7) Planning Conference #1 (Facilities-Only) — 1045Z  
Instructor Note: Student runs the conference; focus is “controlling the conference” and aligning on decision triggers.

Conference Roster (recommended):  
DCC Severe Weather (Student), DCC Meteorologist, ZFW, ZME, ZTL, ZID, ZDC (as applicable)

Student “Control the Conference” Opening Script (expected technique):  
Student:  
“Good morning, this is [Name] in Severe Weather. Purpose of this call is to align on the 09Z TCF trend and identify decision triggers for routing or play implementation. We’ll go weather first, then each Center gives a 30-second operational impact, then we’ll close with action items and next update time.”

Meteorologist (recap on conference):  
“The 09Z TCF supports medium confidence in a more organized corridor from north TX toward the TN/WV boundary later. Expect increasing coverage and fewer gaps compared to 07Z.”

Facility Inputs (scripted):  
ZFW: “No constraints yet, but if this links up we’ll lose efficiency northeast and need more lateral separation for deviations.”  
ZME: “We can absorb deviations if it stays broken. If it turns into a more continuous band, we’ll need structured help.”  
ZTL: “We’ll start seeing downstream compression if reroutes push volume into our arrival gates.”  
ZID: “If the corridor sets up into our southwest-to-northeast flows, we’ll see sector saturation and MIT pressure.”  
ZDC: “If the system reaches the TN/WV boundary area with continuity, we could see significant overflight constraints and arrival metering needs.”

Student Close (expected):  
Summarize: “No TMIs now; we’re building candidate TRANSCON/regional plays.”  
Define triggers: continuity increases, gate restrictions, deviation saturation, loss of primary routes.  
Set next action: “We’ll reassess at 15Z TCF; I’ll schedule a broader call if we need implementation.”

8) Mid-Shift Inject (1215Z): West Coast Push Awareness  
Instructor Inject: Demand tools show increasing West Coast departure banks that, if launched normally, will be airborne and approaching the central corridor after 15Z.

Instructor Question:  
“What do you need to evaluate right now to protect West Coast departures if weather becomes continuous by 15Z?”

Expected Student Elements:  
Identify which transcon flows cross the corridor and when.  
Identify candidate playbooks to protect routing and sector loading.  
Consider pre-coordination with impacted Centers and airlines if implementation likely.

9) TCF RUN #3 — 15Z (Implementation Phase: Regional + TRANSCON Plays)  
Meteorologist Briefing Script (15Z TCF) — Delivered at 1510Z  
DCC Meteorologist:  
“The 15Z TCF shows the corridor is now expected to be more continuous from northern Texas up through the Tennessee/West Virginia boundary. Confidence is medium to high that the system will produce sustained routing constraints with fewer usable gaps than earlier runs suggested. This will affect primary jet routes and will likely force widespread deviations unless we put structure in place. At this point, I recommend moving from planning to implementation—a combination of regional plays and TRANSCON structure to protect West Coast departures and reduce tactical chaos.”  

Student Decision Point #3 (Expected Outcome: Implement Plays)  
Instructor Prompt:  
“Given this 15Z update, what are you implementing, when, and who must be on the call?”

Expected Student Outcome:  
Implement a suite of regional + transcon plays by/after 15Z, including:  
CAN_NOTAP_EAST_1  
PXV_PARTIAL  
Publish/coordinate structured routes with Centers and affected stakeholders.  
Ensure implementation timing protects aircraft that will be entering the constrained corridor.

10) Implementation Conference — 1530Z (Full Collaboration)  
Recommended Participants:  
DCC Severe Weather (Student), DCC Meteorologist, DCC Supervisor, ZFW, ZME, ZTL, ZID, ZDC, Airline reps (major transcon carriers), ATCSCC Planning (as applicable)

Student “Control the Conference” Opening Script (required practice):  
Student:  
“Good afternoon, this is [Name] in Severe Weather. The purpose of this call is implementation based on the 15Z TCF. We will go: weather summary, then impacts by Center, then I’ll present the routing package—including CAN_NOTAP_EAST_1 and PXV_PARTIAL—with start time, affected flows, and expected benefits. We’ll capture objections at the end; if you object, state what you need changed and why.”

Meteorologist (conference summary):  
“15Z TCF supports a more continuous corridor from north Texas to the TN/WV boundary with sustained constraints. Expect fewer gaps and more organized impacts.”

Facility Feedback (scripted conflict/pressure points):  
ZFW: “We support structure. Without it we’ll have widespread deviations and sector issues.”  
ZME: “We need predictable feeds; tactical deviations will overload us if we get multiple uncoordinated streams.”  
ZTL: “If reroutes slam our gates, we’ll need MIT—structured routing helps us plan.”  
ZID: “We want to avoid last-minute pushes into saturated sectors. Implement early enough to catch the West Coast push.”  
ZDC: “If it builds into the TN/WV boundary as forecast, we’ll have constraints that require predictable routing—support implementation.”

Airline Requests (scripted):  
Major Transcon Airline Rep: “We need early clarity. If you’re implementing, please do it cleanly and consistently so dispatch can plan. We’d rather take controlled route time than risk airborne holding and diversions.”

Student Routing Package Statement (must include both plays explicitly):  
Student:  
“We are implementing CAN_NOTAP_EAST_1 and PXV_PARTIAL as the core structure to protect transcon and reduce tactical deviation volume through the constrained corridor. Start time is [student assigns based on evaluation during sim], and we will monitor for necessary adjustments if the corridor shifts north/south.”

Student Close (required):  
“This is the plan moving forward. Do you have any questions or concerns?”  
Assign follow-ups: monitoring triggers, next update time, who to call if sector saturation develops, and how revisions will be handled.

11) Instructor Evaluation Focus (Throughout)  
Instructor observes and scores:  
Did the student change strategy appropriately across the three TCF runs?  
Did they avoid premature TMIs at 07Z posture?  
Did they begin TRANSCON evaluation at 09Z and build parked options?  
Did they implement by 15Z to protect West Coast departures?  
Did they control conferences with structure, time discipline, and clear decision framing?  
Did they communicate forecast confidence and impacts clearly and consistently?

12) End-of-Shift Wrap (1600Z)  
Instructor Prompt:  
“Summarize your strategy changes from 07Z to 15Z and justify the timing of implementation. What would cause you to revise or cancel CAN_NOTAP_EAST_1 and PXV_PARTIAL?”

Expected Student Wrap:  
07Z: monitor only; low/medium confidence with gaps.  
09Z: trend more organized; build/coordinate potential TRANSCON.  
15Z: continuity increases; implement structured suite including CAN_NOTAP_EAST_1 and PXV_PARTIAL; maintain revision triggers based on observed evolution and facility saturation.

Prompt: Generate a terminal training scenario for SFO with low cielings and wind shifts that requires the student to model a TMI (e.g., GDP)
Scenario:
SFO Terminal Training Scenario — Low Ceilings, Wind Shift, and TMI Decision-Making

Background / Scenario Setup (Start Time: 1000 UTC)
You are the DCC/NTML Terminal West desk covering SFO/OAK/SJC with access to FSM/TSD/CDMNet and local coordination lines (NCT, ZOA TMU, SFO ATCT). Assume normal staffing and no other major constraints in the NAS at shift start.

At 1000Z, SFO is running west flow with stable demand and no active initiatives. Weather is deteriorating offshore with a marine push expected to deepen through the late morning, then a wind shift is forecast to force a configuration change later in the day. The operational risk is a capacity cliff: a gradual compression to reduced rates followed by a runway/configuration change that further constrains arrivals and departures.

Current METAR (1000Z):  
SFO reports VFR but trending down: “ceiling lowering on the approach end; visibility good; light winds.”

TAF trend (briefed to the position):  
Ceiling expected to lower into IFR late morning with periods of LIFR possible in the midday window.  
Winds expected to shift later (increasing crosswind/tailwind considerations for west flow), with a likely move to an alternate configuration and increased spacing.  
Improvement is expected later, but timing confidence is moderate.

You have a bank of transcon arrivals scheduled to peak between 1130Z–1500Z and a heavy departure push that will be sensitive to surface saturation if arrivals are not metered.

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “Based on the forecast and SFO’s typical configuration sensitivity, what do you expect will be the first operational failure mode today—arrival rate reduction, departure gridlock, airborne holding, or a full stop? Which tool set do you want ready before the ceiling drops?”

Expected Trainee Answer (minimum):
SFO will first see arrival compression as ceilings drop (IFR/LIFR), leading to arrival rate reduction and surface saturation if not managed.
The wind shift later introduces a second capacity reduction due to runway/configuration change and spacing.
Trainee should prepare to model a GDP early (and/or be ready for a GS into GDP if conditions collapse faster than forecast).

Operational Timeline & Injects

1005Z — Initial Facility Coordination Call (NCT)
Agent: NCT TMU (Approach/Flow)  
“NCT here. We’re watching the marine layer—tops coming up and the ceiling forecast to settle in. If it goes IFR on the ILS, we’ll need increased spacing. We’d like you to be ready to meter SFO before we get stuck holding.”

Trainee Task / Decision Point
Ask for: expected acceptance rate under IFR, trigger points (ceiling/vis), and runway/configuration sensitivity to winds.
Begin GDP modeling options:
Proactive GDP that steps down rates as ceilings drop, then accounts for the wind shift window.
Wait-and-see posture with a planned GS trigger and rapid conversion to GDP if ceilings tank.

1030Z — Weather Update (DCC Meteorologist)
Agent: DCC Meteorologist  
“Marine layer deepening faster than earlier guidance. Expect ceilings to trend from MVFR to IFR earlier than planned. Confidence moderate. There’s also a wind shift later that could force a runway/config change and add spacing. I’m not ready to call it a hard stop, but you should plan for a sustained reduced rate.”

Trainee Guidance (Instructor Notes)
Look for the trainee to ask:
“When do you expect first IFR at the field?”  
“How long is the lowest ceiling window?”  
“What’s the uncertainty band—earliest/latest onset?”  
“What’s the wind shift timing window and expected sustained/gusts?”  
“Do you expect approach minima issues (CAT I/II/III sensitivity)?”  

1100Z — SFO ATCT Calls: Surface Risk Emerging
Agent: SFO ATCT (Supervisor)  
“Tower. We’re starting to see arrival spacing increase. If we keep pushing arrivals without a meter, we’ll choke the departure bank and go surface saturated. We can’t take a surge and then slam the brakes.”

Trainee Options
Option A: Proactive GDP  
Model a GDP beginning soon with stepped rates that anticipate the IFR onset and later wind shift.
Option B: Tactical Metering Only (no GDP yet)  
Coordinate MIT/expectation with ZOA/NCT and attempt to ride it out.
Option C: Wait-and-See with GS Trigger  
Define clear triggers: e.g., ceiling below a stated value, acceptance rate call from NCT, or arrival holding observed; then issue GS and transition to GDP.

Instructor Prompt
“What’s your plan to prevent surface saturation while still maximizing arrivals before the worst conditions?”

Expected response includes:
Meter early enough to prevent uncontrolled bursts.
Tie decisions to measurable triggers: reported ceiling/vis, NCT acceptance rate, airborne holding trend, runway change certainty.

1130Z — NCT Announces Reduced Acceptance
Agent: NCT TMU  
“We’re now planning reduced acceptance due to approach spacing. If the ceiling drops another notch, we’ll have to cut further. Also, winds are trending toward that shift later—timing still uncertain.”

Trainee Task
Decide whether to publish GDP now (preferred) or hold.
If GDP: ensure coordination with ZOA TMU and SFO ATCT on:
Start time and duration
Rates by hour
Exemption policy / compression handling
Expectation for revisions as conditions clarify

Stakeholder Dialogue — GDP vs Wait-and-See Telcon

Telcon Trigger (Instructor): Initiate a coordination telcon by 1145Z  
Participants: NCT TMU, ZOA TMU, SFO ATCT, Airline Dispatch reps (UAL/ASA/SWA as available), DCC Weather

Opening / Conference Control Technique (Student Standard)
Trainee (Conference Lead):  
“This is DCC coordinating SFO constraints. Goal: align on expected acceptance rate through the ceiling drop and potential wind/config change, then decide GDP versus tactical-only. We’ll go NCT for rate, Tower for surface, Weather for timing, then airlines.”

(If the trainee doesn’t structure the call, instructor should prompt them to restate: who speaks, what data is needed, and what decision will be made by end of call.)

Agent: NCT TMU
“We can stay higher for the next hour if ceilings hold, but if it goes IFR we need more spacing. If the wind shift forces the config change later, expect another reduction. I don’t want surprise volume when we’re already compressed.”

Expected Trainee Follow-up Questions
“What’s your current planned AAR now, and what is it under IFR?”  
“What is the trigger for the next reduction?”  
“If wind shift forces config change, what’s the expected sustained AAR?”

Agent: SFO ATCT
“We need predictable volume. If we get hit with a surge during the ceiling drop, departures stop moving and recovery takes hours. I support a program that prevents a spike.”

Expected Trainee Response
Acknowledge surface constraints and commit to predictable flow.
Clarify departure priorities and whether any departure metering is needed.

Agent: ZOA TMU
“Center can support metering, but we need a published plan. If you wait too long, we’ll be stuck holding. We’d rather execute a controlled program than tactical reactions.”

Agent: DCC Meteorologist
“Ceiling timing is the big uncertainty. Earliest onset is within the next hour; latest is a couple hours out. The wind shift later still looks likely, and if it arrives on the early edge, it will coincide with the lower ceilings.”

Expected Trainee Summary
“We have uncertainty in onset, but high confidence in impact window; we need an initiative that’s revision-friendly.”

Agent: Airline Dispatch (UAL)
“We’d rather take a GDP than diversions and extended holding. Please don’t wait until it’s already collapsing.”

Agent: Airline Dispatch (SWA)
“If you run a GDP, keep it realistic—don’t over-restrict early if we can still land.”

Decision Point: Choose a Strategy

Path 1 — Proactive GDP (Preferred)
Instructor Expectation
The trainee:
Models a GDP with stepped rates: higher initially, lower during IFR/LIFR risk window, and another reduced period during the wind/config change window.
Builds in a revision plan as ceilings and wind shift timing firm up.

Trainee Implementation Checklist
Publish GDP in the system.
Notify: NCT, ZOA TMU, SFO ATCT, impacted adjacent facilities as required.
Post concise CDMNet notes: reason, triggers, expected next update time.
Set a revision reassessment time (e.g., every 30–60 minutes or at a specific METAR threshold).

Path 2 — Wait-and-See, Then GS Into GDP
If the trainee chooses to delay:
Instructor will allow 30–60 minutes of “monitoring” then force a deterioration event.

Inject (Collapse)
Agent: NCT TMU (later call)  
“Ceiling just dropped; we’re in immediate compression. We need an arrival stop now while we sort spacing and configuration.”

Expected Trainee Action
Issue a Ground Stop for SFO arrivals (appropriate scope), then model/publish a GDP to transition out of the stop once stable rates are known.

Wind Shift / Configuration Change Event (Late-Day Constraint)
Inject: Mandatory Runway/Config Change
Agent: NCT TMU
“Wind shift is now operationally significant. We are switching configuration and we need increased spacing. Expect the lowest sustainable arrival rate during this window.”

Expected Trainee Response
Issue a GDP revision (or GS if already saturated) to reflect the lowest rate during the configuration change.
Coordinate closely with Tower regarding surface recovery plan (departures vs arrivals balance).

Instructor Focus
Did the trainee revise early enough to prevent airborne holding/delays from compounding?
Did they communicate clearly to stakeholders: what changed, why, and when the next update will occur?

TEB/OAK/SJC Considerations (Local Spillover)
Agent: NCT TMU
“If SFO compresses, we’ll see spillover to OAK/SJC. We need you to watch for local saturation impacts and ensure we don’t create a Bay Area pileup.”

Trainee Task
Monitor demand shift/diversions to OAK/SJC.
If needed, coordinate minor metering or advisories to prevent secondary saturation.

Evaluation Criteria (Instructor Notes)
The trainee is successful if they:
Identify SFO as the primary constraint and anticipate arrival compression leading to surface saturation.
Select and execute a coherent strategy:
GDP early with step-downs and revision points, or
GS into GDP with clear triggers and rapid stabilization.
Control telcons professionally:
Set agenda, solicit specific rate/timing inputs, summarize, then decide.
Coordinate effectively with NCT, ZOA, SFO ATCT, and airlines.
Manage uncertainty by defining triggers and committing to reassessment times.

End of Simulation
Simulation ends after the trainee has:
Implemented at least one TMI (GDP and/or GS),
Executed at least one revision based on updated ceiling/wind shift information,
Completed required coordination calls and documented the plan in operational notes.

Prompt: Generate a terminal training scenario for ORD with convective weather that requires the student to manage near full capacity to a shutdown of the airport.
Scenario:
ORD Terminal Training Scenario — Convective Weather, Near-Capacity to Shutdown

Background / Scenario Setup (Start: 1800 UTC)
Position/Role: Student is the TMU/Traffic Management Coordinator for the Chicago metro (ORD primary; MDW/nearby satellites monitored).  
Facility Environment: High demand afternoon push; multiple arrival streams converging over the Chicago TRACON. ZAU is managing deviations building from the west/southwest with increasing impact to ORD arrival gates.  
Tools Available: TSD/FSM, IDAC/ETMS demand, ATCSCC advisories, N90-style airport configuration feed (simulated), convective overlay (simulated).

Current field condition at ORD (initial METAR):  
KORD 151751Z 21018G28KT 8SM SCT030 BKN060 26/18 A2985  
Winds gusting, VFR at the field, but a convective line is developing to the WSW with increasing lightning within 25–40 NM.

TAF sequence provided to student at start (used for planning):  
KORD 151720Z 1518/1624 21018G28KT P6SM SCT030 BKN060  
  TEMPO 1519/1522 3SM TSRA BKN025CB  
  FM152200 24022G32KT 2SM +TSRA BKN015CB  
  TEMPO 1522/1601 1/2SM +TSRA FG VV002  
  FM160100 28015G25KT 4SM SHRA BKN020  
  FM160400 31012KT P6SM BKN035  
  FM160900 32010KT P6SM SCT040

Operational expectation (instructor note): ORD remains near full AAR early, then rapidly degrades due to convective impacts (arrival gate closures, ramp stops, and eventually an airport shutdown/ground stop).

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “Based on the TAF/METAR sequence and current demand, what do you expect to be the first capacity limiter at ORD: arrival gates, ramp/taxi, runway configuration, or field shutdown—and when?”

Expected trainee answer (minimum):  
Arrival gate constraints and airborne deviations begin first (by 1900–2000Z), followed by ramp constraints and a likely ORD Ground Stop during +TSRA/VV002 window (~2200Z–0100Z), with recovery after 0100Z as intensity drops.

Scenario Flow (Time-Driven Injects)

Phase 1 — Near Full Capacity with Early Deviations (1800Z–1930Z)
Instructor setup: Demand shows ORD scheduled arrivals near peak; minimal slack. Student must decide whether to preemptively coordinate MIT/tactical gate balancing vs “monitor only.”

Inject 1 (1810Z) — ZAU Arrival Management Call
ZAU Arrival Lead: “TMU, we’re starting to see deviations west of DPA. Nothing closed yet, but we’re compressing. What’s your plan—tactical only or do you want to start planning a stop/reduction?”
Expected trainee action: Ask for specifics (which fixes/gates impacted, expected closure time, current acceptance rate) and start a contingency plan (rate steps + possible stop trigger).

Inject 2 (1830Z) — Updated METAR shows trend
KORD 151830Z 22020G30KT 7SM VCTS BKN035CB BKN060 25/18 A2982
NWS/ASOS note relayed by tower (simulated): Lightning detected within 10–15 NM to the WSW.

Decision Point A (Instructor):  
“You can:  
1) Do nothing (monitor).  
2) Coordinate a controlled reduction with ZAU/ORD now to avoid holding.  
3) Prepare a Ground Stop proposal trigger-based (not issued yet).  
Which do you choose and why?”

Phase 2 — Compression, Gate Constraints, and Rate Reduction (1930Z–2200Z)
Inject 3 (1935Z) — ORD Tower / ATCT Coordination
ORD Tower (CIC): “We’re still landing but we’ve got increasing deviations on final, and we may need to suspend ramp movements if lightning gets closer. We can’t support extended strings if they’re unstable.”
Expected trainee response: Confirm lightning policy thresholds, ask about ramp status triggers, and coordinate a planned AAR reduction with ZAU.

Inject 4 (2000Z) — ZAU declares constraints
ZAU Supervisor: “We’re losing efficiency. Recommend ORD AAR down from normal push to a constrained rate by 2015Z. We’re seeing holding starting in the stacks if we don’t.”
Instructor provides target planning numbers for realism:  
2000–2100Z: sustainable AAR 54 if no ramp stop  
2100–2200Z: plan AAR 42 due to TS proximity and deviation routes

Inject 5 (2030Z) — METAR and lightning escalation
KORD 152030Z 23022G32KT 4SM TSRA BKN020CB OVC040 24/19 A2978
ORD Tower: “Lightning is now within the ramp ring. We’re initiating a ramp stop in the next 10 minutes if it holds.”

Decision Point B (Instructor):  
“Do you:  
Coordinate a rate reduction only (AAR steps),  
Issue an immediate Ground Stop into a controlled program (GDP), or  
Wait for an actual ramp stop and then react?”  
Student must justify using expected demand/holding risk and stakeholder coordination time.

Phase 3 — Shutdown Window / Ground Stop (2200Z–0100Z)
Inject 6 (2200Z) — Convective core hits the field
KORD 152200Z 24024G35KT 1SM +TSRA VV004 23/20 A2972
ORD Tower: “We’re shutting down arrivals. Field is effectively unavailable; we cannot accept. Departures stopped; ramp is stopped.”
ZAU: “ORD is N/A. We need a Ground Stop now—include at least first tier and be ready to expand.”

Expected trainee action:  
Implement ORD Ground Stop (define scope, tier, exceptions if any).  
Coordinate with ZAU for airborne holding management and diversion expectations.  
Prepare “out plan” (GDP or controlled release) once weather trends allow.

Inject 7 (2230Z) — Airline and ATCSCC coordination pressure
Airline Dispatch (UAL): “We have multiple ORD inbounds with limited fuel margin. Are you protecting priority flights or issuing a blanket stop? What’s your expected restart time?”
Expected trainee response: Provide clear, time-bounded plan; coordinate diversions with ZAU; emphasize safety and field status; avoid speculative restart times beyond forecast window.

Inject 8 (2330Z) — Worst conditions (TAF TEMPO realized)
KORD 152330Z 25020G30KT 1/2SM +TSRA FG VV002 22/21 A2969
ORD Tower: “No acceptance. No departures. Expect this through at least the top of the hour.”

Phase 4 — Recovery, Re-Start, and Stabilization (0100Z–0400Z)
Inject 9 (0105Z) — Improvement begins
KORD 160105Z 28016G26KT 4SM SHRA BKN020 22/20 A2974
ORD Tower: “We can start limited arrivals once ramp restrictions ease. We’ll need a cautious restart; expect constraints and missed approaches risk until cells clear east.”

Expected trainee action:  
Transition from Ground Stop to controlled release (GDP or metered releases).  
Coordinate restart rate with ZAU (initial low, then step up as conditions stabilize).  
Communicate to stakeholders a structured recovery timeline (rate steps + triggers).

Stakeholder Dialogue (Prompt / Expected Answer Pairs)

1) ZAU Supervisor — Rate Coordination
ZAU Supervisor: “If you don’t reduce now, we’ll be forced into holding that turns into a stop anyway. What rate do you want to run and when?”
Expected trainee: “Plan stepped reduction: implement constrained AAR by 2015Z, then further reduction by 2100Z based on TS proximity. If ramp stop occurs or field drops below stable approach minima, we’ll execute an ORD Ground Stop immediately.”

2) ORD Tower CIC — Ramp/Field Status
ORD Tower: “Lightning policy will stop ramp. If ramp stops, we can’t absorb arrivals on the surface. Are you prepared to stop them at the source?”
Expected trainee: “Affirm. ‘If ramp stop is declared, we will issue ORD GS with first tier immediately and expand as needed; we’ll also coordinate ZAU to manage airborne holding/diversions.’”

3) Airline Dispatch — Pushback on Stop / Need for Predictability
UAL Dispatch: “We want you to wait; our crews can take deviations. A stop will strand aircraft and crews.”
Expected trainee: “We’ll consider deviations while the field is accepting. Once ORD reports N/A or ramp is stopped, acceptance is not available. We will stop at the source to prevent unsafe surface saturation and excessive airborne holding.”

4) ATCSCC Supervisor (Instructor-Role) — Conference Control
ATCSCC (Supervisor): “You’re running the call. Give me your situation, your plan, and what you need from each stakeholder—briefly.”
Expected trainee (model response):  
  1) “Situation: Convective line approaching ORD; VCTS to TSRA; arrival efficiency degrading; ramp stop likely.”  
  2) “Plan: Step AAR down now; trigger GS on ramp stop/field N/A; transition to controlled release on improvement.”  
  3) “Need: ZAU confirm gate status/holding; Tower provide ramp stop and restart estimates; airlines identify critical fuel flights.”

Instructor Controls / Expected Branching

Path 1: “Wait and See” (Higher Complexity Outcome)
If the trainee waits too long:
ZAU reports increasing holding and airborne diversions.
Instructor escalates with “unplanned” immediate ORD N/A → student must issue a rushed GS and manage stakeholder dissatisfaction.

Path 2: Proactive Rate Steps + Triggered GS (Preferred)
If the trainee reduces early:
Holding is minimized before shutdown.
GS is still required at 2200Z, but with fewer airborne aircraft trapped in the terminal environment.
Recovery is smoother with a pre-briefed restart plan.

Teterboro/MDW/Regional Note (Instructor Use)
If trainee asks about alternates/diversions:
Diverts flow to MKE/MSP/DTW/IND; MDW constrained but may take limited diversions.  
Instructor can inject: “MDW requests heads-up—do not assume spare capacity during ORD shutdown.”

End State / Debrief Items (Instructor)
Debrief prompts:
1) “What were your objective triggers for stepping down rates and issuing the Ground Stop?”  
2) “How did you prevent airborne holding from becoming your primary risk?”  
3) “What did you communicate as ‘known’ vs ‘estimated’ to stakeholders?”  
4) “What would you change about your restart plan after 0100Z improvement?”

End of simulation condition: ORD transitions from N/A to limited acceptance, then stabilizes under SHRA with improving ceiling/vis as forecast beyond 0400Z.

Prompt: Generate a terminal training scenario for LGA with low cielings, gusty winds, and compression, with uncertainty in convective weather forecasts. The student will be required to choose between multiple courses of action (e.g., modeling a program or adopting a wait-and-see approach and using a ground stop).
Scenario:
LGA Terminal Training Scenario (Level 2): Low Ceilings, Gusty Winds, Compression, and Convective Uncertainty

Background / Scenario Setup (Start 1030Z)
Position: ATCSCC TMU/Terminal East Specialist (LGA focus)  
Shift Start: 1030Z  
Tools Available: TSD, FSM, CCFP/TCF panels, IDFS, TFMS counts, SWIM/ASDE-X proxy view (surface saturation), Advisories/NTML, EDCT/GDP modeling capability.

Overnight residual moisture and a shallow marine layer are producing low ceilings into the NYC metro with gusty winds expected to increase late morning into early afternoon. Multiple convection-allowing models disagree on whether scattered showers remain non-convective or whether embedded TS develop along a weak boundary west/southwest of the field, potentially impacting LGA final, departure corridor, and fixes mid/late shift.

Initial Weather at 1030Z
METAR (most current):  
  KLGA 181029Z 07012G20KT 2 1/2SM -RA BR OVC008 17/16 A2992 RMK AO2 SLP132
TAF (issued prior to shift):  
  KLGA 180900Z 1809/1912 07010G18KT 3SM -RA BR OVC010  
  TEMPO 1809/1813 1SM RA BR OVC006  
  FM181300 06014G22KT 4SM -RA BR OVC012  
  FM181700 07016G24KT 5SM -SHRA OVC015  
  PROB30 1818/1822 3SM TSRA BKN015CB  
  FM182300 08012G20KT P6SM BKN020  
  FM190400 06010KT P6SM SCT025

Operational posture at 1030Z: LGA is in an IFR arrival posture with compression in the arrival stream and departure metering due to wind/gusts and low ceilings. EWR/JFK are also IFR, creating regional compression and limiting swap options.

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “Based on the current METAR/TAF and what you’re seeing on the TSD/FSM, what do you expect will become the primary capacity constraint today—LGA arrival rate, departure rate, or both—and when?”

Expected Trainee Answer (Key Points):
Low ceilings (OVC006–012) and gusty winds drive arrival rate reduction and compression immediately, with worst conditions through ~13Z and lingering IFR through at least 17Z.
Gusty ENE flow complicates runway configuration and spacing; likely both AAR and ADR constrained.
Convective uncertainty (PROB30 TSRA 18–22Z) introduces risk of rapid degradation requiring tactical initiatives (GS) or GDP revisions later.

Traffic Snapshot (1030Z–1300Z)
Instructor inject (read to trainee):
Demand into LGA is building: peak arrival demand 38–42/hr between 12–15Z (bank push), with heavy Northeast shuttle flows and high short-haul density.
Surface is saturated: gate utilization high; taxi-out trending upward.
NYC airspace already metering due to simultaneous IFR at EWR/JFK.

Instructor: “You have three levers: wait-and-see, model a GDP, or plan for tactical GS if convection materializes. Talk me through your initial plan.”

Decision Point 1 (1045Z): Choose an Initial Course of Action
Option A — Wait-and-See (No immediate program)
Monitor ceilings trend through 12–13Z.
Pre-coordinate with N90/LGA ATCT for triggers: if AAR falls below demand for >60 minutes, move to GDP.
Prepare a rapid GS template (scope + duration) if TS pops earlier than forecast.

Option B — Model an LGA GDP (Proactive)
Build a GDP proposal starting 12Z with a conservative rate through 17Z.
Plan revision triggers based on ceiling improvement and whether TS remains only “probability.”

Option C — Hybrid: Short GDP + Contingency GS
Implement a short-duration GDP covering the worst IFR (12–16Z) and explicitly brief that a GS may be required 18–22Z if TS develops.

Instructor technique: If trainee struggles, ask:  
“What’s your risk tolerance for airborne holding in NYC?”  
“What are you seeing in trend: OVC008 lifting or steady?”  
“How much uncontrolled demand hits 12Z if you do nothing?”

Weather & Forecast Uncertainty Injects (Sequential)
1100Z Update (Ceilings not improving as expected)
METAR: KLGA 181059Z 08014G22KT 2SM -RA BR OVC007 17/16 A2991
DCC Meteorologist (briefing to trainee):  
  “Ceilings are holding at 700–900 feet, and the low-level moisture is deeper than earlier model guidance. I’m high confidence IFR persists through at least 15Z. For convection later, I’m low-to-medium confidence—some guidance keeps it as showers; other runs hint at embedded TS after 18Z, mainly south/west of LGA.”

Instructor Prompt: “Given this, do you change your plan? If yes, what initiative and what start time?”

Stakeholder Dialogue Block 1: Facility Coordination (1130Z Planning Call)
Instructor Role-Play: Initiate a focused coordination call. Trainee is expected to ‘control the call’: identify purpose, state constraints, state proposal, ask for impacts, then decide.

N90 (TRACON)
N90 TMU: “We’re compressing now. With OVC007 and gusts, we can’t stay at normal throughput. We’re trending toward AAR in the low 30s. If ceilings don’t lift by 13–14Z, we’ll need you to take demand out—GDP preferred over airborne holding.”

Expected Trainee Response Elements:
Confirm current acceptance rate and constraint driver (ceiling vs wind vs staffing).
Ask for rate bands (best-case / most-likely / worst-case) through 17Z.
Ask for trigger to declare “need GDP now” (e.g., sustained holding > X minutes, finals saturation, MIT increases).

LGA Tower
LGA Tower: “We’re saturated on the surface. Gusts are creating spacing issues and go-arounds are a concern with the low deck. We can keep moving departures, but arrival compression is the bigger problem.”

Expected Trainee Response Elements:
Clarify runway config and whether gusts risk configuration change.
Ask about gate saturation and EDCT compliance impacts.

Airline Dispatch (DAL)
DAL Dispatch: “If you’re going to do something, do it early. We can hold pushes here, but we don’t want airborne holding into LGA.”

Airline Dispatch (AAL)
AAL Dispatch: “We’d prefer GDP over surprise GS. But if TS becomes a factor later, we need a clear trigger for a ground stop so we can protect diversions.”

Decision Point 2 (1200Z): Student Must Commit to a Plan
Instructor: “You’re now at 1200Z. Demand increases in the next two hours. N90 is signaling low-30s AAR. What are you implementing—GDP now, wait, or tactical only?”

If Trainee chooses GDP
Instructor expected actions checklist:
Model LGA GDP with conservative AAR through 15–17Z.
Publish proposal, coordinate with N90/LGA Tower, notify adjacent centers as required.
State explicit revision triggers tied to:
Ceiling lifting above OVC015,
Gusts decreasing,
Convective confidence increasing/decreasing.

If Trainee chooses wait-and-see
Instructor inject: By 1230Z, holding begins to build at the perimeter, and N90 calls again requesting demand reduction.

Convective Uncertainty Escalation (1500Z–1730Z)
1500Z TAF Amendment (adds more explicit convective risk)
KLGA 181500Z 1815/1912 07016G24KT 4SM -RA BR OVC012  
TEMPO 1815/1819 2SM RA BR OVC008  
PROB40 1818/1822 3SM TSRA BKN015CB  
FM182300 08012G20KT P6SM BKN020

Conflicting Meteorologist Opinions (1600Z)
ZAU-style “facility met” is not relevant here; use N90 vs DCC met conflict.

DCC Meteorologist:  
“Boundary is diffuse. I still think showers dominate, with isolated embedded TS possible but not organized. Confidence medium that we avoid a full shutdown.”

Airline Weather (UAL/JetBlue combined rep):  
“We see higher instability and think TS will be more widespread 18–21Z, potentially forcing stops and reroutes in the NYC terminal area.”

Instructor Prompt: “How do you incorporate disagreement into your plan? What do you tell stakeholders—without overreacting or dismissing risk?”

Expected Trainee Answer Elements:
Acknowledge uncertainty; define objective triggers (radar echoes/top heights, lightning within X NM, N90 gate closures).
Prepare a time-boxed tactical posture: “If TS impacts final/departure corridor, we execute GS for X minutes, then reassess.”

Stakeholder Dialogue Block 2: Compression + Convective Trigger Call (1715Z)
N90 TMU: “Cells are developing west-southwest. Deviations are starting. If this expands, we’ll lose the arrival stream quickly. We want to stay open, but we need protection from a surge.”

CZY/CLE/CXY (adjacent flow, instructor as generic Center TMU):  
“We’re starting to see miles-in-trail increases into NYC. If you don’t take demand out, we’ll be forced into holding.”

Airlines (JBU, UAL):  
“We request a lower rate or a GS before we start diverting. We’d rather sit at the gate than hold over the shoreline.”

Instructor (to trainee): “Do you issue a Ground Stop, revise the GDP, or continue with tactical deviations only?”

Trainee Action Branches (Instructor-Controlled Outcomes)
Branch 1 — Tactical Approach (Minimal TS impact)
Instructor outcome: TS remains isolated; deviations manageable.  
Use minor MIT and tactical reroutes; no GS needed.  
GDP (if running) can be revised upward after 23Z when ceilings improve.

Instructor evaluation focus:
Did trainee avoid unnecessary constraints while protecting against holding?
Did trainee communicate triggers clearly and keep coordination tight?

Branch 2 — Weather Intensifies (TS impacts final; airport effectively pauses)
Instructor outcome: A TS cell crosses key arrival corridor; N90 requests immediate stop.  
Expected trainee action: Implement LGA Ground Stop (appropriate scope/time), then coordinate recovery via GDP revision or controlled release.

Sample Facility Request (inject):
N90: “We’re going to stop taking arrivals for 45 minutes. Departure corridors also impacted intermittently.”

Expected trainee response phraseology:
“Copy N90. We will implement a Ground Stop to LGA effective immediately for [scope], expected duration 45 minutes, with a reevaluation at [time]. Confirm you want all arrivals stopped, including airborne exemptions?”

Instructor Wrap-Up / Debrief Prompts
“What indicators told you the low ceilings were becoming a systemic capacity issue rather than a manageable compression?”
“How did you handle conflicting convective forecasts without losing stakeholder confidence?”
“If you ran a GDP, what were your revision triggers, and did you communicate them clearly?”
“If you issued a GS, did you right-size the scope and duration, and did you plan the recovery?”

End State (Target Learning Objectives)
Manage IFR + gusts producing sustained compression.
Decide between GDP vs wait-and-see, with clear decision logic.
Handle forecast disagreement professionally and translate uncertainty into actionable triggers.
Execute tactical measures (MIT/reroutes/GS) and coordinate efficiently with N90, Tower, and users.

Prompt: Generate a severe weather scenario for DFW involving convective weather that requires the student to balance arrival gates. The student will be required to consider multiple courses of action, including tactical moves, implementing a national playbook, etc.
Scenario:
Severe Weather Training Scenario — DFW Gate Balance / Playbook Decision (Level 3)

Background / Scenario Setup (Start Time: 1030Z)
Position: ATCSCC Severe Weather Desk (DFW Focus)  
Shift Focus: Convective development west/northwest of DFW, eastbound motion, progressive gate impacts requiring arrival gate balancing, and a decision between tactical management vs structured playbook/reroutes.

At 1030Z, the NAS is stable overall, but the morning mesoscale discussion highlights a strengthening boundary across north-central TX. DFW is running normal west-flow operations with high acceptance and no active initiatives.

Initial METAR/TAF package available to the student:
METAR KDFW 101053Z 18006KT P6SM FEW035 SCT080 BKN200 25/18 A2992 RMK AO2  
TAF KDFW 101040Z 1012/1112 18008KT P6SM SCT040 BKN200  
  FM101500 19012G18KT P6SM SCT050 BKN120  
  PROB30 1018/1022 3SM TSRA BKN025CB  
  FM102200 21010KT 5SM -SHRA VCTS BKN030CB  
  TEMPO 1023/1102 2SM TSRA OVC015CB  
  FM110300 22008KT P6SM SCT030 BKN080  

Instructor note: The student has full access to FSM, TSD, CEDAR, IDAC, SWIM/NTML, and may request additional weather briefings at any time.

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “Based on the current DFW TAF sequence and convective risk window, what is your initial concern for DFW capacity—field closure, gate impacts, or both—and what TM tools would you stage first?”

Expected trainee themes:
Identify 18–02Z as highest-impact window with escalating risk from VCTS → TSRA → possible ground stop conditions.
Recognize gate impacts likely precede full field impact (west/northwest storms affecting typical inbound corridors first).
Stage options: tactical reroutes, gate balancing, MIT/AFP coordination, GDP planning, and playbook evaluation.

Meteorologist Briefing Scripts (Uncertainty Built In)

1040Z — DCC Meteorologist (Baseline Brief)
DCC Met: “Current confidence is moderate that storms develop west-northwest of DFW late morning and mature into a broken line. Motion is generally east to east-southeast. The key uncertainty is how quickly coverage fills in. If it stays scattered, DFW can work tactically with deviations; if it consolidates, expect gate closures and intermittent field impacts in the 18–02Z window.”

Instructor coaching point: Ensure student asks:
“What’s the earliest time we lose a primary arrival gate?”  
“What tops and intensity—are we talking deviation-only or hard-block?”  
“Any expectation of training/backbuilding near the Metroplex?”  
“Is there a likely split impact (NW arrival gate first, then SW)?”  

1105Z — ZFW Center Weather (Contrasting View)
ZFW WX (via phone/bridge): “We’re leaning toward faster upscale growth. The boundary looks primed; we expect more coverage than the TAF implies. Our concern is arrival gate saturation—we may have to shut down one arrival gate entirely for periods, and departures will be forced into fewer exit corridors.”

Instructor note: This sets up the student to manage disagreement and decide whether to structure early or wait tactically.

Traffic Picture / Starting Demand (Provided to Student)
At 1030Z, DFW demand is strong with the afternoon bank building.
Planned arrival demand (AAR desired): 90–100/hr building into the afternoon bank.
Projected convective risk window: 18–02Z (from TAF PROB/TEMPO and weather briefs).
Primary operational risk: arrival gate imbalance—one gate becomes unusable, forcing all demand onto remaining gate(s), causing airborne holding and diversion risk.

Decision Point 1 — Facility Call (1130Z)
ZFW Traffic Management Unit: “We’re starting to see early buildups NW of the Metroplex. No impacts yet, but we want to talk arrival gate balancing. If the northwest gate starts getting deviations, we’ll need your help shifting demand and keeping the final from getting saturated.”

Instructor: “What information do you need from ZFW before you propose any initiative?”

Expected trainee questions:
“Which gate(s) are you forecasting to be most constrained first—NW vs SW vs NE?”  
“Are you still getting any aircraft through the constrained gate or is it trending toward closure?”  
“What’s your estimated usable gate capacity if you have to run single-gate arrivals?”  
“Do you anticipate a period where all arrival gates are blocked (airport shutdown)?”  

Student Options (Branching Paths)
The student must choose and defend a strategy with incomplete information:

Option A — Tactical / Minimal Structure (Early)
Maintain normal flows; support ZFW/C90-style tactical deviation management.
Prepare contingency reroutes and a trigger-based GDP only if:
two arrival gates become restricted, or
holding exceeds facility tolerance, or
field impact becomes likely.

Option B — Structured Gate Balance + Early Reroutes
Implement arrival gate balancing immediately through targeted reroutes/MIT to distribute arrivals across available fixes.
Coordinate with adjacent centers for predictable acceptance.
Stage a GDP proposal to begin before peak impacts to avoid airborne holding.

Option C — National Playbook / Regional Play Implementation
If forecast confidence increases toward consolidation, transition to structured routing using a national playbook or prebuilt DFW convective routes (e.g., “DFW WEST-AROUND,” “SOUTH-AND-EAST FEED,” or other facility-preferred structures).
Use playbook to keep flows out of blocked gates and reduce tactical chaos.

Telcon Event 1 — “Gate Balance Planning” (1215Z)
Instructor (as ATCSCC TMU Lead): “We’re going to do a short DFW coordination call. Keep it tight: impacts, forecast, demand, proposed action, and confirm roles.”

Participants
ATCSCC Severe Weather (Student)
ZFW TMU
DFW ATCT / Airport Ops
D10 TRACON (DFW Approach)
Airline Dispatch: AAL, UAL, SWA (major users)
NTMO (Instructor role, as needed)

Scripted Dialogue (Prompt/Expected Answer Pairs)

Student (opening technique):  
“DFW stakeholders, this is Severe Weather. Objective for this call is to align on the likely gate impacts and decide whether we manage tactically or move to structured routing. I’ll go facility-by-facility, then users.”

ZFW TMU: “We’re still wide open right now, but storms NW will likely force deviations. Our big concern is we lose the northwest arrival gate first. If that happens, we’ll have to feed arrivals through a single gate and we’ll be holding.”

D10 TRACON: “Agree. If we lose the NW arrival feed, we can still land, but we’ll compress quickly. If it consolidates into a line, we could be in and out of airport shutdown posture.”

DFW ATCT/Ops: “We can manage volume if it’s balanced. What kills us is when everything shows up on one corner of the TRACON and we run out of room.”

AAL Dispatch: “We want to avoid diversions. If you expect the airport to go down later, we’d rather take delay on the ground than airborne holding.”

UAL Dispatch: “Same. We’d support an earlier program if it reduces diversions and keeps arrivals from stacking.”

SWA Dispatch: “We have a lot of turns at DAL and connections at DFW. Please don’t surprise us with a last-minute stop; give us structure early if you think gates are going to close.”

Instructor (to student): “What do you propose right now, with uncertainty still moderate?”

Expected student response elements (not verbatim):
Summarize forecast and uncertainty.
Propose trigger-based plan:
Tactical until X,
then gate-balance reroutes,
then GDP/GS if field impact.
Set next update time and clarify responsibilities.

Weather Escalation Update (1400Z)
METAR KDFW 101400Z 19010G16KT P6SM SCT045 BKN100 28/19 A2988 RMK AO2  
DCC Met Update (1405Z): “Coverage is increasing faster than earlier. Confidence rising that storms form a broken line and may block the northwest arrival corridor intermittently by 17–18Z. Still some uncertainty whether it becomes a continuous line.”

Instructor inject: Provide a simulated radar depiction showing cells building NW and a developing outflow boundary.

Decision Point 2 — ZFW Concern vs TRACON Preference (1500Z)
ZFW TMU: “We’re seeing the first organized cells. We may have to close the NW arrival gate soon. If you wait, we’re going to be holding. We want you to start moving demand now.”

D10 TRACON (conflict posture): “We can still take arrivals. Let us run it tactically. Don’t lock us into a low-rate posture too early—we’re still landing and departures are moving.”

Instructor: “How do you manage this disagreement and keep the plan coherent?”

Expected trainee actions:
Acknowledge both positions.
Ask for measurable triggers:
gate closure criteria,
max holding tolerance,
expected gate throughput under deviations.
Propose phased plan: tactical now, structured in 30–60 minutes if thresholds hit.
Confirm next telcon time and exact decision points.

Telcon Event 2 — “TMI Decision Call” (1545Z)
Student (conference control technique):  
“We’re going to make a decision on structure for 17–20Z. I’ll summarize forecast, then ZFW/D10 state constraints, then users provide risk tolerance. After that, I’ll state the plan and capture objections.”

Facility Positions
ZFW: “We want structured gate balancing now. If NW gate closes, we can’t deliver what TRACON wants. We need demand reduced or redistributed.”

D10: “We want to stay open as long as possible. If you reduce too early, we lose throughput unnecessarily.”

Airline Requests (Lower Rates to Limit Diversions)
AAL: “If you think we’re going to lose a gate and then the field, we’d rather run something conservative—plan for a much lower posture when storms arrive. We can support a reduced rate so we don’t end up diverting.”  
UAL: “We’d support a conservative rate as well; we’d rather see something equivalent to a 56-type posture during peak storm impact than a late ground stop.”

Instructor prompt: “What are you going to do and how will you message it so it doesn’t sound like guesswork?”

Expected student response:
Choose one:
Tactical with pre-coordinated reroutes and a ready GDP,
or immediate structured playbook with gate balancing.
Communicate as: forecast + impact mechanism + decision trigger + next update.
Use “Here’s what we know / Here’s what we don’t / Here’s what we’re doing now.”

Execution Phase — Two Scenario Paths

Path 1: Tactical Success (Storms Scattered)
1700Z update (Instructor inject): Cells remain scattered; NW gate is partially usable with deviations.  
ZFW: “We’re getting aircraft through in gaps. No full gate closure yet.”  
D10: “We can keep landing with vectors and spacing.”

Student task:  
Coordinate tactical reroutes only for the worst-impacted flows.  
Implement MIT to protect the single busiest arrival stream and prevent oversaturation.  
Keep airlines informed of likely compression without triggering unnecessary GDP.

Instructor check: Student must articulate:
how they’re balancing gates without formal playbook,
what trigger causes shift to GDP or playbook,
how they’ll avoid last-minute surprises.

Path 2: Weather Intensifies (Gate Closure + Shutdown)
1705Z update (Instructor inject): Storm line consolidates; NW arrival gate becomes unusable; departures begin to funnel into limited corridors.  
ZFW: “NW arrival gate is effectively closed. We’re feeding everything through the remaining gate(s) and we’re starting to hold.”  
DFW Ops: “Surface is backing up. If arrivals keep coming in unbalanced, we’ll gridlock.”

Student required actions:
Implement structured gate balancing via reroutes/playbook selection.
Coordinate with ZFW/D10 on:
which arrival fixes remain viable,
any fix balancing requirements,
expected reduced acceptance.
If holding/diversions imminent, transition to:
Ground Stop (tiered as appropriate), then
GDP out of the stop once a sustainable acceptance rate is defined.

Instructor inject (1730Z): Lightning within ramp area triggers intermittent ground operations slowdown; risk of temporary field stop increases.

Playbook / Structure Decision (Instructor-Controlled Prompt)
Instructor: “If the line is consolidating and the NW gate is closed, what structure do you implement: tactical reroutes only, or a national/regional playbook? Defend your choice.”

Expected trainee answer themes:
Use playbook when:
coverage is medium/high,
gate closure is sustained,
tactical deviations cause unpredictable sector loading,
holding/diversions rising.
Stay tactical when:
gaps exist,
impacts are short-lived,
facility can meter locally without spillover.

Instructor-Provided “Conference Control” Techniques (Practice Items)
Student must demonstrate at least 3 of the following during telcons:
Agenda + timebox: “Two minutes forecast, two minutes constraints, two minutes users.”  
Decision framing: “We are deciding between tactical-only and structured playbook beginning 17Z.”  
Evidence statement: “ZFW reports NW gate closure and holding starting now; demand exceeds single-gate throughput.”  
Objection capture: “I have UAL requesting a 56 posture noted; D10 prefers staying open—captured.”  
Clear trigger language: “If holding exceeds X minutes or second gate restricts, we move to GS then GDP.”  
Close with actions: “We will publish reroute advisory by HHMMZ; next update call at HHMMZ.”

End-State / Evaluation Criteria
Instructor evaluates the trainee on:
Identification of gate imbalance as the operational failure mode before full shutdown.
Proper sequencing of tools: tactical → structured reroute/playbook → GS → GDP as conditions worsen.
Ability to manage facility disagreement (ZFW wants early structure; TRACON wants wide open).
Incorporation of airline diversion-risk concerns and requests for lower rates without losing operational credibility.
Clear, controlled telcon conduct with measurable triggers and explicit next steps.

Simulation End
Simulation ends when the student has either:
successfully managed tactically through the peak (Path 1), or
executed a gate-balance structure plus a shutdown posture (GS into GDP) and stabilized the post-impact recovery plan (Path 2).

Prompt: Generate a severe weather scenario for ZAU/ORD arrivals involving convective weather. There is significant uncertainty in the forecast, and conflicting information on how the forecasts will develop. The student will be required to choose between a tactical approach and implementation of an ORD playbook.
Scenario:
ZAU/ORD Convective Uncertainty — Severe Weather Training Scenario (Terminal/En Route Interface)

Background / Scenario Setup (Start 1030Z)
Position: ATCSCC TMU — Severe Weather Desk / Regional Coordination (ZAU focus)  
Date/Sim Time: Start 1030Z, run through ~1900Z  
Primary Airport: Chicago O’Hare (ORD)  
Secondary Coordination Airports (for demand/reroute impacts): MDW, MKE, DTW (as alternates/diversion magnets)

Operational Baseline at 1030Z:  
ORD is in west flow, landing and departing normally.  
ZAU reports “routine” gate availability, but convective ingredients are present northwest of the terminal area with high model spread.  
Student has access to: FSM/ASPM demand, TSD, WITI, Coded departure routes, FEA/AFP tools, and the playbook library (ORD-related playbooks available).

Initial METAR/TAF (as issued to the student at 1030Z):  
METAR KORD 181051Z 26008KT 10SM FEW030 SCT060 18/12 A2998 RMK AO2  
TAF KORD 181100Z 1812/1918 27010KT P6SM SCT040 BKN080  
TEMPO 1816/1819 4SM TSRA BKN025CB  
PROB30 1817/1821 2SM TSRA BKN015CB  
FM182100 30018G28KT P6SM VCSH SCT030 BKN060  
FM190100 32010KT P6SM SCT040  
FM191200 29012KT P6SM SCT050

Instructor Note (weather intent): Storms form NW and track E/SE with uncertainty: either gate impacts only (manageable with tactical reroutes/MIT) or direct terminal/final impacts (requiring shutdown/GS and playbook implementation).

Assessment Question (1035Z)
Instructor: “Based on the current TAF and the convective uncertainty northwest of the field, which parts of the operation are most at risk first: ORD arrival gates, departure gates, or the final approach course? What would be your first indicator that capacity will degrade?”

Expected Trainee Answer (target elements):  
First risk is ZAU arrival gate availability from the NW/W side; possible need for gate balancing and early coordination with C90.  
Watch for: build-ups along primary arrival fixes, increasing deviations, MIT increases, and C90 acceptance rate erosion before field shutdown.

Progressive Weather / ATIS Narrative (Injected Updates)
Update 1 — 1200Z: “It might stay north”
METAR KORD 181151Z 27012KT 10SM SCT035 BKN070 19/12 A2996 RMK AO2  
ZAU Center Weather brief to student: “Convection initiation possible 1530–1700Z northwest of ORD. Confidence moderate on storms existing; low confidence on whether they line up into a boundary that blocks the west gates.”

Update 2 — 1400Z: “More organized than expected”
METAR KORD 181351Z 28016G22KT 10SM BKN050 20/12 A2993 RMK AO2  
TAF AMD KORD 181400Z 1814/1920 29015G25KT P6SM BKN050  
TEMPO 1816/1818 5SM -SHRA VCTS BKN040CB  
PROB30 1817/1820 2SM TSRA BKN020CB  
FM182000 31018G28KT 4SM TSRA BKN025CB  
FM182300 33012KT P6SM VCSH SCT030 BKN060  
DCC Weather guidance: “We’re still split—some guidance keeps the core north near RFD; other guidance brings a broken line across the west arrivals into ORD.”

Update 3 — 1630Z: “Gate blockages begin”
METAR KORD 181621Z 30020G30KT 7SM -SHRA VCTS BKN035CB 19/13 A2989 RMK AO2 LTG DSNT NW  
ZAU reports increasing deviations and compression into the primary arrival streams.

Decision Point 1 (1215Z–1330Z): Tactical vs. Playbook Preparation
Instructor Prompt: “You have uncertainty. Do you:  
1) Stay tactical (MIT, minor reroutes, monitoring) and avoid publishing structure early, or  
2) Build and park an ORD playbook now with an implementation trigger?”

Acceptable Trainee Options (instructor should allow either):
Option A — Tactical posture: monitor, coordinate MIT, prep GS template, prep GDP shell but do not publish.  
Option B — Playbook posture: build/park ORD playbook routes and brief ZAU/C90; define trigger: e.g., “when west gates go unavailable / SWAP / or ORD acceptance drops below X.”

Instructor Success Criteria: trainee articulates triggers, impacts on diversion risk, and the operational cost of being late to structure.

Stakeholder Dialogue: Pre-Telcon Coordination (1335Z)
Agent: ZAU TMU (Center Flow)
ZAU: “We’re watching builds northwest. If that line sets up, our west arrival gates will be the first to go. We can’t promise clean feeds to C90 after 17Z. Recommend you be ready to structure; tactical might not keep up once deviations start stacking.”

Expected Trainee Response:  
Acknowledge risk to west gates; request timing and confidence; ask if MIT is already increasing; ask for worst-case gate closure assumptions.

Agent: C90 TMU (TRACON Flow)
C90: “We’re wide open right now and want to keep it that way. We can work pop-ups. Don’t lock us into a low program rate too early—storms might stay north.”

Expected Trainee Response:  
Confirm current acceptance rate; clarify when they expect to lose finals vs. just gate constraints; propose conditional plan (tactical now, published structure on trigger).

Agent: ORD ATCT (Tower / ATC System Command)
ORD: “Surface is moving, no EDCT pain yet. If we start taking diversions, it’ll get ugly fast. If you’re going to do something, don’t wait until we’re saturated.”

Expected Trainee Response:  
Discuss diversion risk; coordinate likely ground stop vs GDP transition; request runway config expectations and lightning ramp constraints.

Telcon 1 — ORD Weather Uncertainty Planning Call (1405Z)
Participants: ATCSCC Student (chair), ZAU TMU, C90 TMU, ORD ATCT, DCC Weather, Airline Reps (UAL/AAL/SWA as scripted)

DCC Weather Brief
DCC Weather: “Confidence medium storms will develop NW of ORD. Confidence low to medium on field impact. Primary uncertainty is whether storms remain a broken cluster near RFD or form a line that shifts east across the west gates 17–19Z.”

Facility Positions (Conflict Required)
ZAU: “Our concern is gate blockages. Once deviations start, we lose the ability to feed you evenly. Tactical MIT alone won’t prevent airborne holding. We recommend planning for a significant reduction by 17Z and being prepared for a stop if the line crosses.”

C90: “We want to stay wide open as long as possible. We can absorb some deviations and manage finals until it hits the TRACON. A GDP now is premature.”

Airline Inputs (request lower rates to limit diversions)
UAL Dispatch: “We’re not asking you to guess exact impacts, but we want to avoid a posture that creates mass diversions. If storms are even a real possibility, we want a protective rate. If you publish something, keep it conservative—56 or less during the 17Z hour to keep arrivals from launching into a shut airport.”

AAL Dispatch: “We can support reduced rates during impact, but if you go too low too early, you’ll create unnecessary EDCTs. We prefer a step-down plan with a clear trigger.”

SWA Dispatch: “Midway will absorb diversions and gate holds too. If ORD locks up, our network takes it on the chin. We prefer early coordination and predictable structure.”

Instructor Prompt During Telcon
Instructor (as ATCSCC lead): “Chair’s question: Do we publish a structured plan now, or do we run tactical and accept the risk of a reactive stop later?”

Expected Trainee Outcome (either path acceptable):
Path 1: Tactical-first plan with explicit triggers and pre-built templates (GS + GDP shell).  
Path 2: Playbook-first plan (parked immediately; publish upon trigger or as advisory).

Trainee Action Menu (must choose one)
Option 1 — No TMI / Monitor Only
Continue at high rate; accept risk of abrupt stop and diversions.

Option 2 — Tactical Mitigation
Coordinate MIT increases with ZAU arrivals to C90.  
Coordinate minor reroutes around NW build-ups.  
Prepare 1st-tier Ground Stop template and GDP shell (rates ready).

Option 3 — Implement ORD Playbook (expected evaluation target)
Select an ORD convective playbook appropriate for NW-to-E impacts (instructor will provide the local playbook name used at the facility).  
Publish structured reroutes to balance arrivals away from blocked gates.  
Coordinate with ZAU/C90 on acceptance and fix balancing constraints.  
Prepare transition to Ground Stop if field becomes unusable.

Weather Materialization (Instructor-Controlled Fork)
Fork A (supports tactical approach initially): 1500Z–1600Z storms stall north
ZAU deviations manageable; C90 maintains higher acceptance temporarily.

Fork B (drives playbook necessity): 1615Z–1730Z gates collapse
Storm line pushes east; west gates become unusable; ZAU cannot deliver.

Instructor chooses Fork B by default for Level 3 severity.

Inject: 1635Z — ZAU Escalation Call (Fork B)
ZAU: “We’re in the soup now. West gates are blocking. We’re running SWAP and deviations; we cannot feed the rate you’re expecting. If you don’t structure now, you’re going to be holding and diverting in the next 30 minutes.”

Expected Trainee Response:  
Activate planned structure: either publish the playbook now or issue a 1st-tier GS while building GDP out.

Telcon 2 — Implementation / Escalation Conference (1650Z)
Participants: Student (chair), ZAU, C90, ORD, DCC Weather, Airlines

DCC Weather (updated)
DCC Weather: “Storms have organized more than earlier guidance. Expect continued impacts through at least 1830Z. Confidence medium on continued gate blockage; confidence medium-low on a direct field shutdown—depends on whether the line clips the final.”

ZAU Position
ZAU: “Gate blockage is real-time. We recommend immediate structure and a protective posture to prevent diversions. We can’t support wide open.”

C90 Position (conflict persists)
C90: “We’re still trying to run it. If you clamp down too hard, you’ll strand capacity we can still use. We want to avoid a big program until we’re forced.”

Airlines (explicit request)
UAL: “We need you to prevent airborne holding. If you’re going to publish a program, we want 56 during the impacted window and we want you ready to go to a stop if the field gets clipped.”

AAL: “Support reduced rate if it matches real constraints, but don’t set a floor that guarantees under-delivery if storms slide north.”

Instructor Prompt
Instructor: “Chair: Decide. Are you staying tactical and risking a reactive stop, or implementing the ORD playbook now with a protective rate posture?”

Expected Trainee Decision (target):  
Implement ORD playbook now; coordinate with facilities; set clear triggers for a Ground Stop if finals are impacted.

Airport Shutdown Event (1735Z–1810Z)
Inject at 1735Z:  
ORD reports lightning/ramp constraints and inability to sustain arrivals.

Agent: ORD ATCT
ORD: “We’re unable to keep the arrival bank moving. Ramp has intermittent lightning holds and we’re saturating the surface. If this core drifts over the final, we’ll be forced into a stop.”

Expected Trainee Response:  
Issue Ground Stop (scope decision required: 1st-tier vs expanded) and immediately plan GDP out of GS with step-up recovery.

Mandatory Instructor Check: student makes required notifications and properly coordinates cancellation/extension criteria.

Recovery and Revision (1810Z–1900Z)
Inject at 1810Z: storms begin shifting east; gates partially reopen; field condition improves unevenly.

Agent: C90 TMU
C90: “We can start taking arrivals again, but we need controlled flow. Don’t cancel everything at once—give us a step-up.”

Agent: ZAU TMU
ZAU: “We can deliver again on limited gates. If you cancel the stop, do it with structure still in place and manage the restart.”

Expected Trainee Actions:  
Cancel GS when conditions allow.  
Maintain playbook structure until gate availability stabilizes.  
Revise GDP rates upward in steps rather than immediate return to peak.

Instructor Wrap / Debrief Questions
“What exact trigger(s) did you use to move from tactical to structured playbook implementation?”  
“How did ZAU’s gate-blockage concern differ operationally from C90’s desire to stay wide open?”  
“What did the airlines mean by requesting a 56 rate—what problem were they trying to prevent?”  
“If the storms had stayed north (Fork A), what would you have done to unwind or avoid unnecessary TMIs?”  
“What are the risks of delaying structure until after airborne holding begins?”

End State
Scenario ends after the student has either:  
Successfully implemented the ORD playbook, executed a shutdown posture (GS), and transitioned into recovery (GDP revision), or  
Chosen tactical-only and then been forced into reactive GS/GDP under time pressure (graded as higher workload and higher diversion risk).

Prompt: Generate a severe weather scenario involving a comprehensive "Day in the NAS". The weather will span from northern Texas to Tennessee/West Virginia, and the student will be required to change strategies throughout the day, including the use of TRANSCON routes, plays, etc.
Scenario:
Severe Weather Training Scenario: “Day in the NAS” (Comprehensive, Multi-Strategy)

Background / Scenario Setup (ZDC Severe Weather Position)
Start Time: 1030 UTC  
Student Position: DCC Severe Weather Coordinator (Day-in-the-NAS event management)  
Tools Available: TSD, FSM, TBFM demand, CCFP/TCF packages, NTML, playbook library, route builder/parking, collaborative telcon lines.

Operational Theme: A multi-state convective day with evolving confidence and shifting constraints. Weather spans northern Texas through Arkansas/Missouri into Tennessee and WV, requiring multiple strategy changes: tactical deviation → regional structured routing → TRANSCON flow management → ground programs / stops at impacted terminals → recovery and reroutes as new convection initiates.

At scenario start, traffic demand is seasonally high:
Strong morning eastbound push (ZFW/ZME/ZTL/ZDC corridors).
Midday ORD/ATL/CLT/IAD/EWR/LGA flows competing for routes through the same constrained bands.
Late-day second round redevelopment east of the Mississippi drives a final strategy shift.

Initial Weather Picture (1030Z Brief)
DCC Meteorologist (Instructor Voice):  
“Today is a long-duration convective day. We expect multiple clusters. First initiation is already occurring from north TX into SE OK. By late morning and early afternoon, expect a broken but expanding convective line into AR/MO, then a more organized band into TN and KY, with additional growth into WV later. Confidence is medium on the timing of the first line, higher that we’ll have at least one significant constraint band across the mid-South.”

Student Setup Expectation:  
Instructor ensures the student:
Pulls up national view and key choke points (MEM area, BNA/CHA/AVL corridors, ZTL/ZDC boundary gates).
Identifies which flows are most exposed: Texas → Southeast, Southwest → East Coast, Midwest → Southeast.
Prepares an initial “minimum necessary” posture and a plan to scale up.

Assessment Question
Instructor:  
“Based on the 1030Z weather briefing, which flows or metro areas do you expect to become the first capacity problems in the next 3–6 hours, and what indicators will tell you it’s time to shift from tactical deviations to structured routing?”

Expected Student Answer (Key Points):
Initial exposure: ZFW eastbound and ZME/ZTL transitions, especially around MEM/BNA corridors.
Indicators: closure of common jet routes, increasing deviations > 20–40 NM, sector saturation, MIT/AFP triggers, increasing airborne holding at hubs, constrained gates (arrival/departure) and EDCT non-compliance risk.
Strategy trigger: when deviations begin producing systemic compression (MIT increases, gate closures) across multiple centers → move to structured routes / plays.

Scenario Flow Overview
Phase 1 (1030–1300Z): Tactical deviation management; prepare routes and coordination nets.
Phase 2 (1300–1700Z): Primary convective band expands: structured routing (regional plays) and initial TRANSCON posture.
Phase 3 (1700–2100Z): Peak impact: gate closures, miles-in-trail, AFPs, GDPs/GSs at impacted terminals; conference control under stress.
Phase 4 (2100–0100Z): Partial recovery then second-round redevelopment into TN/WV; strategy shift again; recovery management.

Instructor may accelerate/slow phases depending on trainee performance.

Phase 1: Tactical → Pre-Planning (1030–1300Z)

Inject 1 (1045Z): ZFW Advisory
ZFW Traffic Management:  
“We’re seeing early build-ups north of the Metroplex. For now it’s deviations, but it’s already affecting the typical eastbound corridors. No formal gate closures yet.”

Instructor Prompt:  
“What are you doing right now, before the first major constraint forms?”

Expected Student Actions:
Declare monitoring posture, start a short list of candidate playbooks for:
ZFW/ZHU eastbound push into ZME/ZTL
West Coast/Southwest → East Coast flows transiting TX/AR/TN
Ask meteorologist for:
Movement speed/direction, expected coverage category, and time windows of maximum impact.
Identify “next decision point” time for a national telcon.

Controlling-the-Conversation Technique (Practice #1)
Instructor Coaching Line (to student):  
“When you call a facility, lead with: what we’re seeing, what we expect, what we’re going to do, and what we need from them. End with: ‘Any questions or concerns?’—that’s control without sounding dismissive.”

Phase 2: Structured Routing / TRANSCON Decision (1300–1700Z)

Inject 2 (1315Z): ZME Escalation
ZME TMU:  
“The convection is expanding across our southwest corner. We’re starting to lose consistent throughput for eastbound traffic. Expect MIT requests if this fills in.”

Meteorologist Update (1330Z)
DCC Meteorologist:  
“Confidence is increasing that we’ll have a broken line from north TX through AR into western TN. Coverage is expected to be medium with embedded higher intensity cores. Most of the usable gaps close from 15Z to 19Z in the mid-South corridor.”

Instructor Prompt:  
“Do you stay tactical, or transition to a structured plan? If structured, do you go regional only—or TRANSCON as well?”

Expected Student Answer:
Transition to structured plan now due to increasing confidence and anticipated loss of gaps 15–19Z.
Use regional routes for the highest-volume affected flows immediately.
Prepare TRANSCON routes for major market flows likely to saturate alternates once the band matures.

Stakeholder Dialogue: Pre-Telcon (Facilities Only)
Student (expected phrasing):  
“This is [Name], Severe Weather. We’re seeing expansion north TX into AR with expected medium coverage and reduced gaps 15–19Z. Plan is structured routing for the eastbound push to preserve throughput and reduce tactical chaos. I’ll outline proposed routes, expected start time, and the traffic groups covered.”

ZFW:  
“We can support routes that stop the churn. We’re already seeing reroutes stacking up.”

ZME:  
“We need predictability. Tactical is going to choke our departures and arrivals once the line fills.”

ZTL:  
“We’ll take the rerouted volume but you need to consider our constraints downstream—BNA/CHA area may be tight.”

Phase 3: Peak Impact (1700–2100Z)
Training Objective: Student must manage multiple simultaneous constraints, choose and layer TMIs, and handle conflict in telcons while maintaining control.

Inject 3 (1705Z): First Major Break
ZME:  
“We’ve effectively lost our preferred eastbound corridors. We’re going to start running higher MIT on flows coming into us. Expect airborne holding if we don’t get demand under control.”

ZFW:  
“We’re now seeing significant deviation requirements. Traffic complexity is rising quickly.”

Instructor Prompt
“List your available options from least to most restrictive, and justify which one you’re choosing now.”

Expected Options (Student Should Mention):
No assistance / monitoring only (not appropriate now).
Tactical moves: fix balancing, local reroutes.
Structured reroutes (regional plays).
MIT programs (tier-based).
AFP for constrained flows.
GDP/GS for specific high-risk terminals if arrival gates or final approach corridors are impacted.
Broad TRANSCON routes when regional options saturate.

National Telcon Script (1730Z): TRANSCON + Regional Plays
