Participants: DCC Severe Weather (Student), DCC Meteorologist, ZFW TMU, ZME TMU, ZTL TMU, ZDC TMU, Airline Dispatch (AAL/DAL/UAL/SWA), DCC Ops Supervisor (Instructor voice if needed)

Student Opening (Conference Control Required)
Student (expected):  
“Good afternoon, this is [Name], Severe Weather. Purpose of this call is to align on a structured routing strategy for the convective band from north TX through AR into TN. I’ll cover: current impacts, 15–19Z expectation, proposed routing posture (regional + TRANSCON where needed), and implementation timing. We’ll take questions at the end.”

DCC Meteorologist
“Medium coverage with embedded cores is expected through the 19Z hour across the mid-South corridor. Gaps will be unreliable during peak.”

ZME
“We’re already restricting throughput. Without structure, we’ll be holding or forcing ad hoc deviations that ripple everywhere.”

ZTL
“Our concern is the downstream squeeze. If you dump volume into one corridor, we’ll saturate quickly.”

Airline Pushback
UAL Dispatch:  
“We need predictability, but we also need to avoid massive reroute fuel penalties. Can you keep at least one primary eastbound path available?”

SWA Dispatch:  
“If we don’t slow demand, we’re going to see diversions into the Southeast. We’d support an AFP for the worst corridor rather than uncontrolled airborne holding.”

Student Decision Point (Expected)
Implement structured regional routes immediately for the highest volume flows.
Implement TRANSCON routes selectively for flows that would otherwise saturate the same constrained corridor.
Consider an AFP targeted to the corridor where capacity is clearly limited.

Student Close (Control + Commitment)
Student (expected):  
“Plan is set: we’ll implement the structured routes at 1745Z for the primary eastbound push, with a review at 1830Z based on observed gap availability. We’ll keep one alternate corridor available where feasible, but we are prioritizing system stability. Any questions or concerns?”

Terminal Impacts (Layered Problems)
Instructor introduces terminal constraints as the mid-South band reaches major hubs and East Coast routes.

Inject 4 (1835Z): ATL and CLT Arrival Compression
ZTL:  
“Convection is now impacting arrival streams into ATL. We’re seeing compression and increased MIT.”

Airline Dispatch (DAL):  
“We’re concerned ATL will go into a stop-and-go. If you can keep arrivals metered with an AFP or GDP-like posture, we can avoid diversions.”

Instructor Prompt:  
“Do you add terminal TMIs or keep it route-only? What data do you need before deciding?”

Expected Student Answer:
Request arrival counts, holding status, gate/runway acceptance, and MIT values.
If holding/diversions imminent, coordinate for AFP/GDP as appropriate (depending on facility posture and duration).
Maintain route posture to reduce chaos while adding targeted TMIs only where needed.

Phase 4: Recovery, Then Second Round (2100–0100Z)

Inject 5 (2105Z): Temporary Improvement
DCC Meteorologist:  
“We’re seeing a brief decrease in coverage in parts of AR and west TN. This is not a full clear—expect redevelopment eastward toward TN/KY/WV later.”

ZDC:  
“We can take some volume, but watch the Appalachians corridor—if storms redevelop there, it’ll pinch our routes into IAD/BWI and the NE.”

Instructor Prompt
“How do you manage recovery without whiplash? What’s your revision philosophy?”

Expected Student Answer:
Stepwise relaxation: revise routes/MIT incrementally, avoid sudden release of pent-up demand.
Communicate “conditional recovery” with next check time.
Keep contingency routes parked for rapid reactivation.

Inject 6 (2230Z): Redevelopment into TN/WV
ZTL:  
“New convection is building east of the earlier line. This will impact eastbound transitions into ZDC and flows toward the Mid-Atlantic.”

ZDC:  
“We’re starting to see route saturation potential. If this grows, we’ll need structured entries and possible AFPs for IAD/BWI flows.”

Short Telcon Script (2245Z): Recovery + New Constraint
Student (expected):  
“This is [Name], Severe Weather. We’re in partial recovery west of TN, but we’re seeing redevelopment into TN/WV that will constrain entries into ZDC for Mid-Atlantic flows. Plan: maintain current structured routes for stability, add a targeted program for the affected entry corridor if observed coverage meets threshold. Next reassessment at 2315Z.”

Airline Dispatch (AAL):  
“Request: don’t open the floodgates, we’re already stretched on alternates. We’d rather hold rates than divert.”

ZDC:  
“Agree—controlled release is better than a surge into a bottleneck.”

Instructor Evaluation Gates (What to Watch For)

Key Competencies
Strategy changes at the right time (tactical → structured → TRANSCON/AFP → recovery → renewed structure).
Data-driven justification (not just “weather is bad”).
Conference control: structured brief, defined purpose, crisp plan, questions at end.
Stakeholder management: airlines want cost control; facilities want throughput and safety margins.
Revision discipline: stepwise changes, not oscillation.

Instructor Questions (Use Throughout)
“What is your next decision point time and what will trigger a change?”
“What flows are you protecting and why?”
“If this corridor fails, what is your backup plan?”
“What is your plan to prevent saturation downstream?”
“What do you need from each facility on this call to execute safely?”

End-State / Completion Criteria
Scenario ends when the student:
Executes at least two major strategy shifts with coherent rationale (e.g., structured routing + TRANSCON/AFP, then recovery, then renewed structure).
Demonstrates controlled, professional telcon technique.
Coordinates revisions and communicates impacts clearly to facilities and users.
Maintains system stability while balancing throughput vs. diversion risk.

END OF SIMULATION

Prompt: Generate a poor weather scenario for SFO
Scenario:
SFO Terminal Weather Disruption Scenario (Level 3)

Background / Scenario Setup (Start: 0800 UTC)
You are the Traffic Management Coordinator-in-Training responsible for SFO terminal impacts and adjacent Bay Area coordination. You have access to TSD, FSM, TFMS, and local SOPs for SFO/OAK/SJC, including arrival/departure configuration impacts and Miles-in-Trail (MIT) tools.

Current METAR (0800Z):  
SFO is in marine layer conditions with intermittent light drizzle. Ceiling is already down near ILS minimums and trending lower. Winds are light but forecast to increase and veer, creating the risk of runway configuration changes and compression on the final.

KLAX-style summary is not used here; use the TAF/METAR content below as your operational weather picture:

KSFO METAR 0800Z:  
KSFO 080056Z 26006KT 2SM -DZ BR OVC006 12/11 A3006 RMK AO2 SLP178

KSFO TAF (valid 0800Z–1400Z):  
KSFO 0800/0906 26006KT 3SM -DZ BR OVC008  
TEMPO 0802/0806 1SM -DZ FG OVC003  
FM080600 28010G18KT 2SM BR OVC005  
FM081000 30014G22KT 4SM -RA BR OVC007  
PROB30 0813/0816 1/2SM FG OVC002

Operational expectation: SFO is currently landing to west flow on ILS. If ceilings drop to 200–300 ft with reduced vis, plan for arrival compression, potential arrival stops, and significant airborne holding. Gusts after 0600Z increase the likelihood of go-arounds and missed approach saturation.

Local constraints to brief trainee:
SFO arrival acceptance rate is highly sensitive to ceiling/vis and missed-approach frequency.
OAK/SJC become diversion magnets; surface management and gate availability can degrade rapidly.
Bay Approach (NCT) will attempt to “stay open” until missed approaches and runway occupancy force reductions.

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: Based on the weather trend and forecast, what airport(s) and which part of the operation will face the first capacity issue—arrival acceptance, departures, or both—and what is your first indicator that SFO will not sustain normal rates?

Expected trainee answer (elements):
SFO will be primary capacity issue (arrivals first, then departures due to gate/spot saturation).
First indicator: ceiling trending to OVC003–005 with vis reductions; TEMPO 1SM/OVC003 plus gust increase → missed approaches and final compression.
Secondary concerns: diversion pressure to OAK/SJC; potential need to manage Bay Area arrival demand regionally.

Phase 1: Initial Coordination (0810Z–0900Z)

Stakeholder Dialogue 1: NCT (NorCal TRACON) calls
NCT Flow: “SFO’s trending down. We’re still landing, but the ceiling’s flirting with 600 and we’re seeing intermittent 1–2 miles in drizzle. If we go to 300 and a mile, we’ll need spacing and we’ll start losing the final. What are you thinking—program or wait?”

Trainee options (choose and justify):
Wait-and-see (monitor only): No initiative yet; coordinate triggers with NCT (e.g., sustained OVC004/vis ≤2SM, first go-around, or sustained arrival delays).
Proactive GDP: Model a GDP beginning 0930Z with reduced rates anticipating FM080600 and TEMPO/PROB30 deterioration.
Tactical MIT / compression management: Coordinate MIT from feeding Centers (ZLA, ZOA, ZSE, ZLC as applicable via flows) without a formal GDP yet.

Expected trainee response (good practice):
Confirm configuration, current acceptance rate, and planned reduction triggers.
Ask: “Are you seeing missed approaches yet? What’s the tower reporting on RVR/ceilings trend? Any gate holds starting?”

Stakeholder Dialogue 2: SFO Tower
SFO Tower: “We’re still west flow. Arrivals are making it, but we’ve had two go-arounds in the last 20 minutes. Ceiling looks uneven—one bank looks 800, then it drops fast.”

Expected trainee response:
Acknowledge go-arounds as early risk signal.
Coordinate with NCT on whether to publish an advisory and/or move to formal TMI posture.

Decision Point A (0900Z): Student Chooses Path
Path A: Proactive GDP
Instructor Prompt: If you choose a GDP, what start time and initial rate do you model, and what weather trigger would cause you to revise lower or convert to a ground stop?

Expected trainee actions:
Model GDP with stepped rates (higher early, lower during lowest ceiling window).
Build revision triggers tied to TEMPO FG OVC003, PROB30 1/2SM FG OVC002, and observed go-around rate.
Coordinate with NCT + SFO on “max sustainable AAR” and “stop criteria.”

Path B: Wait-and-See → GS into GDP
Instructor Prompt: If you choose to wait, what specific operational trigger will force you into a ground stop, and how will you protect against diversion spirals?

Expected trainee actions:
Define trigger: e.g., sustained OVC003 with vis ≤1SM, missed approach saturation, arrival holding >30 min, or SFO surface gridlock.
Prepare GDP shell and scope for rapid activation.
Coordinate diversion airport constraints early (OAK/SJC gate/ground delay risks).

Phase 2: Deterioration and Compression (0930Z–1100Z)

Event Inject (1005Z): Weather and performance degrade
SFO reports ceilings fluctuating around 500 ft with periodic dips. Gusts begin to build per forecast. NCT begins increasing spacing due to compression and missed approaches.

NCT Flow (1008Z): “We’re compressing. Final is unstable. We can’t run normal acceptance. If the next bank drops to 300 and a mile, we’ll start holding hard.”

Instructor Inject (traffic picture):
Increasing arrival demand from the east; multiple long-haul inbound aircraft nearing top-of-descent.
OAK and SJC are VFR but starting to receive early diversions from other coastal fields.

Stakeholder Dialogue 3: Airline Dispatch (Major carrier)
Dispatch: “We’re already seeing alternates trigger. We need a plan that reduces diversions. If you’re going to throttle, do it early so we can hold at origin instead of burning gas in the terminal area.”

Expected trainee response:
Explain current forecast uncertainty (TEMPO/PROB30 windows).
Offer options: GDP with conservative rates during worst window, or GS if/when the field drops below minima.
Commit to a decision timeline: “Next update in 15 minutes after NCT confirms sustainable rate.”

Decision Point B (1100Z): Formal TMI Required
At 1100Z, NCT reports sustained compression and missed approaches increasing. SFO indicates departure queue is growing due to gate holds and ramp constraints.

Telcon (Fac/Users) — Student Must Control the Conference
Student opening technique (expected):
Identify self and purpose: “This is SFO TMU. Purpose is to align on forecast impacts, acceptance rate, and whether we’re going GDP now or GS into GDP.”
Set order: “We’ll take NCT, then SFO, then ZOA, then users.”
Define decision point: “We will leave this call with one plan and triggers for revision.”

NCT
NCT Flow: “We can’t sustain normal ops. With these go-arounds and the ceiling variability, we’re looking at a reduced arrival posture. If it drops to 200–300 feet, we’ll stop arrivals.”

SFO Airport Ops
SFO Ops: “We’re close to gate saturation if arrivals keep landing while departures slow. Ramp is reporting intermittent holds when visibility drops.”

ZOA (Center)
ZOA TMU: “We’re seeing increasing deviations and holding requests. If you don’t put something in, we’ll stack them and start pushing diversions.”

Airline Requests
Dispatch (Carrier A): “We support a program now. A conservative rate is better than rolling ground stops.”
Dispatch (Carrier B): “We prefer wait-and-see; the ceiling might lift. But we need clear triggers.”

Student must decide and announce:
Option 1: GDP now with step-downs and explicit revision triggers.
Option 2: Immediate 1st-tier GS, then GDP once sustainable rate is defined.

Expected outcome: Student implements a Ground Stop into a GDP if missed approaches/ceilings worsen, or a GDP with aggressive revision triggers if conditions are marginal but degrading.

Phase 3: Lowest Conditions / Stop Arrivals (1130Z–1300Z)

Event Inject (1215Z): Field falls below sustainable minima
Tower reports visibility reduction and a ceiling drop consistent with the TEMPO/PROB30 worst-case window.

NCT Flow: “We just lost the final—multiple go-arounds, spacing is gone. We need you to stop SFO arrivals now.”

Expected trainee actions:
Issue SFO Ground Stop (define scope appropriate to capture imminent arrivals while minimizing unnecessary stops).
Coordinate holding/diversion risk with ZOA and adjacent facilities.
Build GDP out of the stop with low initial rates, then step-up if conditions recover.

Stakeholder Dialogue 4: OAK and SJC Coordination
OAK TMU: “We can take diversions but we’re not built for a sustained stream. If SFO stays down, we’ll hit gate limits quickly.”
SJC Ops: “We can accept some, but please don’t let it become uncontrolled.”

Expected trainee response:
Advise diversion management posture: request controlled release and prioritize aircraft with fuel constraints.
Coordinate with NCT on whether to meter diversions or hold at origin.

Phase 4: Recovery Decision / Program Revision (1300Z–1500Z)

Event Inject (1345Z): Improvement begins but remains fragile
Ceilings lift slightly; drizzle continues. Gusts persist. NCT can accept limited arrivals but warns that another dip will collapse the final again.

NCT Flow: “We can take some, but it’s a tight window. If you overfeed us, we’ll be right back in a stop.”

Instructor Prompt: Do you (a) cancel the GS and run a cautious GDP, (b) extend the GS and wait for sustained improvement, or (c) cancel and go tactical only?

Expected trainee decision quality indicators:
Uses observed trends, not only forecast.
Avoids abrupt rate jumps that trigger holding/diversions.
Communicates clear triggers for tightening again.

Instructor Notes / Evaluation Targets
The trainee should demonstrate:
Early identification of arrival compression risk from ceiling/vis + gusts + go-arounds.
Ability to choose between proactive GDP vs wait-and-see with a prepared GS→GDP transition.
Professional conference control: clear purpose, order of speakers, and firm decision summary.
Diversion mitigation: early coordination with OAK/SJC and managing gate saturation risk.
Use of explicit triggers for program revisions (weather and operational metrics).

End State (Target)
By 1500Z, SFO is operating under a controlled program posture with either:
A GDP that has been revised to match observed recovery, or
A GS that transitioned into a GDP and then gradually stepped up as conditions stabilized.

End of Simulation

Expected Student Actions:
Brief: “We are not pushing ORD traffic into MDW’s constrained side; we’ll cap additional feed and reassess every 30 minutes.”  
Ask ZAU: “Any MDW impacts or sector staffing constraints that change the plan?”

Instructor-Controlled Weather Evolution (Key Turns)
Turn A (Weather Materializes — Use to drive structured routing success)

KORD 131851Z 23015G25KT 4SM TSRA BKN025CB OVC070 22/19 A2981 RMK AO2 LTGICCC OHD

Student Prompt (instructor): “What’s your off-ramp plan? When do you terminate structured routing and return to normal?”

Evaluation Criteria (Instructor Notes)
Look for the student to:
Identify which arrival streams/fixes are first impacted by convection.  
Use decision triggers tied to operations: deviation magnitude, gate closure, holding, acceptance rate change, MIT requests.  
Demonstrate conference control: agenda, concise summaries, defer debates until after proposal.  
Communicate a clear plan: who is included, when it starts, when it ends, reassessment points.  
Coordinate ORD metro impacts (avoid shifting problems to MDW and adjacent flows).

End State
Scenario ends when the student:

ATCSCC Trainee (expected opening):  
“Let’s keep this tight. Purpose is to decide whether we need a KLGA GDP beginning 15Z based on the amended TAF and gate impacts. We’ll do weather, then N90/ZNY constraints, then airline preferences, then I’ll summarize and we’ll decide.”

DCC Met:  
“TAF amendment supports earlier impacts; TEMPO TSRA begins 15Z with higher probability after 20Z.”

ZNY TMU (concerned about gate blockages):  
“Our concern is gate reliability. If the northwest stream collapses we’ll be forced to meter hard; we may not be able to deliver even a modest rate consistently.”

N90 TMU (wants to stay open):  
“We want to stay as wide open as possible. If we over-control early, we’ll waste capacity. We can take deviations until we can’t.”

LGA Tower:  
“Right now we’re good. Once lightning gets close, we could see ramp constraints and departure interruptions. If a core hits the field, it’ll be abrupt.”

Airline Dispatch — JetBlue (requests lower rates):  
“If you think this is going to collapse, we’d rather you set a lower controlled rate up front to prevent diversions. We can work with something like 22–24 instead of chasing a higher number that won’t hold.”

Airline Dispatch — Delta (requests lower rates):  
“We’d prefer you plan conservatively. If you run a GDP, we’d support a lower rate—something equivalent to ~20–24 during the peak impact—to limit airborne holding.”

Airline Dispatch — American (operational pushback):  
“We can accept a program, but don’t crater the rate unless you’re confident the field goes down.”

Expected Trainee Summary / Decision (two acceptable endings depending on trainee choice):  
Path A (Proactive): Propose GDP start 15Z with reduced rate, include revision triggers; explain that the goal is to avoid holding/diversions given TEMPO and gate reliability risk.  
Path B (Delay decision): Hold off until 15Z confirmation but set explicit trigger for GS and commit to quick revision; high-risk path—make instructor press the consequences.

6) Execution Phase (Storms Materialize; Capacity Whiplash)

6.1 — 1530Z: Gate Compression Begins
ZNY TMU (call to trainee):  
“We’re starting to see reroutes and deviations. I can’t guarantee delivery. If you’re going to do a program, do it now before airborne holding spikes.”

Expected Trainee Actions:  
Decide: GDP/AFP now vs. tactical MIT with trigger  
Coordinate with N90 on realistic acceptance and “stop criteria”  
Provide clear customer comms: “expect revisions” / “do not plan on holding in the air”

6.2 — 1655Z: TSRA Over Field (Airport Unreliable)
N90 TMU: “We’re losing finals. I need arrivals stopped.”  
Expected Trainee Response:  
Implement Ground Stop (scope per instructor preference), coordinate duration, and start recovery planning.  
Immediate follow-ups: notify impacted centers/users; establish next telcon time; request updated weather.

DCC Met:  
“Core is crossing the field; expect worst conditions for the next 30–60 minutes, then uncertainty on redevelopment behind it.”

7) Instructor-Controlled Branching
Branch 1: Tactical Success (storms clip north)
Student who chose tactical MIT sees only minor TS impacts; short-lived reduced rate; no full shutdown.
Evaluate whether trainee avoided unnecessary GDP while still protecting for uncertainty.

Branch 2: Full Impact (storms park over LGA)
Student must transition: reduced rate → GS → recovery GDP revision
Evaluate whether trainee manages stakeholder expectations and avoids repeated last-minute revisions.

8) Instructor Evaluation Items (What “Good” Looks Like)
Uses evolving METAR/TAF triggers to justify TMIs without overreacting.
Identifies whether arrival gate blockage or airport acceptance is the binding constraint at each stage.
Controls telcon: states purpose, limits debate, drives to decision, assigns follow-ups, sets next update time.
Manages airline pressure for lower rates while maintaining NAS efficiency.
Executes clean procedural flow: proposal → telcon → program issuance → revision → GS → recovery plan.

1) “At what point did your decision shift from ‘tactical’ to ‘strategic’ and why?”  
2) “What was your trigger for a GS vs. continuing a reduced-rate GDP?”  
3) “How did you address ‘stay wide open’ vs. ‘protect against diversions’ perspectives?”  
4) “If the 1530Z METAR had remained VCTS with no TSRA, what would you have revised and when?”

Background / Scenario Setup (Start Time: 1030 UTC)

Shift Objective: Manage evolving convective impacts across the ZFW–ZME–ZID–ZTL corridor with escalating constraints that begin as tactical deviations and culminate in structured reroutes and/or AFPs.

At 1030Z, the CONUS is in a pre-convective, high-moisture setup. A quasi-stationary boundary is draped from north Texas through Arkansas into the lower Ohio Valley, with an upper-level impulse forecast to increase lift late morning through afternoon. Early returns show light echoes, but model guidance supports rapid upscale growth into a broken line of thunderstorms.

DCC Meteorologist Brief (1035Z):  
“Convective initiation is expected near the Red River and northeast Texas late morning, then expansion northeast into AR/TN/KY/WV through the afternoon. Confidence is medium on coverage becoming widespread enough to require structured routing. The main operational issue won’t be single-cell deviations—it’ll be loss of corridor capacity on the west-to-east flows feeding ZME/ZID/ZTL.”

Northeastbound routings toward ZDC/ZOB/ZNY that normally cross ARK/MEM/BNA airspace

Trigger indicator(s): increasing route conformance failures, miles-in-trail requests, gate closures, rising AAR/ADR degradation at MEM/BNA/ATL, and widespread deviation bands rather than isolated storms.

Scenario Flow (Instructor-Controlled Timeline)

Phase 1: Tactical Deviation Environment (1030Z–1500Z)

Instructor Inject (1115Z): Increasing light-to-moderate precip returns near the Red River; no tops yet.  
Expected Student Actions:
Begin corridor monitoring for ZFW eastbound, ZME west gates, and ZID/ZTL boundary flows.
Establish early coordination posture: notify ZFW, ZME you’re monitoring for corridor constraints.

Stakeholder Dialogue (Prompt / Expected Answer Pairs)

ZFW TMU: “We’re getting scattered buildups north of the Metroplex. No constraints yet. You need anything?”  
Expected student response: “Not yet. I’m monitoring for corridor impacts into ZME; please advise if you see cells organizing into a line or if you need MIT for eastbound pushes.”

ZME TMU: “We’re seeing early deviation requests west of MEM. It’s manageable, but it’s trending.”  
Expected student response: “Copy. Are the deviations impacting both arrival and departure streams, and do you anticipate any gate closures or MIT in the next hour?”

Instructor Technique Cue (Controlling the Call):  
Student should summarize: “So at this time: manageable deviations, no closures, but trending. I’ll check back in 30 minutes or sooner if conformance drops. Anything else you need from Command Center right now?”

Phase 2: Corridor Compression (1500Z–1800Z)

Instructor Inject (1510Z): CCFP now paints a growing broken line from north TX into AR with developing cells near LIT moving NE.  
Meteorologist Update (1520Z):  
“Coverage confidence remains medium, but the organization is increasing. The corridor feeding MEM/BNA will likely narrow. Expect an operational impact window beginning 17Z, with the most significant restrictions 18–22Z.”

Decision Point #1 (Student Options):
No TMI (monitor only; rely on tactical deviations)  
Tactical reroutes (local route suggestions; short-lived)  
Structured reroutes (park and coordinate preferred routes)  
AFP consideration for key constrained flows if demand persists and geometry worsens

Instructor: “What’s your posture right now and what triggers move you from tactical to structured?”  
Expected student answer:  
Tactical now; move to structured when multiple facilities request MIT, gate closures occur, or deviation band forces systemic nonconformance affecting multiple streams.

Phase 3: Gate Loss and Escalation (1800Z–2200Z)

Instructor Inject (1705Z displayed at 1800Z): The line strengthens; tops increase; gaps begin closing over ARK/MEM corridor.  
ZME Call (1807Z):  
“Command Center, we’re starting to lose our west/east routing flexibility. Deviations are stacking. We may need MIT and we may have to stop taking certain internal feeds if this fills in.”

ZAU-style conflict not required here; focus is cross-region corridor.  
Now introduce a facility disagreement:
ZME wants early structured routing to prevent saturation.
ZTL wants to keep normal flows as long as possible to avoid overloading alternates.
Airlines want early reductions to limit diversions into constrained hubs.

Implementation Telcon (1830Z) — Student Leads

Student (Opening / Control Technique Expected):  
“Severe Weather Desk, [Name]. Purpose: corridor constraints from north TX through AR into TN/WV affecting ZME/ZID/ZTL flows. I’ll go facility-by-facility: current constraints, next 2 hours, then I’ll propose routing posture and ask for concerns.”

ZFW: “DFW/HOU eastbounds are already deviating north and south. We can keep them moving, but we need a predictable path into ZME.”  
Expected student response: “Copy. I’m proposing we stabilize eastbound routing to minimize last-minute deviations; I’ll coordinate a primary and alternate track.”

ZME: “We’re concerned about saturation over MEM. If AR fills in, we’ll lose the corridor and we’ll need MIT 20 at least, possibly stops on select internal feeds.”  
Expected student response: “Understood. Before we set MIT, confirm: which sectors/gates are your limiting factor and what acceptance rate can you realistically sustain through 20Z?”

ZID: “We’re getting increasing requests to deviate around southern Indiana and Kentucky. If you dump reroutes north, we’ll hit our boundary.”  
Expected student response: “Copy. We’ll avoid overfeeding your south boundary; we’ll balance with a southern option into ZTL where feasible.”

ZTL: “ATL flows are sensitive. If you reroute too much into us, we’ll need additional MIT for arrivals and departures.”  
Expected student response: “Acknowledged. We’ll cap reroute volume and coordinate any MIT changes before implementation.”

Airline Dispatch (UAL): “We’d rather see you slow the corridor now than force airborne holding later. If this becomes a line, we want early action.”  
Expected student response: “Copy. We are prioritizing diversion avoidance and stability; we’ll propose routes that reduce in-trail conflicts and minimize last-minute gate swaps.”

Airline Dispatch (DAL): “If MEM and BNA get hit, we need alternates protected. We’d like conservative routing early.”  
Expected student response: “Understood. We’ll keep alternates in mind and avoid pushing everyone into the same constrained fixes.”

Student (Proposed Plan — example posture):
Begin structured reroutes for TX-origin eastbound flows bound for BNA/SDF/CVG/CRW to avoid the AR/MEM convective band.
Implement mild MIT first (e.g., 20) only if ZME confirms saturation, with a trigger to increase.
Keep a southern option available to prevent overloading ZID.

Student (Closing / Control Technique Expected):  
“My proposed posture is structured reroutes starting 19Z with a 2-hour validity, with tactical flexibility maintained. I’m going to ask each facility: can you support this plan as stated, and what’s your single limiting factor?”

Phase 4: Worst-Case Materialization (2200Z–0100Z)

Instructor Inject (2210Z): The corridor becomes a near-continuous line with embedded severe cells. Multiple aircraft report inability to penetrate; reroutes become primary.  
Meteorologist Update (2220Z):  
“Confidence is now high on corridor closure behavior through at least 00Z. Expect slow eastward progression and backbuilding. The best gaps are inconsistent.”

Expected Student Actions:
Expand reroute scope and duration.
Evaluate AFP for key city pairs if demand exceeds stabilized corridor capacity.
Coordinate MIT adjustments if ZME/ZID/ZTL cannot safely accept volume.

ZME: “We’re at the point where we may need to stop select feeds for 30–60 minutes if that line sits.”  
Expected student response: “Understood. If you need a stop, tell me the scope and desired duration; I’ll coordinate with adjacent centers and users immediately, and we’ll build a recovery posture out of it.”

ZTL: “We can’t absorb unlimited reroutes. If everyone goes south, we’re forced into compression for ATL flows.”  
Expected student response: “Copy. I’ll meter reroute releases and ensure ZID gets a balanced share to avoid overload in your airspace. Provide your max feed rate for the reroute stream.”

Instructor Evaluation Focus (Level 3 Complexity Targets)

Look for the student to:
Detect transition from tactical to systemic constraints using objective TSD indicators (nonconformance, MIT requests, gate closures, demand/capacity mismatch).
Run a disciplined telcon: brief purpose, go facility-by-facility, summarize constraints, propose plan, solicit concerns, close with clear next steps.
Maintain volume balance between northern and southern options rather than “dumping” to one side.
Use meteorologist inputs appropriately as confidence changes from medium to high.

End-State / Completion Criteria

The scenario ends when the student:
Establishes structured routing (with alternates) for the corridor,  
Coordinates constraints and acceptance rates with ZFW/ZME/ZID/ZTL,  
Demonstrates conference control techniques, and  
Executes a stable recovery posture as the line exits and gaps re-emerge (instructor may simulate improvement after 0030Z).

Instructor (Final Question): “What were your triggers, what did you decide, and what did you communicate so the system understood the plan?”  
Expected student answer: concise trigger → action → coordination → outcome chain.

Prompt: Generate a Level 2-3 terminal training scenario for Detroit Metropolitan Airport (DTW) starting at 1100 UTC. Later, conditions should deteriorate beyond forecast, requiring a transition from a GDP to a Ground Stop due to frequent runway closures. Scenario:

Select/sequence TMIs (rate reduction → GDP → GS) as conditions deteriorate beyond forecast.  
Conduct GDP/GS telcons, manage stakeholder conflict, and defend decisions with data.  
Coordinate with ZOB/N90 adjacent impacts and communicate advisories/revisions cleanly.

6) GDP Telcon (1450Z) — Facility vs Airline Conflict  
Telcon Title: “DTW Winter GDP Coordination”  
Time: 1450Z  
Participants: ATCSCC (Student), DTW TRACON (D21), DTW Airport Ops, ZOB TMU, Delta (DAL) SOC, other users as available.

Opening / Conference Control (Instructor evaluates)  
Student (expected technique):  
“This is ATCSCC, DTW discussion for winter impacts. We’ll go facility impacts first, then users. Goal is to agree on start time, rate profile, and triggers for escalation.”

Facility Inputs  
DTW TRACON (D21):  
“We’re trending down faster than we’d like. We can work 40 right now, but with SN and OVC007 we’re increasing final spacing. When the heavier band hits, we’ll be closer to 30 or below, especially with plow cycles. Recommend GDP start 16Z at 36, stepping down 30 by 18–19Z.”

ZOB TMU:  
“Agree with D21. If you start later, we’ll hold in the enroute structure. We want the program out early so we don’t get trapped when the band goes +SN. Start 16Z. We can support 36/30/28.”

DTW Airport Ops:  
“Plow plan is continuous. If snowfall rates climb, we’ll have periodic runway closures for treatment. Expect at least one closure cycle in the 19–23Z window; could be more.”

Airline Inputs / Pushback  
DAL SOC:  
“We’d prefer to keep the operation as fluid as possible. Our forecast indicates the worst visibility won’t hit until closer to 20Z. We support a GDP but want a later start and higher initial rates—start 18Z at 36, then step down if needed. A ground stop is not warranted at this time.”

Student (expected response elements):  
Acknowledge DAL concern.  
Cite ceiling/vis trend and runway condition trajectory.  
Re-center to objective: avoid holding/diversions by acting before the worst window.  
Offer clear decision and revision plan.

Example Student Response (model answer):  
“Understood, DAL. Facilities are already increasing spacing and runway conditions are degrading with RVR down to 3500. To avoid holding and protect arrival integrity ahead of the 19Z deterioration, we’re proposing GDP start 16Z with stepped rates. We will build in a revision trigger: if conditions hold better than forecast through 18Z, we can revise upward; if braking action drops or closures increase, we’ll revise down and consider a stop.”

Decision (Instructor expected outcome)  
Outcome: Student issues GDP with earlier start and more restrictive rates than DAL requested, documenting user objection.

Instructor Prompt:  
“State the final program you’re going to publish: start time, rates, scope, and your first revision trigger.”

8) Decision Point #2 — GDP Revision vs Ground Stop (Trigger)

Expected trainee reasoning:  
If runway closures are frequent/unpredictable and braking is POOR with RVR ~2400 and +SN, a low rate GDP may not protect from airborne holding/diversions.

Phase 1 (1500Z–1700Z): Compression & Early Holding

Instructor guidance: Allow the student to watch demand build and identify that arrival rates remain nominal initially but compression begins as deviations start on arrival streams.

Operational inject at 1620Z (ZMA calls student):  
ZMA TMU: “We’re starting to see deviations building west of the field. We can keep arrivals going for now, but our arrival gates are getting messy—expect compression. If this fills in, we’re going to lose most of the west side gates first.”

Instructor prompt: “What’s your immediate posture—do nothing, issue a planning advisory, start pre-coordination for a GDP, or start with tactical initiatives (miles-in-trail / fix balancing / reroutes)? What data do you need right now?”

Expected actions (acceptable):
Pull TSD demand by quarter-hour, identify peak, identify arrivals already airborne vs on-ground.
Call ZMA/MIA Tower for triggers: lightning criteria, runway closure thresholds, and which gates will be impacted first.
Consider tactical MIT or minor reroutes to keep holding from exploding (no GDP yet if student chooses “wait and see,” but must be actively coordinating).

Phase 2 (1700Z–1900Z): Gate Closures → GDP Decision

Trigger inject at 1715Z
ZMA TMU: “We’re now losing flexibility. West and southwest arrival feeds are getting blocked by cells; departures are deviating hard and we’re starting to miss flow times. We can’t sustain demand. If this persists into 18Z, we need a program to keep from holding the sky.”

MIA ATCT/TRACON (Miami Approach) at 1720Z:  
“We can still accept, but the spacing is increasing and we’re getting lightning proximity calls. If the line moves over the final, we’ll be forced into stop-and-go with runway closures.”

Instructor: require a GDP proposal
By 1730Z, instructor expects the student to develop at least two options:
Option A (Early GDP): Start 18Z with a stepped-down rate to prevent holding.
Option B (Delayed GDP): Start 19Z, accept risk of holding/surface saturation.

Instructor prompt: “Model a GDP proposal for MIA. State your proposed start time, rate profile, and what weather trigger you’re protecting against. Then prepare to defend it on a telcon.”

GDP Telcon #1 (Target time 1745Z) — Conflict: ZMA/ZOB vs MIA/Airlines

Telcon objective: Decide GDP start time/rates amid uncertainty.  
Student role: Chair the telcon; control agenda; summarize decisions; assign action items.

Opening / Conference Control (required technique)
Student (Chair):  
“Command Center, [Name], running the MIA GDP discussion. We’ll take facilities first, then carriers, then I’ll restate the proposal and ask for final concerns. Please keep comments operational and time-bounded.”

Facility positions
ZMA TMU:  
“Convection is expanding and we’re losing multiple arrival gates. We’re already compressing. We need an earlier start to avoid airborne holding. Recommend GDP starting 1800Z with an initial reduced rate and ability to revise.”

ZOB TMU (downstream posture/flow concern):  
“We’re watching reroutes and potential diversions northbound. If MIA starts holding, that stacks into our eastbound flows and creates cascading MIT. We support early TMIs to keep airborne holding from ballooning.”

MIA ATCT/TRACON:  
“We’re still landing, but the runway environment is becoming unstable with lightning in the vicinity and heavy rain bands approaching. If we keep running high and we get forced to stop, the surface will gridlock and you’ll get diversions.”

Airline positions (pushback to maintain rates)
AAL Dispatch:  
“We want to avoid unnecessary delays. Our internal forecast shows the strongest cores staying west until closer to 19Z, with runway recovery windows. We prefer no GDP until 1900Z or a minimal program that can be canceled quickly.”

UAL Dispatch:  
“Agree. If you start at 18Z and the weather delays, we eat delay we can’t recover. We’d rather take tactical holding than lock in GDP delays prematurely.”

Weather input
DCC Meteorologist (briefing on call):  
“Confidence is medium that the line consolidates eastward into the metro between 18–20Z, with embedded cells. Expect frequent lightning and heavy rain producing rapid AAR swings. The probability of a sustained recovery window before 20Z is low.”

Chair control / decision articulation (student script expectation)
Student (Chair):  
“Here’s what I’m hearing: ZMA and ZOB are seeing gate degradation now and want an 18Z start to prevent holding; MIA concurs instability is increasing. AAL/UAL prefer 19Z start based on recovery expectations. Given medium confidence of consolidation by 18–20Z and current compression, we will proceed with an 1800Z GDP with a stepped rate and planned review at 1830Z for revision/cancel triggers. Any final operational concerns—specifically, runway closure thresholds or gate loss timing?”

Instructor note: If the student fails to summarize and force closure, have stakeholders talk over each other until the student reasserts control.

GDP Implementation (Issued ~1755Z)

Instructor directive: Student publishes a GDP advisory with:
Start: 1800Z  
Rationale: Arrival gate closures + increasing holding risk + lightning/runway closure volatility  
Planned update: 1830Z (or sooner on METAR deterioration)

Traffic/Delay Injects (for realism)
1805Z: ZMA reports airborne holding beginning on multiple arrival streams; MIT increases.
1830Z: MIA reports intermittent ramp lightning holds begin for some carriers (in/out of ramp), reducing departure throughput.

Operational deterioration inject
At 1915Z (use METAR VRB25G40KT 1/2SM +TSRA FG BKN004CB OVC012), instructor injects:
MIA reports runway closures “stop-and-go” due to lightning within 5 NM and heavy rain reducing braking action reports.

Airlines (AAL/UAL) immediate response (push to delay GS):

UAL Dispatch: “Agree. Prefer a lower GDP rate over a full stop, unless the field is actually closed.”

Instructor prompt: “Given the METAR and ZMA’s gate status, what’s your decision threshold for GS vs rate reduction? How do you limit diversions?”

GS Telcon (Target time 1930Z) — Conflict: ZMA/ZOB urge earlier GS, Airlines push delay

Conference control requirement
Student (Chair):  
“Let’s run this quickly. Facilities: confirm whether MIA is effectively unable to accept arrivals and for how long. Then carriers: diversion concerns and recovery expectations. I’ll restate the action and scope.”

Facility positions
ZMA TMU:  
“We have widespread gate blockage and cannot deliver. We recommend a Ground Stop now to prevent uncontrolled diversions and excess holding.”

ZOB TMU:  
“If you don’t stop arrivals, diversions and holding will spill into our flows and impact multiple hubs. A stop now is cleaner than uncontrolled airborne holding.”

MIA ATCT/TRACON:  
“We’re in lightning restrictions and heavy rain. We can’t sustain continuous arrivals. Expect intermittent closures over the next hour.”

Airline positions (request alternatives)
AAL Dispatch:  
“We request you try a very low rate first, or a short GS with rapid review. Our concern is mass diversions and misconnects if the stop runs long.”

UAL Dispatch:  
“If you GS, please keep it as tight as possible and use a clear end time with an early review.”

Chair decision language (expected)
Student (Chair):  
“Based on ZMA’s inability to deliver and MIA’s intermittent closures under lightning/heavy rain, we are issuing a Ground Stop for MIA effective immediately. We will set an initial end time of 2030Z with a mandatory review at 2000Z. Scope will be tiered to capture the majority of volume contributing to holding/diversions. We will coordinate a GDP revision out of the stop once acceptance stabilizes.”

Ground Stop Implementation (Issued ~1935Z)

Instructor injects after issuance:
1945Z: Diversion reports begin increasing for airborne arrivals already committed; ZMA requests continued stop.  
2000Z review: METAR improves temporarily (KMIA 152010Z ... 2SM TSRA ...), but Convective SIGMET still active and embedded cells remain.

MIA ATCT/TRACON (2002Z):  
“We’re taking a few arrivals between cells but can’t promise continuity. Lightning is still frequent.”

ZMA TMU (2005Z):  
“We recommend extend GS; if you cancel and it shuts again, we’ll be right back in chaos.”

AAL/UAL (2006Z):  
“We see a break and want to start moving. Can we come out of the stop into a low GDP rate to restart without flooding the system?”

GDP/GS Transition Decision Point (Out-of-Stop Plan)

Instructor prompt:  
“Build a controlled recovery plan: What rate do you propose out of the GS? What triggers do you use to raise rates? How do you message uncertainty to stakeholders while still controlling the plan?”

Expected trainee approach:
Cancel/step down GS only when ZMA confirms gates partially restored and MIA can accept sustained arrivals.
Implement/continue GDP with a conservative rate initially; plan explicit revision windows every 30 minutes.
Communicate clearly: “We will reassess at XX:XXZ; if METAR improves above X and gate availability returns, we’ll increase rate.”

End-State (Instructor-Driven)
By 2105Z, temporary improvement occurs (KMIA 152105Z 09010KT 6SM -RA VCTS...), then additional storms re-fire, driving continued instability through 22Z.

Instructor ends scenario once the student has:
Recognized compression and holding onset early,  
Chaired and defended an earlier GDP amid airline pushback,  
Escalated appropriately to a GS when gates/runways became unreliable,  
Demonstrated conference control (agenda, sequencing, summary, closure), and  
Built a credible out-of-stop recovery posture (low rate, review triggers, stakeholder messaging).

Instructor Notes / Evaluation Points (Use During Run)
Does the student request: gate status, lightning criteria, runway closure triggers, airborne holding counts, and demand split (airborne vs ground)?
Does the student articulate why GDP is preferable to uncontrolled holding (diversions, sector overload, safety margins)?
Does the student manage conflict professionally: acknowledge airline cost, but anchor decisions in facility constraints and observed METAR/SIGMET trend?
Does the student control the telcon: facilities first, airlines second, concise weather brief, decision restatement, final concerns, action items?
Does the student publish clear review times and revision triggers rather than open-ended TMIs?

Scenario:

ZLA: “We’re starting to see compression on the arrival streams. If we keep launching, we’ll be holding them in ZLA airspace and then diverting. Recommend GS now; start 1st-tier, be ready to expand.”  

UAL (System Ops): “We want the narrowest scope possible. We have multiple inbound banks and we’re trying to recover from the last two nights. Suggest a short GS—30 minutes—then reassess.”  

SWA: “Agree—keep it short. We can absorb some holding if needed; we’d rather try to get a few in.”  

NTMO (Instructor): “Chair, confirm your trigger for extension/cancellation and what you need from SCT to reopen.”  

4.3 Expected Student Decision & Readback  
Student (Chair expected):  
“Decision: Issue KSAN GS effective 0120Z for arrivals, 1st-tier scope initially. Rationale: field has dropped to 1/2SM FG VV002, SCT cannot sustain arrival acceptance without airborne holding.”  
“Extension/cancel triggers: cancel when KSAN sustains ≥ 2SM and OVC004 or better for two consecutive observations and SCT confirms they can accept releases.”  
“SCT, confirm you want the GS start time at 0120Z and advise expected reopen window.”  

SCT: “Affirm. We’ll reassess at 0200Z, but this is rolling fog—could bounce.”

5.2 Telcon Script — Day 1 GS Cancellation Debate (0240Z)

ZLA: “We do not support immediate cancel. The fog is rolling; we’ve seen it drop back to VV001 within 30–45 minutes. If you cancel too early, you’ll rebuild holding and diversions.”  

SCT: “We can take a limited release, but we do not want to get flooded. Recommend cancel the GS only if we can cap releases and be ready to reinstate rapidly.”  

Student (Chair expected):  
“We will cancel the GS effective 0250Z with a controlled restart: notify Centers to stagger releases; monitor first 30 minutes closely.”  
“If KSAN returns to ≤ 1SM FG or VV002 again, we’ll reissue GS without delay.”  

Instructor Note: Ensure trainee uses diversion recovery tools immediately on cancellation: coordinate with SCT/ZLA for release pacing, prioritize previously diverted aircraft repositioning, and solicit SAN gate status.

9) Telcon Script — Conservative Facilities vs Airline Recovery (Day 2 Example at 0700Z)  
SCT: “We recommend keeping the GS in place even though it briefly improved—this is rolling fog and we also have gate acceptance issues.”

Prompt: Generate a Level 3 terminal training scenario for Dallas/Fort Worth International Airport (DFW) starting at 1500 UTC involving rapidly deteriorating thunderstorms that trigger a multi-phase traffic management response and result in ~120 diversions over two days. The scenario should require the student to manage thunderstorms with either GS or GDP and thereafter face an unexpected and rapid deterioration in weather conditions causing a spike in diversions. The unpredictability in weather forces the student to update TMI decisions as conditions oscillate between improvement and deterioration. Scenario:
Level 3 Terminal Training Scenario — DFW Rapid TS Deterioration / Diversions Surge
Facility/Position: ATCSCC TMU (Terminal East/Severe Weather Desk—DFW focus)  
Simulation Start: 1500 UTC (Day 1)  
Training Level: Level 3 (high tempo, uncertainty, multiple revisions, stakeholder conflict)  
Primary Training Threads:  
Build/justify an early GS vs GDP strategy with incomplete/uncertain convective forecasts  
Rapid shift from “manageable TS impacts” to “airport intermittently unusable”  
Multiple TMI revisions as conditions oscillate (improve/deteriorate)  
Escalating diversion risk culminating in ~120 diversions across two days (scenario accounting)  
Conference control: keep telcons structured, decision-focused, and documented  

Background / Scenario Setup (1500Z Day 1)
You take the position at 1500Z with DFW in a high-demand push and convective potential building west/northwest of the Metroplex. ZFW is already reporting increasing deviations on westbound departures and inbound streams from the west.

Explicit triggers tied to: lightning proximity, field closure likelihood, west gate blockage, ZFW reroutes saturating arrival fixes, surface saturation/departure gridlock

Student Decision Space (TMI Options Provided)
At multiple points, the trainee must choose a path and revise as reality diverges from forecast:

Option Set A — Early GDP (demand management bias):  
GDP start 1730Z (or earlier if trainee chooses) with stepped rates  
Maintain arrival flow control pre-impact to reduce holding/diversions  
Risk: unnecessary delay if storms miss/fragment  

Option Set B — Tactical GS into GDP (reactive bias):  
First implement 1st-tier GS when field/gates collapse  
Convert to GDP when reopening window becomes clearer  
Risk: diversions and holding increase if GS is late or reopening is uncertain  

Option Set C — Hybrid:  
Short GS for the worst cell passage + GDP for long tail recovery  
Requires tight revisions and clear comms  

Stakeholder Dialogue Block 1 — Pre-TMI Coordination (1545Z)
Instructor (as ATCSCC TMU Supervisor): “Call ZFW TMU and D10 and see what they’re seeing. Get timing, confidence, and what they can actually deliver.”

Call: ZFW TMU (1545Z)
ZFW TMU: “Cells are firing faster than the last run. West arrival gates are starting to pinch from deviations. If this line organizes, we’ll lose the ability to feed D10 cleanly. I’d rather see you get a program in early than have us holding 30–40 minutes in-trail with nowhere to go.”

Trainee (expected):  
Confirm which gates/fixes most at risk, expected closure window, and whether reroutes are already saturating fixes  
Ask: “If we do nothing until 18Z, what does holding/diversion risk look like?”  
State intent: “I’m building a candidate GDP/GS plan; I’ll bring you into a telcon with D10 and users.”

Call: D10 TRACON (1550Z)
D10: “Right now we’re workable, but if that northwest build moves toward the final, we’ll hit a point where we’re in-and-out of stops. I’d rather not take a big arrival push right as the weather hits the boundary.”

Instructor prompt: “You need them on record: what’s their capacity estimate if they’re running but deviating? And what’s their posture if the core is over the field?”

Assessment Question 2 (Decision Point at 1605Z)
Instructor: “You have increasing VCTS and an updated package showing TEMPO TSRA beginning 18Z-ish, but METARs are already trending worse. What are you issuing first: a GDP proposal, a GS, or waiting? Defend it to facilities and airlines.”

Telcon 1 — GDP/GS Strategy Disagreement (1615Z)
Participants: ATCSCC (Trainee Chair), ZFW TMU, D10 TRACON, DFW Tower (as needed), ATCSCC Weather Desk, Airlines (AAL, UAL, SWA, DAL), ATCSCC Supervisor (Instructor)

Conference Control Script (expected trainee technique)
Trainee (Chair):  
“Everyone, this is ATCSCC for DFW planning. Purpose is to decide whether we need a program before 1730Z based on convective impacts. I’m going to take facility impacts first, then weather, then users, and I’ll close with a decision and next update time. Please keep comments to operational impacts and rate requests.”

Facility positions
ZFW TMU: “We recommend an early initiative. If the west gates pinch, we can’t deliver—then you’ll get holding and diversions. We’d support starting a program prior to the 17Z hour.”

D10 TRACON: “Agree. Stay ahead of it. If it hits like it’s building, we’ll be in a posture where we may have to go stop-and-go. Early management reduces airborne holding.”

Weather conflict / uncertainty injection
ATCSCC Weather Desk: “Confidence is medium on timing. The environment supports rapid intensification. You could see the ‘TEMPO’ conditions begin earlier than 18Z and persist in waves.”

Instructor (as Supervisor, to trainee privately or as on-line): “Make them quantify: earliest/latest onset and worst window. Don’t accept ‘medium’ without brackets.”

Trainee (expected):  
“Understood. Give me an earliest onset for TSRA at the field and an earliest for west gate blockage. Also, are we looking at a single line or multiple rounds?”

Airline pushback (rate severity / diversion risk framing)
AAL: “We don’t support a deep cut based on uncertainty. If you start too early, we’ll burn unnecessary delay and misconnects.”

UAL: “We’d prefer you wait until there’s a confirmed impact. If you’re going to run something, keep it mild. Our internal forecast shows storms may stay northwest and not sit on the field.”

SWA: “Diversions are our biggest risk. If you’re forecasting a shutdown, we’d rather see a lower rate earlier than trap airplanes airborne with nowhere to go.”

Decision pressure (what the trainee must do)
ZFW TMU (firm): “If you wait and we lose gates, you’ll be reacting with a GS after the fact. We’ve seen this movie.”

D10 TRACON: “We can stay open until we can’t. But when it goes, it goes fast.”

Trainee (expected decision statement):  
Summarize: onset window, gate risk, surface/holding risk  
Declare action: GDP proposal or GS with scope + review time  
Set next telcon time and triggers for revision  

Example acceptable closure (trainee):  
“Based on facility risk to delivery and medium confidence for earlier onset, we will issue a GDP proposal with a start in the 17Z hour and include a contingency for a short-notice GS if the field goes ‘unable.’ Next update in 20 minutes or sooner if D10 reports lightning/arrival stop.”

Instructor Inject — Rapid Deterioration (1705Z–1745Z)
At 1705Z, METAR drops to TSRA 3SM and winds gust higher; by 1745Z, METAR hits 1SM +TSRA BKN008CB.

Instructor (to trainee): “Conditions deteriorate faster than the telcon consensus. Now defend either:  
Why your early initiative prevented holding/diversions, or  
Why you must escalate immediately (GS / deeper GDP revision).”

Telcon 2 — Escalation to Stop/Posture (1735Z)
ZFW TMU: “We’re now seeing gate closures. We can’t feed you reliably. Recommend immediate GS to stop the bleed.”

D10 TRACON: “We’re intermittently unable. We can’t guarantee final acceptance in the next 30–60 minutes.”

UAL (pushback): “If you stop everything now, you’ll create a massive backlog and tomorrow becomes impossible. Can you scope it tighter or delay the start?”

SWA: “We need to avoid trapping aircraft airborne. If you’re going to stop, stop early and clearly.”

Trainee (expected conference control + decision):  
“I’m implementing a 1st-tier GS effective immediately due to field conditions and delivery uncertainty. This is to prevent airborne holding and diversions. We will reassess in 30 minutes with updated METAR/field status and transition into a GDP for recovery.”

Diversion Spike Event (Operational Consequence)
Day 1 (1800Z–0600Z): oscillation and cascading diversions
Instructor Narrative:  
Airport reopens briefly then shuts again as cells redevelop along approach corridors  
Diversions begin as arrival holding reaches unacceptable limits and alternates saturate  
Airlines request lower rates to reduce diversions; facilities demand demand management to prevent airborne gridlock  

Key trainee actions expected:  
Extend/shorten GS appropriately and communicate clearly  
Convert GS → GDP with realistic recovery rates and revision cadence  
Coordinate ZFW/D10 for “able” windows and surface constraints  
Document objections and rationale; set next update times consistently  

Day 2 Continuation (Second Round / Compounding Diversions)
Instructor Setup (1200Z Day 2):  
Residual recovery is incomplete; schedule still heavy. New convection forecast returns (TAF shows TEMPO TSRA again 18–23Z). Airlines are now highly sensitive to diversions; they want preemptive low rates.

Instructor: “Your decisions today are influenced by the operational trauma from last night. Show how you adjust strategy without over-controlling the NAS unnecessarily.”

Telcon 3 — Airlines now request lower rates (e.g., 56) to limit diversions
AAL: “We can’t take another diversion wave. We’d rather take delay on the ground. Request planning for a 56 arrival posture during the worst impacts.”

UAL: “Agree on lowering the rate early today. Yesterday proved the forecast can undercall intensity.”

ZFW TMU: “We support early management again. If the line fires, we’ll lose gate integrity.”

D10 TRACON: “We want to stay as open as possible but we cannot accept a surge into a potential ‘unable’ window.”

Trainee (expected):  
Present a structured plan: “If we see X by time Y, we move to rate 56; if lightning within Z miles or final blocked, we GS; when able, we come out on stepped rates.”  
Keep the telcon controlled; lock next update time; ensure facilities confirm feasibility  

Managing the “~120 Diversions Over Two Days” Outcome (Instructor Accounting)
Instructor guidance: As the trainee makes late/insufficient TMIs during the “unable” windows, inject diversion growth; if they stay ahead (earlier GS/GDP + conservative recovery), inject fewer diversions.

Scenario target (by end Day 2): cumulative diversions approximately 120 systemwide attributable to DFW constraints and recovery whiplash.

Instructor Tools / Prompts (Evaluation Focus)
Decision-quality prompts (ask at each revision)
“What new information changed your decision?”  
“What’s your next update time and what data do you require by then?”  
“How are you preventing airborne holding from turning into diversions?”  
“What is your recovery strategy and how will you avoid over-releasing?”  

Conference-control behaviors to observe
Establish purpose + sequence (facilities → weather → users → decision)  
Summarize frequently using operational terms: “deliverable rate,” “unable,” “gate blocked,” “holding risk,” “diversion risk”  
Close with: decision, effective time, scope, next update, and who must coordinate  

End State (Simulation End)
End of sim: Day 2 late evening after multiple revisions, with cumulative diversions approaching ~120 across two days.  
Instructor wrap-up questions:  
“Where did you intentionally accept delay to reduce diversion risk?”  
“Which trigger points worked, which failed, and what would you tighten?”  
“How did you manage stakeholder conflict while maintaining operational control?”

Scenario:
text

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “Based on the weather and peak demand, which areas are most likely to hit capacity first, and what do you expect the first TMI trigger to be—airport rates, enroute constraints, or both?”

Expected trainee focus
North/Central Florida (KMCO/KTPA/KJAX) will constrain first from MVFR/IFR + gusts + rain bands, reducing acceptance and increasing airborne holding risk.
South Florida will begin VFR but becomes constrained later due to volume + increasing VCSH/SHRA; primary near-term risk is ZMA sector saturation and over-water flow management.
Trigger likely: Airport acceptance constraints at MCO/TPA/JAX, followed quickly by enroute metering/AFP as demand reroutes and stacks build.

Shift-Start Task Checklist (Student Must Verbalize)
Review active advisories and expected bank demand (Florida + Caribbean + Northeast connections).
Validate airport AAR/ADR trends (MCO/TPA/JAX first, then MIA/FLL).
Identify choke points: north FL boundary, Gulf transition, over-water fixes, ZMA internal sectors, and ZJX/ZHU handoffs.
Establish an initial TMI ladder: GDP(s) first, then AFP for Florida/Caribbean flows, then holiday Gulf routes if reroute monitor shows systemic congestion.
Prepare telcon control plan: agenda, time box, decision points, and revision triggers.

Event Timeline & Decision Points

1) 1130Z — Early Demand Spike + North/Central Florida Weather Deterioration
Instructor inject (traffic):
Major banks scheduled into MCO/TPA/JAX beginning 1230Z with heavy Northeast + Midwest feed.
Caribbean departures are filing early to protect arrival windows into Florida.

KMCO ceiling trending down faster than forecast; rain intensity increasing.
KJAX nearing IFR with gusts producing compression.

Instructor: “What initiative do you want first—GDP at MCO/TPA/JAX, or an AFP to shape the whole region?”

Option set (student chooses)
Sequential GDPs (MCO first, then TPA/JAX) with conservative rates to prevent holding/diversions.
AFP for Florida region immediately to shape demand broadly.
Wait-and-see with tactical metering only (high risk given peak volume).

Expected best practice path: Begin with GDP(s) for the first constrained airports, while preparing for an AFP once congestion spills into enroute.

2) 1200Z — First GDP Telcon (MCO Lead) + Airline Resistance
Trigger: MCO begins missing rate; departure metering increases; ZJX indicates boundary issues.

Telcon Participants: ZMA TMU, ZJX TMU, ZHU TMU, ZFW TMU, Airline System Ops (DAL/AAL/UAL/JBU/SWA), ATCSCC Supervisor (Instructor role)

Telcon Script (Prompt/Expected Answer Pairs)

ATCSCC (Student opens):  
“Winter peak demand is exceeding acceptance in north/central Florida. Objective: prevent airborne holding and diversions by shaping demand early. We’ll go airport-by-airport, then discuss regional shaping and routes.”

ZJX:  
“We’re already compressing into MCO/TPA/JAX. With the gusts and low ceilings, we can’t keep feeding the current demand. If you don’t take action now, you’ll see holding stacks and diversions within the hour.”

ZMA:  
“South Florida is still moving, but we’re absorbing reroutes and early Caribbean pushes. If MCO/TPA lock up, it will cascade into ZMA sectors and we’ll lose flexibility for MIA/FLL later.”

ZHU:  
“We’re seeing flow restrictions building at the Gulf transition points. As flights start deviating around weather and turbulence, we’ll lose altitude options and start hitting sector counts.”

ZFW:  
“We can support restrictive TMIs early. Better to meter on the ground than build a system-wide airborne problem.”

DAL System Ops (airline push):  
“Rates you’re discussing will strand our connections. We’d prefer to keep rates higher and accept some minor holding. We’re not seeing it as bad as the forecast yet.”

AAL System Ops:  
“We support minimal constraints first—tactical MIT, then reassess. A GDP now creates guaranteed delay and misconnects.”

SWA System Ops:  
“We’re concerned about diversions more than delays. If you’re going to cap it, do it early and predictable, but don’t whipsaw rates.”

ATCSCC (Expected student controlling technique):  
“Copy all. Here’s the decision point: we’re choosing between predictable ground delay now versus uncontrolled airborne holding and diversions later. We will set an initial program with revision triggers tied to observed ceiling/vis and missed AAR, then we’ll reassess on the top of the hour.”

Expected student action: Issue MCO GDP first, with stated trigger for expansion to TPA/JAX if miss rate continues.

3) 1230Z — METAR Deterioration + GDP Expansion Pressure
METAR trend inject
KMCO 011230Z 02018G28KT 2SM RA BKN012 OVC025 18/17 A2996  
KTPA 011230Z 01018G26KT 3SM RA BKN012 OVC025 17/16 A2994  
KJAX 011230Z 02022G32KT 1 1/2SM RA BKN006 OVC015 16/15 A2991

Expected: Expand to TPA and JAX GDPs (or a combined regional shaping plan) and explicitly tie it to measurable field conditions (IFR ceilings/vis + gusts) and missed AAR.

4) 1330Z — Congestion Spillover + AFP Trigger (Florida/Caribbean)
ZMA reports increased reroute absorption; Caribbean departures begin queueing for limited windows. Airline dispatchers begin requesting “lower rates to prevent diversions,” but only after first diversions occur.

Inject: first diversion calls
Two MCO-bound aircraft divert to ATL/CLT due to holding fuel.
TPA arrivals begin holding; gate holds reported.

Instructor: “Do you keep stacking GDP revisions airport-by-airport, or do you implement an AFP to manage the whole demand picture?”

Expected: Implement AFP covering Florida region (including Caribbean flows into Florida), with clearly stated scope and end time triggers.

5) 1400Z — Holiday Gulf Routes Discussion (Structured Reroutes)
Problem: Gulf transition flows saturating; turbulence and weather deviations reduce usable airspace. The student must decide whether to activate holiday Gulf routes to stabilize flows and reduce complexity.

ZHU (advocates restrictive TMIs):  
“We need structure. Deviations are starting to smear across multiple sectors. If we don’t lock in holiday Gulf routes, we’ll be doing tactical firefighting for hours.”

ZFW:  
“Agree. We can support routing changes now before the west/central banks launch. Give us a clean time window to brief and implement.”

Airline pushback (UAL/AAL/DAL):  
“We can file around it ourselves. A route program will add miles and delay across the board. Keep it tactical.”

Expected student controlling technique:  
“We’re past the point where individualized deviations are efficient. We’re implementing structured routes to protect throughput and reduce secondary holding. We will publish the route advisory with a firm effective time, and we will reassess when sector counts and reroute monitor stabilize.”

Expected outcome: Activate holiday Gulf routes for Florida/Caribbean-bound flows to reduce sector complexity and protect critical corridors.

Stakeholder Dialogue Inserts (Instructor Use)

A) ZMA / Caribbean Pressure (1500Z)
ZMA:  
“Over-water arrivals are bunching. If you don’t keep the AFP in place, we’ll end up with long in-trails to the fixes and we’ll lose the ability to recover for MIA/FLL when the showers increase.”

Student expected response:  
Acknowledge, ask for concrete constraints: “What’s your sustainable feed rate by fix and what sectors are limiting?” Then restate the plan: “AFP remains; revision at HH:30; holiday Gulf routes remain until reroute monitor shows stability.”

B) Airline Diversion Mitigation Request (1515Z)
AAL:  
“We want a lower arrival posture into MCO/TPA—something like a significantly reduced rate—to keep us from launching flights that will divert.”

Student expected response:  
Ask for their diversion thresholds and propose a rate revision aligned to observed AAR misses and fuel-diversion risk; ensure they understand trade: fewer diversions but more ground delay. Document requests, announce decision time.

C) ZJX Boundary Concern vs Airline Rate Pressure (1545Z)
ZJX:  
“If you try to keep rates up, we’ll just end up holding at the boundary. We need the demand shaped earlier.”

Airlines:  
“Keep rates; we’ll manage.”

Student controlling technique (expected):  
“Decision is grounded in boundary delivery capability and observed missed AAR, not preference. We will run the safer posture for the next hour, then revise upward if performance supports it.”

Required Student Actions (Graded Items)
Identify first constrained nodes and articulate why (field conditions + demand).
Implement sequential GDPs (MCO → TPA/JAX) with clear revision triggers.
Transition to AFP when congestion becomes regional/systemic.
Implement holiday Gulf routes when tactical deviations become inefficient.
Run telcons with control: agenda, time-boxing, explicit decision points, documented objections, and revision schedule.
Manage diversion pressure: lower posture when needed, communicate tradeoffs, and prevent airborne holding cascades.

Instructor Notes / Scoring Guide (Quick)
Strong performance indicators
Uses measurable triggers (missed AAR, ceilings/vis, sector counts, holding reports).
Issues early predictable TMIs rather than reacting late with rolling ground stops.
Balances equity: avoids over-constraining unconstrained airports while protecting the system.
Controls telcons: summarizes, decides, assigns follow-ups, sets next review time.

Common failure modes
Waiting too long and then needing emergency ground stops.
Airport-by-airport micromanagement without recognizing regional spillover.
Allowing airlines to drive decisions without tying to capacity evidence.
Inconsistent messaging/revisions causing customer whipsaw.
