Prompt: Generate a terminal training scenario for Detroit (DTW) involving worsening winter weather and runway contamination. The student must manage the transition from a GDP to a Ground Stop as conditions deteriorate beyond the forecast, balancing restrictive TMI requests from the facility against airline pushback.
Scenario:
DTW Terminal Training Scenario — Worsening Winter Weather / Runway Contamination (GDP → Ground Stop)

Background / Scenario Setup (Start 1030Z)

Position/Role: Student is the ATCSCC Terminal East Specialist for DTW and associated metro airports.  
Shift Start: 1030 UTC (winter morning push).  
Tools Available: FSM, TSD, OIS/NTML, advisory database, CEDAR, arrival counts, airline contacts, facility hotlines.

Operational Context:  
A fast-moving winter system is lifting north through southeast Michigan. Early forecasts indicate manageable IFR with intermittent snow, but the system is trending colder with higher snow ratios. DTW is currently operating in a normal winter arrival configuration, but braking action and runway contamination will become the key limiter. DTW Airport Ops has limited plow capacity due to one unit OTS, increasing the risk of rapid degradation during heavier bands.

Current field conditions at 1030Z (METAR):  
KDTW 171030Z 04010KT 1 1/2SM -SN BR OVC008 M06/M08 A2988 RMK AO2 P0001  
Airport reports: light accumulation beginning on movement areas; first friction check pending.

Forecast emphasis for the scenario: ceilings/visibilities deteriorate more than expected; snow intensity increases; runway contamination builds; braking action drops; facility requests aggressive TMIs while airlines resist due to misconnections and deicing constraints.

TAF sequence provided to student at start:  
KDTW 171120Z 1712/1812 05012KT 2SM -SN OVC010  
  TEMPO 1712/1716 3/4SM SN OVC006  
  FM171600 06014G20KT 1SM SN OVC006  
  FM171900 07016G24KT 1/2SM +SN OVC004  
  FM180200 06012KT 1SM -SN OVC008  
  FM180600 32010KT 3SM -SN OVC015  
  FM181000 30008KT P6SM SCT020

Initial DTW rates (for planning at 1030Z):  
Current acceptance (vfr-ish winter): ARR 60 / DEP 54 (surface moving).  
Anticipated IFR/deice constraint mid-morning: ARR 44–48.  
If heavy snow / low braking: ARR 30–36, potential temporary closure for plowing.

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt at 1035Z)

Instructor: “Based on the DTW forecast and current METAR, what do you expect the capacity risk is today, and what is your first initiative—if any?”

Expected trainee answer (key points):
DTW is primary risk due to deteriorating ceiling/vis and snow with likely runway contamination impacts.
Consider early GDP to protect against holding/diversions as acceptance likely drops after 16Z and sharply after 19Z.
Identify triggers: braking action < GOOD, RVR reductions, +SN onset, plow cycle / runway closures, departure deice gridlock.

Phase 1 — Early Coordination / GDP Decision (1045Z–1200Z)

Instructor Cue (1045Z)
“Model options: (A) no TMI yet, monitor; (B) propose a DTW GDP starting 16Z; (C) preemptive GDP starting 15Z with step-down rates.”

Stakeholder Dialogue — Pre-GDP Coordination Call (1100Z)

Agent: ZOB TMU (Center Flow)
ZOB: “We’re already seeing reroutes and altitude caps around Lake Michigan. DTW arrivals are starting to string out. If you wait until the heavy band arrives, we’ll be holding in ZOB and ZAU.”

Agent: DTW ATCT (Tower Supervisor)
DTW Tower: “We’re still landing fine, but deice is ramping up. Airport Ops is calling for an increased plow cadence by late morning. If we go heavy snow, we’ll need periodic runway closures.”

Agent: D21 (DTW TRACON)
D21: “Right now we can run 48 in the current conditions. If the 16Z timeframe goes to 1SM snow with OVC006 and deice saturation, we’ll be closer to 40. If the +SN hits and braking drops, we’ll need 30-ish and may have to pulse arrivals.”

Agent: Airline Dispatch (DAL, AAL, UAL)
DAL: “We can manage delays with a GDP, but we need stable planning. Too aggressive early will strand crews and worsen misconnections.”
UAL: “We’d prefer you wait—our meteorology says the worst band holds south until 19Z.”
AAL: “If you run a GDP, keep it flexible. We’re deice-limited at outstations and can’t absorb large EDCT swings.”

Expected trainee action:
Build a GDP proposal with step-down rates aligned to the forecast (start 16Z or 15Z), aiming to reduce holding/diversions while maintaining some flexibility.

GDP Proposal (Recommended Starting Point for Student)
Proposed DTW GDP (draft for telcon):
Start: 1600Z  
Scope: standard DTW arrivals (1st/2nd tier as needed per demand)  
Rates:  
16Z–18Z: 40  
18Z–19Z: 36  
19Z–02Z: 30 (aligned with +SN window)  
02Z–06Z: 36  
06Z–10Z: 44  
10Z–12Z: 54–60 (recovery, if conditions improve)

Instructor note: If trainee selects “wait and see,” allow it—but ensure later workload spikes and a forced GS transition.

GDP Telcon (Facility vs Airline Pushback) — 1215Z

Student (running the call) — required conference control technique:
“I’ll go facility first for constraints, then users, then we’ll decide on start time/rates. Please keep comments to impacts and requested changes.”

Facility Statements
DTW Tower:
“Deice is in effect now. Ops is reporting increasing accumulation. We can sustain arrivals, but runway condition is the limiting factor once snowfall rate increases.”

D21 TRACON:
“Support GDP start 16Z. Rates as proposed are reasonable. If we get +SN or runway closures, we’ll need to drop further or stop.”

ZOB TMU:
“Agree with early program. Protect the enroute system—if you wait, we’ll have holding and diversions. We also have CLE/DTW interactions on some flows.”

Airline Pushback
UAL:
“We object to a 16Z start. Our forecast keeps heavy snow south until 19Z. Recommend start 18Z and keep 40 until 19Z, then reassess.”

DAL:
“We can accept 16Z start, but the 30 rate for seven hours is too punitive. Consider 36 and use tactical stops if needed.”

AAL:
“We’ll support the program if you commit to frequent reviews and allow compressions/revisions quickly.”

Expected trainee resolution
Confirm the rationale (protect against holding/diversions; deice; runway closures) and implement GDP with defined review triggers:
onset of +SN,  
RVR/vis < 1/2SM,  
runway closure for plowing,  
braking action reports MED/POOR,  
departure delays exceeding +45 with surface saturation.

Phase 2 — Conditions Deteriorate Beyond Forecast (1400Z–1800Z)

Weather update sequence (delivered progressively by instructor)

METAR 1400Z:  
KDTW 171400Z 06014G20KT 1SM SN OVC006 M06/M08 A2986 RMK AO2 P0003

Airport Ops report (1415Z):
“RWY 22L contamination increasing; plow cycle every 35–40 minutes. Friction check pending.”

METAR 1600Z:  
KDTW 171600Z 07016G24KT 3/4SM SN OVC005 M06/M08 A2983 RMK AO2 P0005

Runway condition report (1620Z):
Braking action trending MED on primary landing runway; request reduced arrival acceptance and spacing.

Instructor Prompt (1625Z)
“DTW calls: they are no longer confident they can sustain the GDP rate due to runway condition and plow closures. What are your next steps?”

Expected trainee actions:
Call D21/DTW Tower to validate actual acceptance and closure windows.
Decide whether to:
  1) Revise GDP to lower rates and/or
  2) Initiate a Ground Stop (likely 1st tier initially) to absorb the immediate constraint and prevent airborne holding.
Communicate clearly to airlines that runway condition changes are operationally non-negotiable.

Phase 3 — GDP to Ground Stop Transition (1800Z–2100Z)

Event Inject: Heavy Band Arrives Early + Runway Closure
METAR 1830Z:  
KDTW 171830Z 07018G26KT 1/4SM +SN FZFG OVC003 M06/M07 A2981 RMK AO2 P0010

DTW Tower (1835Z) calls student:
“We’re going to close the arrival runway for plowing and sanding. Expect no arrivals for 35 minutes. Braking action now POOR on the last report.”

D21 TRACON:
“We can’t take arrivals. We’re shutting the airport down. Do not launch anyone at us.”

Assessment Question (Instructor)
“What’s the appropriate initiative right now: GDP revision only, or a Ground Stop—what scope and duration?”

Expected trainee answer:
Ground Stop required immediately due to arrival runway closure and POOR braking; GDP alone will not prevent airborne holding/diversions in the near term.
Start with 1st-tier GS if feasible, then expand as needed based on demand and airborne list.
Plan a GDP revision out of the GS with conservative rates until runway condition improves.

Ground Stop Conference (Conflict: Facility Requests Restrictive Scope / Airlines Push Back) — 1840Z

Student (conference control technique):
“We are in an immediate safety-driven constraint: runway closure and POOR braking. I’ll take DTW/D21 first for operational status, then ZOB, then users. We will decide stop scope and planned review time.”

Facility Side (Restrictive Requests)
DTW Tower:
“We need a hard stop. We can’t accept any arrivals until the runway is treated and we get a new friction report. Expect at least 35 minutes, possibly longer.”

D21 TRACON:
“Recommend 2nd-tier to keep pressure off. If aircraft keep coming, we’ll see holding, and we’re not staffed for extended holding with deice constraints.”

ZOB TMU:
“We support expanding scope. We already have aircraft deviating and burn issues. If this becomes multiple closures, we’ll have diversions to CLE/GRR/FNT.”

Airline Pushback (Prefer Lower Rates vs Hard Stop)
DAL Dispatch:
“We’d rather accept a very low arrival rate than a stop. A stop creates diversions and crew timeouts.”

UAL Dispatch:
“If you stop too broadly, you’ll strand outstations. Can you keep it 1st-tier and short—review in 20 minutes?”

AAL Dispatch:
“Agree with DAL: we prefer a reduced rate posture. If you must stop, give a realistic end time and don’t roll it in 10-minute increments.”

Expected trainee decision
Implement DTW Ground Stop immediately, safety-driven:
Scope: start 1st-tier, expand to 2nd-tier if airborne holding builds or if D21 confirms multiple closure cycles.
Duration: 1840Z–1920Z (40 minutes) with a defined review at 1910Z.
State plainly: “This is due to runway closure and POOR braking action; we are preventing airborne holding and diversions.”

Instructor grading focus:
Clarity of trigger (runway closure + POOR braking).
Strong conference control; short, directive, non-negotiable on safety.
Proper execution: advisories, coordination calls, and update cadence.

Phase 4 — Out of Ground Stop into Revised GDP (1920Z–0100Z)

Reopen Inject
DTW Tower (1915Z):
“Runway reopening. Braking action improved to MED after treatment, but snowfall remains heavy. We can accept limited arrivals with increased spacing.”

D21:
“We can take 22–26 arrivals per hour for the next couple hours. If +SN persists, we may have to pulse again.”

GDP Revision Telcon — 1925Z
DTW Tower: “We’re fragile. Keep it low until we get out of the band.”  
D21: “Support 26 now; if it improves, we can step to 30–32 later.”  
ZOB: “We need stability—publish a plan and stick to review points.”  
DAL: “We can live with 26 if it prevents another stop. Please avoid optimistic step-ups.”  
UAL: “Agree—keep it conservative; we don’t want diversions.”  
AAL: “Concur. Give us a known low rate rather than repeated stops.”

Expected trainee revised rates (example):
20Z–22Z: 26  
22Z–01Z: 30  
01Z–04Z: 36 (if snowfall decreases)  
(Adjustable if the trainee uses different logic; instructor ensures outcome remains “low and stable until band passes.”)

TEB/Metro Considerations (Optional Complexity Inject for Terminal East)
If instructor wants added realism without changing primary DTW focus:
Expect diversions to CLE/GRR/FNT/YYZ; coordinate with adjacent specialists if airborne list grows.
Ensure trainee monitors alternate airport weather and runway availability.

Instructor Prompts / Evaluation Items (Throughout)

Key Questions the trainee should ask facilities
“What is your current arrival acceptance and why (spacing, braking, plow cycle)?”
“Is this a full closure or periodic closure? What are the cycle times?”
“What is the next friction report time and trigger for improvement?”
“What’s your diversion posture—are you requesting we prevent launches?”

Key information the trainee should provide users
Clear trigger: “Runway closure / braking POOR / +SN with RVR reductions.”
Specific plan: “Stop scope, end time, review time, and expected out-of-stop GDP rates.”
Stability commitment: “Next scheduled review at XX:XXZ; revisions only on agreed triggers.”

Performance Outcomes
Student identifies the need for a GDP early to manage predictable demand/capacity mismatch.
Student recognizes when conditions exceed forecast and transitions to a Ground Stop promptly.
Student balances facility needs against airline concerns, but does not compromise safety.
Student runs conferences with control: structured order, concise summaries, explicit decisions, and documented objections.

End of Simulation (Instructor Termination Point)
Simulation ends once the trainee:
1) Issues the DTW Ground Stop, coordinates scope/duration properly, and  
2) Cancels the GS and implements a revised GDP reflecting degraded winter operations, with documented review triggers and stakeholder notifications complete.

Prompt: Generate a terminal training scenario for Miami (MIA) involving summertime convective weather. The student must manage arrival/departure compression and gate closures, deciding when to implement a GDP and eventually a Ground Stop as lightning and runway closures peak.
Scenario:

Position: ATCSCC (Terminal East) / MIA focus  
Shift Start: 1000Z  
Tools Available: TSD, FSM, TBFM view, CEDAR/CBP advisories, FDPS demand, SWIM feeds, airline contact list, lightning monitor, ramp/rwy closure status board.

At 1000Z the operation is in a typical summer pattern: moist airmass, sea-breeze boundary expected, and a mid-level disturbance that may enhance convection later. MIA is currently west flow with no active TMIs. Demand is climbing into the late morning arrival push.

Current METAR at takeover (for context):  
KMIA 101000Z 11007KT P6SM FEW020 SCT050 27/24 A3006

Planned weather storyline (what the instructor will drive):

Instructor: “Based on the TAF and current demand trend, what do you expect will be the first operational constraint at MIA today: arrival rate reduction, departure push restriction, or a ground stop? What time window and why?”

Expected trainee focus:

If storms train over the field, expect temporary runway inspection/closure for water and braking action checks.”

Stakeholder Dialogue — GDP Planning Telcon (Target ~1530Z)
Instructor (as ATCSCC TMU): “We’re discussing MIA impacts for storms building toward the terminal area. Goal is to avoid holding and diversions.”

ZMA Approach
ZMA: “We can probably stay near normal for another hour, but the corridor deviations are increasing. I recommend planning a GDP that starts before the peak so we’re not forced into holding when the gates pinch.”

MIA Tower
Tower: “Surface can’t absorb long departure queues if ramp shuts. If we take too many arrivals during a ramp stop, we’ll gridlock fast.”

ZMA Center Side (ZMA TMU)

Airline Dispatch — American (AAL)
AAL Dispatch: “We have a heavy bank into MIA. We can support a GDP if it prevents airborne holding. We want clear triggers for a GS if lightning ramps go red.”

Airline Dispatch — Delta (DAL)
DAL Dispatch: “If you GDP too early and the storms slide south, we’ll take unnecessary delay. We prefer a conservative GDP start closer to confirmed impacts.”

Airline Dispatch — United (UAL)
UAL Dispatch: “We’re more concerned with diversion risk on the international feed. If you see ramp closure risk, we’d rather meter on the ground than hold airborne.”

Instructor prompt to trainee:  
“Summarize the plan you’re proposing: start time, initial rate posture, and the trigger you’ll use to escalate to a Ground Stop.”

Expected trainee answer elements:
Propose GDP timed ahead of 16–19Z impacts (or a stepped plan).
Define triggers: lightning within 5 miles + ramp closure; runway closure; acceptance rate collapse; sustained TSRA over field.
Communicate contingency: GS into GDP revision.

Escalation Event — Ground Stop Trigger (2020Z)
MIA Tower (urgent): “Ramp closed due lightning. RWY 09 closed for water inspection. We cannot accept the arrival demand at anything close to normal. We need a Ground Stop now.”

ZMA Approach: “We’re going to start holding in the air within 15–20 minutes if you don’t stop the feed. Deviations are blocking efficient streams.”

Ground Stop Coordination Script (Instructor/Student Practice)
Instructor: “As the student, you will:  
1) state scope recommendation (1st tier vs expanded),  
2) state planned duration,  
3) state the out plan (GDP revision).”

Expected student phrasing (example):  
“Implement MIA Ground Stop due to lightning/ramp closure and runway closure. Recommend 1st-tier to immediately stop near-term arrivals; evaluate expansion if acceptance remains zero. Plan to transition to GDP revision once ramp reopens and runway status is confirmed.”

Airline Pushback (during GS call)
AAL Dispatch: “Support GS if the ramp is closed, but we need a realistic duration. We’re already seeing diversion decisions being made.”  
DAL Dispatch: “If you can keep any arrivals trickling in, do it. Diversions are operationally expensive.”  
ZMA: “Acceptance is effectively zero until ramp and runway reopen. A trickle won’t be stable.”

Recovery — GS Extension / GDP Revision Telcon (Approx 2300Z)
Weather Improving (METAR)
KMIA 102300Z 24010KT 6SM -SHRA VCTS SCT020 BKN040 27/25 A2996

MIA Tower: “Lightning is moving off. Ramp is reopening in phases. RWY 09 back open after inspection. We can start building back up, but we need time to drain the surface and clear queues.”

ZMA Approach: “We can take arrivals again, but not at full bank rates immediately. Expect residual deviations and compression.”

Airline Requests
UAL Dispatch: “We need a cautious recovery rate; avoid a second stop.”  
AAL Dispatch: “We’d like a stepped increase; keep it stable for planning.”

Instructor prompt to trainee:  
“Build your out-of-stop plan: what GDP rates do you propose for the first two hours after cancellation, and what data are you using to justify it?”

TEB-Style Note Not Applicable / Adjacent Coordination (South Florida)
Instructor: “Ensure the student coordinates impacts to nearby flows: FLL, PBI, and MCO overflight constraints. Look for proactive calls to ZMA TMU and impacted departure centers when GS/GDP changes occur.”

End-State / Evaluation Criteria
Instructor will assess whether the student:
Identified early compression risk and communicated it clearly.
Chose an appropriate posture (GDP early vs wait/GS into GDP) and defended it with weather + demand logic.
Executed correct escalation to Ground Stop at lightning/ramp closure peak.
Managed recovery via GDP revision with stable, stepwise rate increases.
Controlled telcons professionally: concise recap, clear ask, clear decision, documented dissent.

End of Simulation
Stop the scenario after the student cancels/steps down the Ground Stop and publishes the GDP revision with required notifications completed.

Prompt: Generate a terminal training scenario for Las Vegas (LAS) involving high event-driven demand and volatile wind shifts. The student must navigate multiple cycles of Ground Stops and GDPs as runway configurations become restricted, coordinating between ZLA and stakeholders.
Scenario:
LAS Terminal Training Scenario — High Demand + Volatile Winds (Ground Stops/GDP Cycles)

Background / Scenario Setup (Start: 0800 UTC)
Facility/Role: ATCSCC (Command Center) — Western Terminal Specialist managing LAS (primary), with coordination to ZLA, LAS Tower, and Airlines.  
Shift Theme: High, event-driven demand into LAS with multiple wind-driven configuration changes. Student must manage repeated transitions between Ground Stops and GDPs, including out-of-status recovery, rate revisions, and stakeholder coordination.

Operational Context:
A major event in Las Vegas (concert + convention) drives arrival push 0900–1300Z and a second push 1600–1900Z.  
Winds will be volatile with rapid shifts, triggering frequent configuration restrictions and runway crossings that reduce acceptance rates.  
Anticipate surface saturation risk: gate holds, ramp congestion, and departure metering impacts due to stop-and-go arrival management.

Initial Airport Status (Instructor brief):
LAS in west flow and running efficiently at the start, but forecast indicates wind shifts that will force periodic single-runway or reduced acceptance configurations.
Student has access to FSM, TSD, ASD/ASPM feeds, SWIM/NTML, and airline contact list.

Weather (woven into setup)
At check-in, the student is provided the current METAR and TAF package for LAS:

METAR sequence:
KLAS 080756Z 24008KT 10SM FEW080 18/05 A3005 RMK AO2
KLAS 083956Z 21014G24KT 10SM SCT070 22/06 A3001
KLAS 085556Z 17018G30KT 7SM BLDU SCT060 26/07 A2996
KLAS 091256Z 09015G25KT 10SM FEW070 28/08 A2992
KLAS 092056Z 25022G35KT 6SM BLDU SCT050 30/06 A2990

TAF:
KLAS 080520Z 0806/0912 24008KT P6SM FEW080
FM080900 22012G22KT P6SM SCT070
FM081400 17018G30KT P6SM BLDU SCT060
TEMPO 0815/0819 16022G35KT 3SM BLDU BKN050
FM082000 09015G25KT P6SM FEW070
FM090100 25018G30KT P6SM BLDU SCT060
TEMPO 0902/0906 26025G38KT 2SM BLDU BKN050

Instructor Note (for pacing): Winds drive two major configuration restrictions:
1) SE wind period (approx 14–20Z) forcing reduced acceptance and/or runway crossing constraints.  
2) Strong W/NW gusts (after 01Z) creating intermittent arrival slowdowns with BLDU and turbulence advisories.

Assessment Question (1030 UTC)
Instructor Prompt:  
“Based on the demand profile and the wind-driven configuration risk at LAS, when do you expect the first capacity issue, and what’s your first initiative choice if the airport drops below demand?”

Expected Trainee Answer (target):
Identify LAS as the primary constraint; anticipate first risk by 0900–1000Z as winds trend gusty and demand increases.
Propose GDP if reduced rates are forecast/known; propose short-scope Ground Stop if configuration change is imminent/uncertain and surface saturation is likely.

Traffic / Demand Injects (Instructor-controlled)
Demand snapshot (arrivals scheduled):
0900–1000Z: 52 scheduled  
1000–1100Z: 58 scheduled  
1100–1200Z: 62 scheduled  
1200–1300Z: 56 scheduled  
1600–1700Z: 54 scheduled  
1700–1800Z: 60 scheduled  
1800–1900Z: 57 scheduled  

Baseline acceptance (if stable west flow): 44–46 AAR.  
Restricted acceptance (single runway / crossing constraints): 24–30 AAR.  
Airport shutdown posture (if winds shift + ramp saturation): 0–10 AAR (effectively “stop arrivals” for short periods).

Scenario Timeline & Decision Points

Phase 1 — Early Demand Push + First Wind Shift (0800–1100Z)

0830Z Instructor Inject
Updated METAR trend indicates gusts building sooner than forecast. Tower reports increased spacing on finals due to turbulence on approach.

Agent Dialogue (Instructor/Roleplay)
LAS Tower: “We’re still west flow, but we’re starting to see approach compression. If gusts build, we may need extra in-trail and we’ll lose the high side of the rate.”
ZLA TMU: “Heads up, we’re seeing minor vectoring and speed control already. No gate closures yet, but I’m not confident we hold full acceptance through the 10Z hour.”
Airline Dispatch (SWA): “We’ve got a heavy bank inbound 10–13Z. If you’re going to run a program, we need lead time to protect turn plans.”

Instructor Prompt:  
“Do you model a GDP now based on forecast risk, or wait for confirmation and be prepared to Ground Stop into a GDP?”

Acceptable Trainee Options:
1) GDP proposal starting 0930Z with stepped rates and revision trigger.  
2) Wait-and-see with a planned 1st-tier Ground Stop template ready, plus coordination pre-brief with ZLA/LAS.

0905Z Trigger Event — Configuration Restriction Begins
Wind swings and increases; Tower reports runway crossing limitations and reduced arrival acceptance.

Agent Dialogue
LAS Tower: “We’re constrained—crossing coordination is slowing arrivals. We can only support about 28 arrivals per hour until this settles.”
ZLA TMU: “We’re starting to build holding in-sector for arrivals already. If demand stays where it is, we’ll be stacking airplanes.”
Airline Dispatch (UAL): “We don’t want diversions to PHX/LAX. We’d rather see you lower the rate now than take airborne holding later.”

Instructor Prompt:  
“What initiative do you implement first to stop the bleeding, and what scope?”

Expected Trainee Action (target):
Implement a 1st-tier Ground Stop immediately to arrest airborne holding and prevent diversions.
Concurrently model a GDP out of the stop with a near-term low rate (28) and planned step-up if winds stabilize.

Telcon #1 — Ground Stop to GDP Transition (0930Z)  
Objective: Align facilities/users on immediate stop + controlled restart.

Student Conference Control Technique (required)
Student should open with:
“This is Command Center, LAS program discussion. I’ll go around the room—Tower, TRACON/TMU, Center TMU, then users. I’ll summarize and propose a decision at the end.”

Scripted Stakeholder Dialogue (Prompt / Expected Answer Pairs)
Student (to LAS Tower): “Confirm current acceptance and configuration constraints.”  
  LAS Tower: “We’re effectively at 28 AAR right now. We can’t support the previous rate with the crossing restrictions and gusts.”
Student (to ZLA TMU): “What are you seeing—holding, gate impacts, or reroutes?”  
  ZLA TMU: “Holding is starting. If we don’t stop arrivals, we’ll be forced into miles-in-trail and ad-hoc holds. We support a program now.”
Student (to Airlines): “User impacts and preferences—rate vs. diversion risk.”  
  UAL Dispatch: “We want a lower rate early to limit diversions. Recommend 28 until confidence improves.”  
  SWA Dispatch: “Agree—if you’re going to do it, do it now. We can manage ground delay better than holding.”
Student (summary/decision statement):  
  “We’ll execute a 1st-tier Ground Stop now due to acceptance drop, then transition to a GDP with an initial 28 rate, revisable upward if Tower confirms recovery.”

Instructor Evaluation Focus: student keeps order, states problem succinctly, confirms rates, declares decision and next update time.

Phase 2 — Brief Recovery, Then Second Restriction (1100–1500Z)

1115Z Inject — Temporary Improvement
Tower indicates a short window of improved acceptance.
LAS Tower: “We can take a few more—maybe 34 for the next hour if winds hold.”
ZLA TMU: “We can deliver 34; but if winds swing again, we’ll drop fast.”

Instructor Prompt:  
“Do you revise the GDP up, hold conservative, or cancel?”

Expected Considerations:
Demand still high; canceling risks immediate reimplementation.
Conservative revision (28→34) with a short review window is often preferable.

1410Z Inject — SE Wind Period Arrives Early
Winds settle into sustained SE with gusts; configuration becomes more restrictive than earlier.
LAS Tower: “We’re now in the SE wind regime. Arrival acceptance is 24–26 with the way we have to run the surface.”
ZLA TMU: “Gate constraints plus deviations—delivery is inconsistent. We can’t support a smooth feed.”

Airline Conflict Inject
AAL Dispatch: “Request 20–22 for the next two hours to avoid diversion cascade.”  
SWA Dispatch: “We can’t support 20—Vegas turns will collapse. Keep it at 26–28 and manage with revisions if needed.”

Instructor Prompt:  
“How do you balance stability vs. throughput, and what do you tell the users?”

Expected Trainee Response:
Acknowledge diversion risk; choose a defendable rate aligned to facility acceptance (e.g., 26) rather than user-preferred extremes.
Set a review time and communicate that rate changes will track Tower-confirmed acceptance.

Telcon #2 — Rate Re-Set Under SE Flow (1445Z)
Conference control requirement: student must prevent side arguments and keep to decision.

Scripted Dialogue
ZLA TMU: “We recommend start now with 26. If you delay, we’ll have holding in-sector.”
LAS Tower: “We confirm 26 sustainable; anything higher will create missed approaches and surface gridlock.”
AAL Dispatch: “We request 56 is impossible; we’re asking for 20–22 to protect from diversions.”  
UAL Dispatch: “We can support 26, but we need you to stop yo-yo revisions—pick something stable.”
Student (control line): “Understood. We’re setting 26 based on Tower acceptance. Next review in 30 minutes. If acceptance improves, we’ll revise up; if the field starts missing approaches or gate-holds, we’ll revise down and consider a short stop.”

Phase 3 — Airport Shutdown Posture (1500–1700Z)

1555Z Trigger — Ramp Saturation + Arrival Halt
A short-fuse wind surge produces rapid configuration constraints; Tower reports inability to accept arrivals temporarily.
LAS Tower: “We’re saturated. We need an arrival stop while we reset the surface. Expect at least 30–45 minutes.”
ZLA TMU: “We can’t meter this tactically—recommend a Ground Stop immediately.”

Instructor Prompt:  
“Do you extend GDP low, or implement a Ground Stop? What scope and what’s your plan to restart?”

Expected Trainee Action (target):
Implement Ground Stop (1st-tier initially, expand if needed) due to near-zero acceptance.
Build a GDP out with conservative low rates for restart and step-ups.

Telcon #3 — Shutdown / Restart Plan (1605Z)
Scripted facility-user pressure: airlines want very low restart rate to avoid diversions; facility wants structured restart.
LAS Tower: “No arrivals until we reset; then we can take 20 for the first hour back.”
ZLA TMU: “Agree. We’ll need a clean restart; we support 20 then 26.”
UAL Dispatch: “Request 20 until you’re sure the SE winds are stable. Diversions are already staging.”
SWA Dispatch: “We need predictability—fine with 20 for one hour, then step up if you actually can.”

Student required close-out:  
“We’re executing a Ground Stop now due to zero/near-zero acceptance, then restarting with a GDP: 20 for the first hour, 26 the following hour, with a defined review time at +30 minutes after restart.”

Phase 4 — Evening Bank + Strong W/NW Gusts (1700–2100Z)

1800Z Inject — Winds Shift Back, But Gustier
Winds swing west with BLDU; acceptance improves but is unstable.
LAS Tower: “We can move back toward west flow, but gusts are strong. We can support 32–36 if it stays consistent.”
ZLA TMU: “We can deliver. But if gusts spike, expect compression again.”

Instructor Prompt:  
“Do you cancel the GDP, revise upward, or hold a modest rate to avoid another stop?”

Expected Good Outcome:  
Revise upward cautiously (e.g., 26→32→36) with short review windows rather than immediate cancel, given volatility.

TEB/Other Airport Impacts
Not applicable in this LAS scenario. Instructor Note: If the trainee attempts to apply irrelevant metro constraints, redirect: focus remains on LAS/ZLA system.

Instructor Controls / Scoring Items
Key Performance Objectives
1) Early recognition of demand-capacity mismatch under wind uncertainty.  
2) Correct selection and sequencing: GS → GDP out during hard acceptance drops.  
3) Quality of GDP modeling decisions: stable rates, realistic step-ups, revision timing.  
4) Conference discipline: orderly roll call, clear summary, decision statement, next update time.  
5) Stakeholder management: document objections, but base rates on Tower/ZLA acceptance.

Required Student Behaviors (observable)
Ask Tower for: acceptance rate, configuration, expected duration, restart capability.  
Ask ZLA TMU for: holding status, gate impacts, MIT needs, reroute feasibility.  
Ask airlines for: diversion pressure, bank sensitivity, tolerance for ground delay.  
Communicate: “decision, rationale, timeframe, next coordination point.”

End State
Scenario ends when:
Student has executed at least two cycles of Ground Stop into GDP, plus one GDP revision upward after recovery, while maintaining coordination with ZLA and LAS Tower and addressing airline requests without losing operational control.

Prompt: Generate a terminal training scenario for San Diego (SAN) involving recurring, unexpected fog and low ceilings. The student must manage a multi-day cycle of Ground Stops and diversion recovery, accounting for gate saturation and airport volume constraints.
Scenario:
SAN Terminal Training Scenario (Level 3): Recurring Fog / Low Ceilings, Multi‑Day Recovery

Background / Scenario Setup
Facility/Role: ATCSCC TMU – Terminal (West) desk; primary focus SAN with secondary consideration LAX/SNA/ONT diversions.  
Shift Start: 0800 UTC (Day 1)  
Tools Available: FSM/TSD, TFMS, IDMS, SWIM/ASPM views, AAR/ADR worksheets, diversion list, gate-occupancy notes from airport ops, reroute monitor, call‑out lists.

Instructor Setup Note (Simulation Framing): This exercise runs as a “compressed multi‑day” sequence. The student will work Day 1 (0800–1800Z), then receive structured handoffs and immediately “roll” into Day 2 (0800–1600Z) with carryover delays, diverted aircraft, and gate constraints.

Day 1 – Initial Weather Picture (0800Z)
METAR (Day 1):  
KSAN 010756Z 00000KT 1/2SM FG VV002 16/16 A3008 RMK AO2 SLP180 T01610161  

TAF (Day 1):  
KSAN 010520Z 0106/0212 VRB03KT 2SM BR OVC006  
TEMPO 0106/0114 1/2SM FG VV002  
FM011500 25008KT P6SM SCT008 BKN020  
FM012100 24010KT P6SM SCT015  
FM020300 VRB03KT 2SM BR OVC006  
TEMPO 0205/0212 1/2SM FG VV002

Operational Context Provided to Student at 0800Z:
SAN is in LIFR at shift start with intermittent RVR reductions.  
Morning bank demand is moderate but building; several inbound transcons are already airborne.  
Forecast suggests improvement mid‑morning but with high recurrence risk after 03Z.

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “Based on the current SAN METAR/TAF and the bank structure, which airport(s) do you expect will face capacity issues in the next 6 hours—and why?”

Expected Trainee Answer (Key Points):
SAN will be the primary constraint due to VV002 and FG driving AAR collapse and potential arrival holding/diversions.
Secondary impacts likely at LAX/SNA/ONT due to diversion absorption and gate constraints, plus regional flow compression if SAN arrivals get held/resequenced.

Day 1 Timeline & Decision Points

0805Z – Airport Ops Call (Gate Constraint Introduced)
SAN Airport Ops (Agent): “We’re already tight on gates this morning. Two overnight diversions returned late and are still on the ground. If we get more diversions in, we’ll be looking at hard‑stand or remote parking within the hour.”

Instructor Prompt: “What additional information do you need before selecting a TMI?”

Expected Trainee Actions / Questions:
Current AAR/ADR from TRACON; surface saturation indicators (departure delays, gate holds).
Inbound airborne count to SAN by time band (next 60–120 minutes).
Diversion plan: primary alternates, current gate availability at alternates, fuel-critical aircraft.

0810Z – NCT TRACON Brief
NCT (Agent): “SAN is fluctuating between 1/2SM FG VV002 and 1SM BR OVC004. We’re not stable. If it stays like this, we’ll be effectively minimal rate with a real chance of a stop if RVR drops again.”

Instructor: “Given this uncertainty, what are your options right now?”

Trainee Options (All Valid; Must Choose & Defend):
No TMI yet; monitor and prepare a rapid GS trigger if RVR drops.  
1st‑tier Ground Stop to prevent airborne holding and protect alternates.  
Short GDP (conservative) to meter arrivals until forecast improvement window.  
GS now + immediate GDP proposal to control demand and protect gate recovery.

Instructor Evaluation Focus: Whether the student prioritizes diversion prevention and gate protection over “waiting for improvement.”

0820Z – Weather Worsens Unexpectedly (Fog Recurrence Early)
METAR Update (Day 1):  
KSAN 010820Z 00000KT 1/4SM FG VV001 16/16 A3007 RMK AO2 SLP176 T01610161

NCT (Agent): “We just went VV001. We can’t support normal arrivals. Expect missed approaches and holding if you keep feeding.”

Instructor Prompt: “Do you need a Ground Stop? If yes, scope and duration?”

Expected Trainee Answer (Target):
Yes: implement a 1st‑tier Ground Stop immediately; choose duration long enough to capture near‑term arrivals and prevent airborne stacks; plan to transition to GDP if fog persists beyond the initial window.

Stakeholder Dialogue – Day 1 Ground Stop / GDP Transition

0830Z – Initial Ground Stop Coordination (Facilities‑Only)
ATCSCC Student (Trainee): Initiates facilities call.

NCT: “Support a GS. Field is down to essentially zero acceptance when RVR bottoms out. We need to stop the push and stabilize.”  
SAN Tower: “We’re in and out. Departures are also impacted—expect gate holds if arrivals can’t park.”  
ZLA TMU: “We have multiple SAN inbounds airborne. If you don’t stop this, we’ll hold in ZLA and risk fuel and diversions to LAX.”

Instructor (Role Guidance): Evaluate whether the trainee:
States the problem, expected duration, and planned next step (GDP proposal if not improving).
Captures and repeats back facility constraints and airborne risk.

0840Z – Users Brought In (Airline Diversion Risk vs. Gate Saturation)
AAL Dispatch: “We’d rather take delay on the ground than end up diverting. What’s the plan and when do you expect improvement?”  
SWA Dispatch: “SAN gate situation is our big concern. If our diversions get stuck at LAX, we’ll be out of position for the next rotation.”  
UAL Dispatch: “Request a conservative program now. We need predictability to avoid long‑haul diversions.”

Instructor Prompt: “What do you tell the users about gate saturation and how that affects your choice of GS vs GDP?”

Expected Trainee Response Elements:
Gate saturation increases diversion risk; GS protects alternates and prevents airborne holding.
GDP provides predictability and helps manage recovery sequencing once acceptance returns.
Communicate likely program evolution: GS now; GDP when improvement timing becomes credible.

Day 1: Program Evolution

0930Z – Partial Improvement, Still Unstable
METAR Update (Day 1):  
KSAN 010930Z 24004KT 1SM BR OVC003 16/16 A3008 RMK AO2 SLP179

NCT: “We can take a trickle, but it’s unstable. If you restart too hard, we’ll have missed approaches and immediate holding.”

Instructor Prompt: “How do you come out of the GS—cancel, extend, or convert to GDP?”

Expected Trainee Choice (Preferred):
Convert to a GDP out of the GS with stepped rates, allowing the surface and gate situation to recover.  
Maintain conservative rates until ceilings become reliably ≥ OVC008 and vis improves.

1030Z – Day 1 Telcon: Recovery GDP
NCT: “We can support a controlled rate if you keep it conservative for the next 2 hours.”  
SAN Airport Ops: “We need time to tow and reopen gates. If you surge arrivals, we’ll gridlock.”  
Airline (SWA): “Keep the rate lower now; we’re prioritizing turning diversions back in cleanly.”  
Airline (DAL): “Request earlier release if the ceiling lifts—we don’t want unnecessary delay.”

Instructor Technique Prompt (“Controlling the Conference”):
Student must summarize: current METAR, trend, risk, proposed plan, and then ask:  
  “Any operational concerns with this plan?” (not “Is everyone okay with it?”)

Day 1 End-State (Carryover Setup)
By late Day 1, SAN improves briefly, but evening recurrence is forecast in the TAF (FM020300…). The student is told multiple aircraft diverted earlier and some are still out of position for Day 2 morning.

Instructor: “You will brief the oncoming shift. Your handoff must include: diversion tail numbers/locations, remaining gate constraints, and what triggers would cause you to re‑stop SAN overnight.”

Day 2 – Recurring Fog Cycle & Diversion Recovery

Day 2 Background (0800Z)
Shift Start: 0800Z (Day 2)  
Handoff Note: SAN had intermittent improvement overnight but fog redeveloped earlier than forecast. Multiple aircraft remain displaced; several morning departures are delayed due to late‑arriving equipment.

METAR (Day 2):  
KSAN 020755Z 00000KT 1/2SM FG VV002 15/15 A3009 RMK AO2 SLP186 T01500150

TAF (Day 2):  
KSAN 020520Z 0206/0312 VRB03KT 1SM BR OVC004  
TEMPO 0206/0216 1/4SM FG VV001  
FM021700 26008KT P6SM SCT010 BKN025  
FM030300 VRB03KT 1SM BR OVC004  
TEMPO 0305/0312 1/4SM FG VV001

Assessment Question (Day 2 Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “What’s different today compared to Day 1, and what does that change about your initiative selection?”

Expected Answer:
Day 2 includes network recovery constraints: displaced aircraft, crew legality, gate saturation from returns.  
This increases value of early, predictable TMIs to prevent repeated diversions and protect alternates.

Day 2 Decision Fork
Path A – Early Controlled GDP (Proactive)
Implement GDP early with conservative rates through TEMPO window, step up at FM021700.

Path B – Wait & See, Then GS into GDP (Reactive)
Monitor until VV001/FG triggers missed approaches; then GS; then GDP once improvement timing is credible.

Instructor Requirement: Student must articulate trigger criteria (e.g., ceiling/vis thresholds, missed approach rate, gate saturation indicator, airborne count).

Day 2 Stakeholder Conflict: “Stop Early” vs “Keep It Moving”

0835Z – Facilities Call
NCT: “We’re seeing VV001 hits again. If you keep feeding, we’ll stack and divert.”  
SAN Tower: “Surface is brittle. Gate holds are already in play because yesterday’s diversions are still cycling.”  
ZLA TMU: “We can manage some holding, but diversions are the bigger threat. Recommend controlling demand now.”

0840Z – Airlines Call
SWA Dispatch: “We’re asking for a lower controlled rate now. We cannot afford another round of diversions—our gates are saturated regionally.”  
UAL Dispatch: “Support early action. Predictability is more valuable than optimistic forecasts.”  
AAL Dispatch: “If you go conservative, build in a mechanism to revise quickly if it clears.”

Instructor Prompt: “How do you address ‘revise quickly’ while maintaining credibility?”

Expected Technique:
Commit to a scheduled reassessment time (e.g., top of the hour) and specify objective triggers for revision.

TEB-Style Constraint Equivalent for SAN (Diversion Alternates Management)
Instructor Insert (Regional Management Task):  
The student must manage alternate saturation (LAX/SNA/ONT) similarly to how one would manage a satellite airport constraint:
Set a diversion monitoring plan.
Communicate to facilities/users that alternates are becoming constrained.
If alternates saturate, tighten scope/duration of GS/GDP to prevent uncontrolled diversions.

ZLA (Agent): “LAX is absorbing multiple SAN diversions; ONT ramp is getting tight. If SAN stays down, you need to stop the feed earlier to avoid scattering aircraft.”

Day 2 End: Recovery Posture
When SAN improves near FM021700, the student must:
Sequence return‑to‑field diversions without surging beyond gate capability.
Coordinate phased increases with NCT/SAN ops.
Provide a closing brief emphasizing multi‑day fog recurrence risk with the overnight TEMPO window.

Instructor Notes / Performance Objectives
The trainee is successful if they:
Recognize recurring fog as a multi‑day system risk and protect the NAS from repeated diversion cycles.
Choose and defend a coherent strategy (proactive GDP vs reactive GS→GDP) using objective triggers.
Explicitly manage gate saturation as a capacity limiter, not just runway acceptance.
Control conferences with a concise structure: Situation → Impact → Plan → Timing/Triggers → Ask for concerns.
Maintain a running recovery picture: displaced aircraft, alternates, surface constraints, and program revision timing.

End of Simulation

Prompt: Generate a terminal training scenario for Dallas/Fort Worth (DFW) involving rapidly developing thunderstorms and high diversion rates. The student must choose between GDPs and Ground Stops as the weather oscillates, adapting the plan to unpredictable shifts in conditions.
Scenario:
DFW Terminal Weather Disruption Scenario (Rapid TS Development / High Diversions)

Background / Scenario Setup (Start Time: 1030Z)
Position/Role: Command Center Terminal Specialist (DFW focus), coordinating with ZFW, DFW TRACON (D10), DFW ATCT, and airline SOCs/dispatch.  
Shift Context: Overnight into morning push. DFW is running near demand with multiple hub banks building through late morning. Convection is forecast to develop rapidly near the Metroplex with uncertain placement and short-cycle intensification/decay—high risk of rolling Ground Stops, GDP revisions, and diversions.

At 1030Z, DFW is operating normally in south flow, but the convective environment is already unstable west/northwest of the field. The student has FSM/TSD/NTML, can coordinate via telcon, and can issue GS/GDP as needed.

Initial Observed Weather (METAR)
KDFW 271030Z 17010KT 10SM FEW050 SCT090 24/19 A2992 RMK AO2

Forecast Weather (TAF) — Given at Sim Start
KDFW 271020Z 2710/2812 17012KT P6SM SCT060  
  FM271400 17015G22KT P6SM SCT050 BKN080  
  TEMPO2716/2720 3SM TSRA BKN025CB OVC060  
  FM272000 20018G28KT 5SM -SHRA VCTS BKN030CB  
  TEMPO2721/2801 2SM TSRA BKN015CB  
  FM280100 22012KT P6SM VCSH SCT025 BKN050  
  PROB30 2804/2808 2SM TSRA BKN020CB

Instructor Note: The key training objective is forcing the student to manage oscillating field impacts: brief improvements that tempt “cancel/relax,” followed by rapid redevelopment that drives diversion risk and surface saturation.

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “Based on what you see at 1030Z—current METAR, the TEMPO TS windows, and the gust trend—what do you expect the primary constraint will be at DFW today: rate reduction, gate blockage, runway configuration risk, or full airport shutdown? What’s your first action?”

Expected Trainee Response (Target):
Identify rapid convective onset risk 16–20Z and highest uncertainty 21Z–01Z.
Recognize that diversion risk rises before full shutdown due to gate holds, ramp closures, and inability to meter arrivals tactically.
First action: get D10/ZFW weather and ops brief, determine trigger criteria (e.g., ramp closure, final approach blocked, SWAP/route impacts), and be prepared to GS tactically then transition to GDP if the impact persists beyond one hour.

Instructor Setup / Tools
Student has: TSD/FSM, arrival demand by hour, EFSTS/AFP view (as applicable), advisory database, and coordination lines.  
Traffic Assumption (for simulation realism):
Demand builds into late morning/early afternoon with hub bank pressure.
Multiple arrival streams from west/northwest susceptible to early deviations.

Instructor Technique: The scenario is driven by three weather “swings”:
Pre-impact build (14–16Z): strong outflow/gusts, first cells start causing route deviations.
First hit (16–19Z): rapid TS intensification; the field intermittently unavailable; diversions spike.
False recovery then second hit (19–01Z): brief improvement prompts debate; then stronger TS cluster redevelops with ramp stops and renewed airport closure.

Scenario Timeline & Injects

Inject 1 — 1105Z Weather Briefing (DCC Meteorologist)
DCC MET: “We’re seeing initiation west of the Metroplex earlier than the models suggested. Confidence is medium that storms will impact arrival corridors by 16Z. Stakeholder Dialogue Set 1 — “Stop Now vs Keep It Moving”
Use this as a scripted telcon (student must control the call).

Telcon Start Script (Student “Controlling the Conference”)
Student (Chair): “Stand by—this is the DFW weather coordination line. Goal is a decision in the next five minutes. I’ll go facility-by-facility, then airlines. Please state: current acceptance rate, trigger for stop, and expected duration.”

D10 TRACON
D10: “Current acceptance 72, but not stable. If the cells clip final or we lose both west gates, we go stop. I can’t support holding.”

ZFW
ZFW: “Gate structure is collapsing west side. If D10 stops, airborne holding will cascade quickly. I recommend a GS now to protect airborne, and a GDP for the bank.”

DFW Tower
Tower: “We’re still launching, but lightning is close. Ramp may restrict intermittently. If ramp stops, arrivals will back up on the surface.”

AAL Dispatch
AAL: “We want a lower controlled rate. If you keep launching to a field that stops, diversions spike. We can support a GDP, but we need it soon.”

UAL Dispatch
UAL: “Agree—if it’s going to oscillate, don’t do rolling stops. We’d rather take ground delay than divert.”

Instructor Expected Student Output: Student summarizes in one statement and assigns next action:
“I’m issuing a 1st-tier GS effective immediately due to imminent loss of west gates and lightning risk; building a GDP to start at XXZ with rates tied to the TEMPO TSRA window; next update in 20 minutes.”

Counts/Indicators (Instructor provides verbally):
Diversions in progress: 6 and rising.
Surface: departure queue increasing, ramp slowdowns.

Instructor Prompt: “What scope GS do you choose (1st tier vs broader), and what’s your criterion to expand?”

Expected Response:
Start 1st tier if uncertainty exists; expand if stop persists beyond 30–45 minutes and airborne holding risk increases from farther centers.

Airline Dispatch (SWA): “Do not cancel the program. Every time you cancel, we surge and then you stop again.”

Decision Point B

Cancel GS and rely on tactical metering, and/or
Convert to GDP with conservative rates.

Instructor Note: If student cancels too aggressively, the second hit will create a larger diversion spike.

KDFW 271715Z 21020G32KT 2SM TSRA BKN012CB OVC040 23/21 A2979 RMK AO2 LTGICCG OHD

UAL: “We want even lower—32—until the ramp opens.”

Student Task: Build an Adaptive Plan
Available TMIs (Student may choose combination)
Ground Stop (1st tier; expand if needed)
GDP (stepped rates aligned with TEMPO TSRA windows)
GDP Revision (based on observed improvement/deterioration)
Advisories / SWAP coordination (as supporting actions)
Controlled recovery release plan (metered releases out of stop)

Instructor Prompt: “Given the oscillation, how do you prevent a surge when conditions briefly improve?”

Expected Answer Elements:
Keep GDP in place through the highest-risk window.
Use conservative step-ups; require facility confirmation before rate increases.
Use releases in blocks; coordinate triggers with D10/Tower (e.g., ramp open + stable final + gates available).

Stakeholder Dialogue Set 2 — GDP Rate Negotiation Under Diversion Pressure
GDP Revision Telcon Script (Student Leads)
Student (Chair): “We are in TSRA with ramp restrictions and an arrival stop. Objective is to reduce diversions and avoid repeated rolling GS. Facilities first: state when you can take arrivals and at what rate.”

D10: “If ramp is closed, arrivals are essentially zero. When ramp reopens and final is workable, I can start at 24–32 for one hour, then reassess.”

Tower: “Ramp closure is the limiter. Expect at least 30–45 minutes of no movement once lightning is inside five.”

ZFW: “We can’t feed you through blocked gates. Plan for constrained flows even after the field reopens.”

UAL Dispatch: “Request 32 until we see stable arrival acceptance.”

Student Required “Conference Control” Technique (scripted)
Student: “I’m going to summarize and decide: For the next hour, planning arrival capacity 32 due to ramp and gate constraints. If we get a verified ramp open and stable finals for 20 minutes, we’ll step up to 44, then reassess. If ramp stays closed, we remain in GS and the GDP will absorb delay. Any questions or concerns with that plan?”

State decision
State trigger(s)
Invite questions without reopening debate

By late in the scenario, the student should have:

Prompt: Generate a "Day in the NAS" scenario for the Southeast/Florida region during a holiday peak. The student must manage extreme volume and saturation using sequential GDPs, AFPs, and holiday Gulf routes while coordinating across multiple centers (ZMA, ZJX, ZHU).
Scenario:
Day in the NAS — Southeast/Florida Holiday Peak (ZMA/ZJX/ZHU)  
Scenario Level: Severe Weather / National Traffic Management (High Complexity)  
Shift: 0800Z–1600Z (Holiday Peak)  
Student Role: DCC/TMU National Desk (Southeast/Florida focus) with full access to TSD/FSM/FEA/AFP tools, Advisory Database, and playbook routes.

Background / Scenario Setup (0800Z)
It’s a major holiday travel day with heavy eastbound and southbound demand into Florida from the Northeast, Midwest, and Texas. Load factors are high; airlines are minimizing cancellations and strongly prefer initiatives that reduce diversions.

At shift start, the NAS is already tight:
Morning bank demand into MIA/FLL/PBI/TPA/MCO is above typical peak.
ZMA is running close to saturation on arrival streams and internal departures (MIA/FLL/PBI).  
ZJX is managing heavy north-south flows over JAX/OMN/CTY corridors.  
ZHU is feeding Florida over the Gulf with limited deviation options if weather builds.

Initial Weather (Holiday Pattern):  
Moist unstable airmass with sea-breeze convection expected later. Early in the shift, ceilings are VFR at the Florida terminals, but the forecast supports convective development along both coasts late morning into afternoon. Gulf convection potential may require structured routing.

Shift-Start Checklist (Student Must Complete by 0810Z)
Instructor Prompt: “Before you decide on any initiatives, run your holiday-peak opening check.”

Expected Student Actions (checklist):
Open/verify TSD demand loads for MIA/FLL/PBI/MCO/TPA, plus ATL/CLT/IAD/EWR/JFK connectivity.
Review FSM for Florida metros and note any existing miles-in-trail, SWAP, or flow constraints.
Pull FEA for ZMA/ZJX/ZHU corridors: JAX gate complex, Gulf feeds, and Bahamas/Caribbean routes.
Check Advisory Database for active GDPs/GS/AFP/CTOPs (none at 0800Z).
Review playbook route library for:
Holiday Gulf Routes (Gulf-to-Florida structured routes)
ZJX/ZMA north-south congestion routes
Identify first decision points:
When convective impacts begin (TAF VCTS windows)
Volume saturation thresholds (hourly demand vs. acceptance rates)
Prepare initial stakeholder call list: ZMA TMU, ZJX TMU, ZHU TMU, major carriers (AAL/UAL/DAL/SWA/JBU), ATCSCC supervisor/NTMO.

Assessment Question (0815Z)
Instructor: “Based on the demand picture and the forecasts, which airport(s) and which airspace/facility interfaces are most likely to hit capacity first today?”

Expected Answer (what the trainee should identify):
Primary risk is South Florida terminal complex (MIA/FLL/PBI) due to holiday volume + forecast TS windows 16–22Z.  
Secondary risk is MCO/TPA (central Florida convection window 18–23Z) with downstream impacts to ZJX/ZMA handoffs.  
Airspace interfaces likely to saturate first: ZJX→ZMA, ZHU→ZMA, and north-south flows through ZJX.

Operational Timeline & Injects

1) 0830Z — Demand Spike / Early Saturation Concerns
Inject: TSD shows an early surge of departures from Northeast and Midwest to Florida. Airlines are “pushing the bank” early to beat storms.  

Instructor Prompt: “Your first two hours are VFR. With holiday volume, do you do nothing, start planning a GDP, or pre-stage an AFP?”

Student Options (Instructor accepts any, but must be defended):
Option A (Wait/Monitor): No TMI yet; build triggers and prep calls.  
Option B (Early GDP Planning): Draft conditional GDP(s) for MIA/FLL and/or MCO keyed to convective windows.  
Option C (AFP Planning): Pre-build AFP templates for ZJX/ZMA constrained flows for later use.

Expected Skill Focus: articulate triggers, avoid premature over-control, but plan for rapidly escalating holiday impacts.

2) 0900Z — First Coordination Call (Facilities Only)
Student initiates a facilities-only call to frame the day.

Stakeholder Dialogue (Facilities Only)
Student (DCC/TMU): “ZMA, ZJX, ZHU—holiday demand is heavy into Florida today. I’m seeing the first bank compressed and the TAFs carry VCTS mid-shift. I’m looking for your expected acceptance rates and your earliest concern time.”

ZMA TMU: “We’re fine right now, but we’re going to be running tight. If we lose any arrival gates to TS, the MIA/FLL/PBI complex will stack fast. We need early heads-up if you’re considering initiatives.”

ZJX TMU: “North-south is already thick. If everyone tries to beat the weather, we’ll saturate JAX/OMN flows. We can stay ‘wide open’ early, but later we’ll need structure, especially if the east coast sea breeze line builds.”

ZHU TMU: “Gulf feeds are manageable now, but if convection pops over the central Gulf, we’ll need holiday Gulf routes. We can’t take large-scale deviations plus peak volume without metering.”

Instructor Note: If student fails to summarize a plan and next check-in time, prompt: “Control the call—set expectations, time hacks, and triggers.”

Expected Student Technique (conference control):
Summarize: “No TMIs now; next update at 1030Z; triggers at first sustained deviation or rate drop.”  
Assign: “ZMA to advise earliest rate reduction estimate; ZHU to advise Gulf route readiness; ZJX to advise gate congestion points.”

3) 1030Z — Weather Confidence Diverges / Airline Pressure
Inject: DCC meteorologist indicates typical sea-breeze timing, moderate confidence; airlines want to “ride the VFR” and avoid early programs.

Meteorologist Briefing (script)
DCC Met: “We’re still VFR now. Timing remains consistent with the TAFs: VCTS developing late morning into afternoon, with the greatest operational risk being cell mergers near the coastal metros. Confidence is medium on coverage and timing; high confidence that impacts will occur somewhere in the Florida peninsula between 16Z and 23Z.”

Instructor Prompt: “What initiatives are available to you today, and what is your selection logic given holiday volume?”

Expected Answer: Student should cite: GDPs (airport-specific), AFPs (airspace constraints), SWAP/structured routing (holiday Gulf routes), tactical reroutes, and—if necessary—Ground Stops into GDPs.

4) 1130Z — First TMI Decision Point: Sequential GDPs Begin
Inject: ZMA reports arrival compression beginning to appear due to increasing in-trail on internal departures and pre-convective route clustering.

ZMA TMU call (script):  
“Command Center, we’re starting to see compression building. If this stays on trend, we’re going to need some form of demand management into the South Florida metro before the convective window opens.”

Instructor Prompt: “Do you issue a GDP now, and if so, for which airport(s) and why sequential vs. combined?”

Student Options:
Option A: Single MIA GDP first (largest driver), then add FLL if needed.  
Option B: Metro approach: MIA + FLL GDPs coordinated to prevent spillover diversions.  
Option C: No GDP yet; prep AFP for ZJX/ZMA constraints.

Expected Outcome (preferred for this scenario): Student chooses sequential GDPs—start with MIA, with a planned follow-on GDP for FLL if demand shifts.

Telcon #1 — GDP Proposal (Facilities + Users) at 1200Z
Student runs the call and must practice “controlling the conference” techniques.

Controlling the Conference (Instructor expects these behaviors)
Start with: purpose + time hack + data points.
Present: demand, forecast, proposed start/stop, and expected revision triggers.
Ask: one targeted question per stakeholder (avoid open-ended debate).
Close: restate decision, note objections, set next update time.

Scripted Dialogue
Student (DCC): “All stations, Command Center. Purpose is to discuss a possible MIA GDP due holiday demand saturation and forecast convective impacts. Time hack 1200Z. I’ll take facilities first, then users. ZMA—current acceptance and your earliest rate risk?”

ZMA TMU: “Right now we can run normal, but once storms start building near the metro, we’ll lose the ability to keep the streams clean. If everyone pushes early, we’ll have airborne holding and diversions.”

ZJX TMU: “From our side, we can feed now. Later we expect route clustering and gate saturation. If you’re going to do something, do it early enough to prevent a pile-up.”

ZHU TMU: “Agree. If the Gulf gets active, we’ll be forced into structured routing. We’d rather meter than deviate everybody.”

Student (DCC): “Users—AAL, DAL, UAL, SWA—any requests?”

AAL: “We want to avoid diversions into already-full alternates. If there’s a program, we want it early and stable so we can plan swaps.”

DAL: “We prefer not to start too early. But if you’re going to run it, keep revisions minimal.”

UAL: “Holiday loads are high. We can support a GDP if it prevents a ground stop and limits airborne holding.”

SWA: “We’re concerned about Florida alternates. We prefer lower rates later rather than a late ground stop.”

Student (DCC): “Decision: issuing an initial MIA GDP now with a planned reassessment at 1330Z. Trigger for expansion to FLL is sustained convection impacting coastal arrival corridors or ZMA acceptance reduction. Any final facility concerns limited to that scope?”

ZMA TMU: “No further concerns. We’ll advise immediately if acceptance changes.”

Instructor Note: If the student lets the call devolve into rate bargaining, inject:  
Instructor (as Supervisor): “Bring it back—summarize operational risk, then close with decision and triggers.”

5) 1330Z — AFP Trigger: ZJX North-South Saturation
Inject: ZJX reports north-south corridor saturation with widespread reroutes around early convection offshore and increasing military airspace interaction.

ZJX TMU (script):  
“We’re getting saturated on the north-south flows. We can’t just keep opening the faucet into ZMA. We need an AFP for the ZJX/ZMA interface or we’re going to lose the ability to manage the streams.”

Instructor Prompt: “Do you layer an AFP on top of the GDP(s), and what risk are you managing?”

Expected Answer: Student recognizes:
GDP manages airport acceptance;  
AFP manages airspace constraints and route clustering feeding Florida;  
holiday volume requires both, sequenced and coordinated to avoid whiplash.

Expected Action: Student implements an AFP targeting primary Florida-bound flows through ZJX (north-south constraints) while maintaining the earlier GDP.

Telcon #2 — AFP Implementation + Holiday Gulf Routes (Facilities + Users) at 1400Z
Conflict Required: ZHU wants holiday Gulf routes early; some airlines push back to avoid extra fuel/time.

Scripted Dialogue
Student (DCC): “Purpose: ZJX saturation and Florida feed management. We are implementing an AFP for the constrained ZJX flow into ZMA and coordinating holiday Gulf routes as needed. Time hack 1400Z. Facilities first.”

ZJX TMU: “We need structure now. We can’t safely absorb this volume with ad hoc deviations. AFP will let us meter and reduce compression.”

ZMA TMU: “Agree. Without metering, we’ll see holding and diversions into limited alternates.”

ZHU TMU: “We recommend implementing holiday Gulf routes now to protect the Gulf feeds. If convection develops west of Florida, deviations will spill into our core sectors.”

AAL: “We can accept Gulf routes if they’re stable and published early. We want predictability more than shortest time.”

DAL: “We need to minimize additional flying time. If Gulf routes are used, keep them limited to flows that actually need it.”

UAL: “Support earlier structured routes. We’re already seeing customers file around weather; we prefer you make it consistent.”

Student (DCC): “Decision: AFP goes active now for ZJX constraints feeding ZMA, with holiday Gulf routes implemented for ZHU-to-Florida flows that would otherwise deviate into constrained areas. We’ll reassess at 1500Z and adjust scope based on observed deviations and facility acceptance.”

ZHU TMU: “That works. We’ll coordinate specifics on the affected city pairs.”

Instructor Note: Student must explicitly state: scope, time window, and revision criteria.

6) 1500Z — Escalation: Sequential GDP Expansion + Diversion Risk
Inject: Convection begins to impact coastal metro arrival corridors consistent with the TAF TEMPO windows (MIA/FLL) and later central Florida. ZMA reports increasing arrival vectoring and unstable arrival rates; airlines request lower rates to limit diversions and stop the “push.”

ZMA TMU: “We’re starting to lose the clean arrival gates. If this builds, we may need additional demand management into FLL and possibly MCO later.”

Airline (AAL) request: “We want lower rates rather than late ground stops. Diversions are the worst outcome today.”

Instructor Prompt: “How do you sequence initiatives from here: add GDPs, revise AFP parameters, and expand holiday Gulf routes?”

Expected Action (scenario flow):
Add FLL GDP (sequential expansion) to protect alternates and reduce spillover.
Maintain/adjust AFP to manage ZJX/ZMA interface.
Keep holiday Gulf routes in effect for ZHU feeds.

End-of-Shift (1600Z) — Student Handoff Requirements
Instructor Prompt: “Brief the oncoming shift in 3 minutes—weather, TMIs, and next triggers.”

Expected Handoff Content (must include):

ZMA metro arrival gate impacts and alternates sensitivity
Next triggers:
If central Florida convection increases: consider MCO/TPA demand management
If Gulf convection intensifies: expand holiday Gulf route scope
If ZJX constraints worsen: tighten AFP parameters / extend time window
User impacts:
Airlines prefer stable, earlier initiatives to prevent diversions and rolling ground stops
Next scheduled coordination:
1630Z facilities check
1700Z users telcon (if needed)

Instructor Grading Focus (embedded throughout)
Uses tools (TSD/FSM/FEA/AFP) to justify actions with demand + forecast.
Proper sequencing: GDPs first for airport acceptance, AFP for airspace saturation, routes for structural stability.
Conference control:
Purpose/time hack
Facility-first then users
Targeted questions
Decision + scope + triggers
Note objections without yielding control
Holiday considerations:
Diversion minimization

Stable initiatives over frequent revisions

END OF SIMULATION

We’re initiating a GDP effective 14Z to keep demand below acceptance and prevent ZOB holding/diversions. We’ll reassess for escalation if runway treatment or braking action forces a closure.”

3) Escalation Call (Around 1535Z) — “GDP No Longer Works”

Prompt: Generate a summertime convective weather scenario for MIA involving gate closures and Ground Stops.
Scenario:
MIA Summertime Convection — Gate Closures, Ground Stops, and Recovery

Background / Scenario Setup (Start 1400Z)
Facility/Role: Command Center TMU / Terminal East desk (MIA primary).  
Shift Time: Scenario begins 1400Z (summer weekday).  
Tools Available: FSM, TSD, ATCSCC advisories interface, CCFP/TCF, NEXRAD mosaic, SWIM/ASDE-X (surface trend), airline customer line.

Operational Baseline at 1400Z
MIA is in a normal daytime arrival bank. Surface ops are steady, no initiatives in place.
Sea-breeze convection is expected to initiate west of the field and rapidly expand; uncertainty exists on whether storms remain inland (gate impacts only) or drift over the airport (full stop potential).

Initial METAR/TAF provided at position assumption
KMIA 141353Z 11010KT P6SM SCT025 SCT050 29/24 A3006 RMK AO2
*KMIA 141130Z 1412/1518 11012KT P6SM SCT025 SCT050  
  FM141600 12014G20KT P6SM VCTS SCT030CB BKN050  
  TEMPO 1417/1421 3SM TSRA BKN020CB OVC040  
  FM142100 14010KT P6SM VCSH SCT025 BKN050  
  PROB30 1500/1504 2SM TSRA BKN015CB  
  FM150500 09008KT P6SM SCT030*

Forecast Narrative (Instructor briefing at 1405Z)
Expect thunderstorms to build west/northwest of MIA with outflow boundaries producing quick wind shifts and rapid intensity changes.
The primary impact is arrival/departure gate loss (west/northwest) followed by possible airport shutdown if a core crosses final/surface.

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “Based on the current weather and the 16–21Z TEMPO TSRA window, what specific capacity and routing risks do you expect for MIA in the next 2–4 hours? Which gates are most likely to be affected first?”

Expected Trainee Answer (key points)
Gate impacts likely before field impact; expect west and northwest gate closures first with reroutes to east/southeast flows where available.  
Anticipate arrival rate reductions due to deviations, spacing, and potential stops; risk of diversions if holding builds and the field goes down.  
Prepare for short-fuse Ground Stops (1st-tier then expand) and GDP out of the stop if convection persists.

Timeline & Injects (Instructor-Controlled)

Inject 1 — 1430Z: Early Build / “Looks Manageable”
Weather Update (METAR):
KMIA 141430Z 12014G20KT P6SM VCTS SCT030CB BKN050 30/24 A3004

Instructor Cue: Show developing cells 30–50 NM WNW moving E, tops increasing.  

Decision Point A (options for the trainee)
No TMI yet; monitor and pre-coordinate with ZMA/N90-style routing partners (here: ZMA sectors, MIA TMU).  
Tactical reroutes for west gate arrivals/departures.  
Pre-plan Ground Stop criteria (triggers) and draft a GDP model for rapid transition.

Instructor: “What are your triggers for a Ground Stop versus tactical management only?”

Expected Answer Elements
GS triggers: arrival gate closure with sustained inability to deliver, holding exceeding threshold, field lightning/ramp closure, or final blocked.  
Tactical only if deviations small, gates still usable, and delivery rate stable.

Inject 2 — 1535Z: ZMA Reports Gate Degradation
ZMA TMU call (scripted)
ZMA TMU: “We’re starting to see heavy deviations in the northwest arrival streams. Two of our primary feeds are getting pinched. We can still move some aircraft, but it’s becoming one-way traffic around the cells.”

Instructor Prompt: “What additional information do you need from ZMA right now?”

Expected Trainee Questions
“Which fixes/routes are unusable and for how long?”  
“Are you running any miles-in-trail? What’s the current acceptance rate you can deliver?”  
“Are departures impacted the same way—any departure gates blocked?”  
“What’s the likelihood the line drifts onto the final/surface?”

Inject 3 — 1615Z: First Gate Closure + Ramp Lightning
METAR:
KMIA 141615Z 14008KT 6SM VCTS RA BKN025CB OVC050 28/24 A3001

MIA TMU call (scripted)
MIA TMU: “We’ve got ramp lightning; some carriers are pausing ramp for 20–30 minutes. We’re also losing the west departure gate intermittently due to cells.”

Instructor: “Do you move now with a Ground Stop, or continue tactical? State your rationale.”

Expected Actions (acceptable paths)
Path 1 (Proactive): Issue 1st-tier Ground Stop for MIA arrivals for 45–60 minutes to prevent airborne holding/diversions; immediately build GDP to restart at a reduced rate.  
Path 2 (Wait/See): Continue tactical + MIT; prepare GS if arrival counts collapse or ramp closure persists.

Stakeholder Dialogue — Planning Telcon (Target 1630Z)

Telcon Setup (Instructor Role)
Instructor (as ATCSCC Planner): “We’re convening for MIA convective impacts. Goal: stabilize demand, minimize diversions, and align on triggers for stop/restart.”

Participants: MIA TMU, ZMA TMU, ATCSCC Weather Unit, Airline Dispatch (AAL/UAL/DAL as users).

Weather Brief (ATCSCC Weather Unit)
ATCSCC WX: “Convection is organizing west-northwest of MIA with repeated redevelopment along outflow. Confidence is medium that the west/northwest arrival and departure corridors will remain impacted through 21Z. Uncertainty: whether the strongest core drifts over the field between 18–20Z.”

Instructor Prompt to Trainee: “Restate the weather in operational terms—what does ‘medium confidence’ mean for your plan?”

Expected Answer
Plan for sustained gate restrictions, build flexible initiatives (GS-to-GDP), and avoid overcommitting to a fast recovery until trends support it.

Facility Positions (Conflict/Pressure)

ZMA TMU (capacity-driven)
ZMA: “We can’t keep feeding you reliably through the northwest. Deviations are growing and we’re starting to get compression. If you don’t slow demand, we’ll build holding and you’ll see diversions.”

MIA TMU (surface/ramp-driven)
MIA: “Ramp is intermittent. If arrivals keep coming while gates can’t work, we’ll gridlock. I need you to meter hard or stop them until we can move the surface.”

Airline User Request (diversion avoidance)
AAL Dispatch: “We’d rather take delay on the ground than diversions. If you anticipate a stop, do it early and come out with a controlled rate. We can support a lower rate if it prevents airborne holding.”
UAL Dispatch: “Agree—avoid stacked airborne holding. Give us clarity on restart timing; we’ll manage swaps and holds.”

Instructor-Driven Decision Requirement
Instructor: “Choose one:  
1) Tactical management only,  
2) Ground Stop into GDP, or  
3) GDP without a stop.  
Then state your start time, scope, and the first revision trigger.”

Expected Outcome (recommended for training)
Ground Stop into GDP to prevent holding/diversions with gate/ramp impacts and increasing uncertainty.

Execution Script — Ground Stop and Transition to GDP

1640Z: Implement Ground Stop (if selected)
Trainee actions (evaluation points)
Select scope (start 1st-tier; be prepared to expand if weather grows).  
Set duration 45–60 minutes with review time.  
Coordinate with ZMA and MIA TMU; issue advisory.

MIA TMU (response)
MIA: “Copy. The stop will help the surface. Call me before you cancel; we need a controlled restart.”

1705Z: Conditions Worsen — Airport Impact Risk Increases
METAR:
KMIA 141705Z 17018G28KT 2SM TSRA BKN012CB OVC030 27/24 A2998

ZMA TMU
ZMA: “We’re now effectively blocked west and northwest. Any aircraft that try to penetrate are turning back. This is not sustainable.”

Instructor Prompt
“Do you extend the stop, expand scope, or convert directly into a GDP with very low rate? Defend your choice.”

Expected
Extend GS and publish GDP out of the stop with conservative initial rate until gates reopen and ramp resumes.

Recovery Telcon (Target 1730Z): Coming Out of the Stop

Facility Updates
MIA TMU: “Ramp is still intermittent. We can accept a restart but slow. We need a controlled sequence.”
ZMA TMU: “We can deliver from the east/southeast only; west side remains blocked. Expect deviations for at least another hour.”

Airlines Push for Predictability
AAL Dispatch: “Keep the restart rate low and steady; we’re trying to avoid diversions and gate conflicts.”
UAL Dispatch: “We need a clear step plan—don’t jump too fast.”

Instructor Requirement
Instructor: “Announce your restart plan: first hour rate, step-up logic, and the trigger to revise upward or cancel.”

Expected Answer Elements
Initial low restart rate (conservative) due to blocked gates + ramp.  
Step up only when:
ZMA reports two arrival corridors usable,  
ramp restrictions end,  
observed arrival throughput stabilizes for ≥30 minutes.

Final Inject — 2000Z: Improvement and GDP Revision Opportunity
METAR:
KMIA 142000Z 13010KT P6SM VCSH SCT025 BKN050 29/24 A3003

ZMA TMU
ZMA: “We’re getting gaps now. West side is reopening with deviations. We can increase delivery.”

MIA TMU
MIA: “Ramp is open. Surface is moving again. We can take more.”

Instructor Prompt
“Revise/cancel initiatives and outline your notification/coordination steps.”

Expected
GDP revision upward or cancel if stable; ensure all required coordination calls and advisories are completed.

Instructor Notes / Performance Objectives
Evaluate the trainee on:
Early identification of gate-driven constraints vs field shutdown risk.  
Clear triggers for tactical vs GS vs GDP decisions.  
Professional telcon control: concise weather restatement, operational translation, and a firm plan.  
Diversion avoidance strategy: prioritizing ground delay over airborne holding when appropriate.  
Correct sequencing of: GS issuance → coordination → GDP modeling → revision/cancel → notifications.

End of Simulation
Scenario ends after the trainee issues the final revision/cancellation and briefs the status: current weather trend, expected stability window, and remaining risk (PROB30 follow-on convection if applicable).

Prompt: Generate a high-demand event scenario for LAS complicated by gusty, variable winds and runway changes.
Scenario:
LAS High-Demand Event Scenario — Gusty/Variable Winds & Runway Changes (Level 2/3 Terminal)

Background / Scenario Setup (Start 1030Z)
Position: ATCSCC TMU (Terminal West Desk) supporting LAS and adjacent constraints (ZLA/ZLC interface).  
Shift Start: 1030Z. Student has access to TSD/FSM/NTML, surface metering view, and reroute monitor.

Operational context:  
A major early-morning event surge (convention departures + sports charter push) drives banked demand at LAS. The day starts with manageable VFR, but gusty/variable winds are expected to drive multiple runway configuration changes, producing abrupt capacity swings and surface congestion risk.

Initial observed weather (1030Z):  
LAS is VFR with increasing wind variability; winds are beginning to “hunt” with gusts building. Tower reports intermittent tailwind components on the primary departure runway when winds swing.

TAF (issued prior to sim start):  
KLAS 121020Z 1210/1312 22008KT P6SM FEW120  
  FM121200 24012G22KT P6SM SCT120  
  TEMPO1213/1217 20020G32KT 5SM BLDU SCT080  
  FM121700 16012G26KT P6SM SCT090  
  TEMPO1218/1222 14018G30KT 5SM BLDU BKN080  
  FM122300 VRB06KT P6SM FEW090  
  FM130300 02010G18KT P6SM SCT090  
  FM130700 25012G20KT P6SM SCT100

METAR sequence to be used by instructor (injects):
