1315Z (BLDU begins): KLAS 121315Z 21022G33KT 6SM BLDU SCT090 20/04 A3001  
1705Z (wind shift): KLAS 121705Z 16013G27KT 10SM SCT090 24/06 A2996  
1900Z (peak SE gusts): KLAS 121900Z 14020G32KT 5SM BLDU BKN080 26/06 A2993  
2330Z (lull/variable): KLAS 122330Z VRB05KT 10SM FEW090 22/05 A2996  
0315Z (north wind): KLAS 130315Z 02012G20KT 10SM SCT090 19/04 A2999  
0730Z (back to W/SW): KLAS 130730Z 25014G22KT 10SM SCT100 17/03 A3003

Demand picture (instructor brief):
1130–1400Z: departure push (event charters + convention), high IFR mix inbound/outbound.
1600–2100Z: heavy arrivals (connections + reposition), plus continued departures.
Key risk: each runway change triggers stops/starts, taxi congestion, and missed-approach/go-around risk due to gust spread and crosswind/tailwind swings.

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “Based on the TAF/METAR trend and the demand bank, where do you expect capacity issues first: LAS arrival rate, LAS departure rate, or both—and during which window?”

Expected trainee answer (target):
Capacity issues begin as early as 13–17Z due to gusty winds/BLDU causing configuration instability and increased spacing.
Both arrival and departure rates become constrained, with the worst disruption during 18–22Z when SE winds/gusts peak and surface congestion increases.

Training Objectives (What the student must demonstrate)
Recognize config instability as a capacity reducer even in VFR.
Use TM tools to anticipate runway change impacts and manage demand before gridlock.
Coordinate effectively with LAS Tower/TRACON and ZLA/ZLC on rate changes, stop/slow initiatives, and reroutes.
Select among multiple TMI options and adjust as uncertainty resolves:
No action / monitor
Advisory only
Tactical MIT / minutes-in-trail to meter banks
Ground Delay Program (GDP) for LAS (arrival-focused or combined)
Short-duration Ground Stop (GS) during runway flip
Reroutes to deconflict with departure headings during config changes

Initial Student Actions (1030–1115Z)
Instructor instructions: Give the student 10 minutes to:
Pull LAS FSM, current AAR/ADR, and demand histogram (next 6 hours).
Identify likely runway config sensitivity with winds “hunting.”
Draft a decision tree: “If winds exceed X gust spread or tailwind observed → do Y.”

Instructor prompt: “What are your triggers for moving from ‘monitor’ to a TMI?”

Expected elements in answer:
Trigger on: repeated runway changes inside 60–90 minutes; rising taxi times; departure delays trending +20/+30; tower reporting tailwind components; BLDU lowering vis; required spacing increase.
TMI options and escalation plan.

Stakeholder Dialogue & Inject Timeline

1115Z — Facility Call: “We want to stay open”
LAS Tower (agent): “LAS Tower. We’re still west flow, but winds are getting gusty and variable. We can run it for now. We’d like to keep the push moving.”

L30 TRACON (agent): “We’re good wide open at the moment. If we keep flipping, it’ll start to hurt. What do you need from us?”

Instructor prompt to trainee: “What do you ask next before you commit to any initiative?”

Expected trainee asks:
“Are you anticipating a runway change window/time?”
“Any observed tailwind components or crosswind limits being approached?”
“What’s your current acceptance rate and what’s realistic if you flip?”
“Surface status: taxi-out time, gate holds, EDCT compliance impacts?”

1315Z — Weather Deterioration Inject (BLDU + gusts)
METAR inject to student: KLAS 121315Z 21022G33KT 6SM BLDU SCT090 20/04 A3001

L30 TRACON: “We’re starting to see more spacing on finals due to gust spread. Departures are getting intermittent holds for crossing. If winds keep this up, we may have to reconfigure.”

LAS Tower: “We’re seeing occasional tailwind on the departure runway when it swings. It’s workable now but it’s not stable.”

Instructor: “You have a heavy departure bank still on the surface and the next arrival bank is building. Pick an action: (A) monitor, (B) tactical MIT, (C) GDP proposal, or (D) short GS for the flip.”

Expected decision logic (acceptable paths):
Path 1 (tactical first): Implement targeted MIT from key feeders to smooth arrival peak while keeping departures moving.
Path 2 (preemptive GDP): Build GDP starting prior to the worst gust/flip window to prevent airborne holding and diversions.
Path 3 (wait + react): Monitor until runway change declared, then GS into GDP (higher risk; allowed but must be defended).

Telcon #1 (1410Z) — “Do we meter now or later?”
Participants: ATCSCC Desk (student), LAS Tower, L30 TRACON, ZLA TMU, ZLC TMU, Airline System Ops (SWA/UAL/AAL), ATCSCC Weather Desk.

Student technique requirement (controlling the conference):
Open with purpose + timebox: “This is a 5-minute coordination to align on LAS capacity and decide if we need metering before the next bank.”
State current facts (METAR, observed impacts) + forecast uncertainty (winds continue gusty; shift expected).
Propose one primary plan and one contingency.
End by asking: “Any objections or operational constraints not considered?”

Scripted dialogue:

Student (expected): “We’re seeing KLAS winds 210 at 22 gust 33 with BLDU, and TRACON reporting increased spacing and intermittent departure holds. Demand peaks through the next two hours and again 16–21Z. I’m proposing we start with tactical metering and be prepared for a short GS if a runway flip is executed.”

ATCSCC Weather Desk: “Confidence is medium on sustained gusts through 22Z with a noticeable wind shift toward 160 degrees around 17Z and peak gust potential around 19–21Z.”

L30 TRACON: “If we stay west, we can keep it moving, but the variability is the killer. If it goes SE and stays there, we’ll have to re-align flows and we’ll lose some throughput during the transition.”

ZLA TMU: “We’re concerned about climb/departure headings during a configuration change. If LAS flips, we’ll need a clean window to re-sort departures to the correct gates.”

ZLC TMU: “We can absorb some reroutes but not if we get a sudden stop-start. Give us a heads-up if you’re going to run a short stop.”

SWA System Ops: “We’d rather see a controlled rate now than a bunch of airborne holding later. If you’re going to cap arrivals, do it early enough to protect turns.”

UAL System Ops: “Agree. If a runway change is coming, we’d support a lower arrival rate plus a brief stop during the flip rather than diversions.”

LAS Tower: “We can give you a 20-minute runway change window if needed, but we don’t want to do it twice.”

Instructor prompt: “What is your plan and what is your contingency? State it as a clear decision.”

Expected trainee output:  
Primary: tactical MIT + advisory now; prepare GS window if flip occurs.  
Contingency: GDP if instability persists into 17–22Z peak, to protect holding/diversions.

1705Z — Runway Configuration Stress (wind shift realized)
METAR inject: KLAS 121705Z 16013G27KT 10SM SCT090 24/06 A2996

LAS Tower: “Winds have shifted. We’re initiating runway reconfiguration. Expect reduced acceptance during the transition.”

L30 TRACON: “We need a clean window. If you can stop arrivals briefly, we’ll restart on the new flow with workable spacing.”

Instructor: “Student must choose: issue a short GS, meter with MIT only, or absorb holding. What do you do?”

Expected action:  
Issue short-duration GS (scope as appropriate) to protect the transition, then come out with a controlled rate (MIT or GDP depending on recovery).

Telcon #2 (1730Z) — “ZAU-style gate concerns translated to LAS: departure headings/gates”
Participants: Student, LAS Tower, L30, ZLA, ZLC, Airlines.

ZLA TMU: “If you try to stay wide open during the flip, we’ll get departure gate blockages and we won’t be able to take the volume. We need you to smooth it.”

L30 TRACON: “We want to stay as open as possible, but we can’t take the surges during the changeover. Give us a controlled ramp-up.”

Airline (SWA) request: “If you’re going to run controlled rates, keep them low enough to avoid diversions. We’d rather see a conservative posture than a whipsaw.”

Student (expected): “We’ll run a 20-minute arrival stop during the reconfiguration, then restart with metering for one hour while we verify stability. If winds continue to hunt or BLDU reduces vis further, we’ll convert to a GDP to protect the 19–22Z bank.”

1900Z — Peak Gusts + BLDU (Airport nearing shutdown posture)
METAR inject: KLAS 121900Z 14020G32KT 5SM BLDU BKN080 26/06 A2993

LAS Tower: “Visibility is down with blowing dust and winds are gusting 32. We’re having trouble keeping a consistent operation. We may have to pause depending on surface control.”

L30 TRACON: “We’re compressing hard. We can’t sustain the planned rate. If this persists, we need you to formalize it.”

Instructor: “At this point, do you convert to a GDP, extend GS, or run MIT only? Defend it.”

Expected trainee decision (target):
Convert to GDP (or revise existing) to protect the system through the unstable 19–22Z window, with the understanding that runway stability is not guaranteed and revisions may be required.

Resolution / Recovery (2330Z onward)
METAR inject: KLAS 122330Z VRB05KT 10SM FEW090 22/05 A2996

LAS Tower: “Winds have dropped off. We can stabilize and recover.”

L30 TRACON: “We can step rates back up. Surface is still catching up, but we’re moving.”

Instructor end-state: Student should:
Coordinate lifting/relaxing TMIs in an orderly way (avoid over-release).
Brief adjoining facilities and airlines on recovery timeline.
Document key decisions, triggers, and lessons.

Instructor Notes / Grading Focus
Evaluate the student on:
Early recognition that runway changes + gust spread reduce capacity even in VFR.
Clear, concise conference control and decision framing.
Proper escalation/de-escalation of TMIs: monitor → MIT → GS (flip) → GDP (peak) → recovery.
Coordination quality with Tower/TRACON and ZLA/ZLC to prevent stop-start surges.
Ability to incorporate airline priorities (diversion avoidance) without ceding operational control.

End of Simulation
Instructor: “Brief the oncoming specialist: current TMIs, expected wind trend, remaining demand banks, and your triggers for canceling or revising initiatives.”

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)

Instructor: “Based on the current KSAN METAR/TAF trend and the coastal pattern, which airports or sectors will face capacity issues first, and what is your initial TMI posture for SAN arrivals in the next 60–90 minutes?”

Expected Trainee Response (key points):  
Primary constraint: KSAN arrival acceptance due to FG/OVC002–004 and variable vis.  
Secondary constraints: SNA/CRQ likely similar; potential ripple to ZLA arrival sectors feeding SAN and to alternates’ surface/gates.  
Initial posture: Consider SAN Ground Stop (targeted scope) if vis trends toward 1/2SM FG OVC002 and arrival rate becomes unstable; prep GDP if duration likely beyond a short stop; coordinate alternates early.

Instructor Notes (Run the Simulation)
Student must choose among options:  
  1) No TMI / Monitor (risk: holding/diversions).  
  2) Tactical MIT/Expect Departure Releases (risk: inconsistent compliance).  
  3) SAN Ground Stop (GS) followed by GDP if IFR persists.  
  4) GDP only (risk: too slow to prevent airborne holding if deterioration is rapid).  
Instructor should present realistic “pressure” from facilities and airlines: tower wants immediate stop; airlines want to avoid “random diversions” and prefer an orderly program.

Decision Point 1 (D1 0845Z): Weather Deterioration

Update METAR (D1 0845Z):  
KSAN 0845Z 24003KT 1/2SM FG OVC002 16/16 A3001

Stakeholder Dialogue (Prompt/Expected Answer Pairs)

SoCal TRACON (Approach):  
TRACON: “Command Center, SoCal. SAN is dropping hard—1/2 mile fog, ceiling 200. We’re starting to see missed approaches. We need an arrival plan now. Recommend a Ground Stop.”  
Expected Student: Confirm current acceptance rate, missed approach frequency, and whether any arrivals are still getting in; propose 1st-tier GS immediately to stop near-term airborne holding and uncontrolled diversions; state intent to transition to GDP if trend persists.

SAN Tower:  
Tower: “We’re on the edge of CAT I mins. If this holds, we won’t be able to keep arrivals continuous. Gate space is okay for now but we can’t absorb diversions returning.”  
Expected Student: Acknowledge; confirm runway configuration, approach mins, and current arrival count; advise tower of GS issuance and expected review times.

ZLA (Center TMU):  
ZLA: “We’ve got arrivals already committed from the north and east. If you don’t stop it, we’ll hold and start spilling into adjacent sectors.”  
Expected Student: Coordinate: “We are issuing SAN GS now; advise holding parameters; request ZLA identify aircraft closest-in to manage exceptions and coordinate alternates.”

Airline Dispatch (Alaska/United/Southwest typical mix at SAN):  
Airline: “We want predictability. If this is going to be 3–4 hours, a GDP is better than rolling diversions.”  
Expected Student: “Understood. Immediate GS to arrest airborne risk; we’ll work a GDP proposal within 20 minutes based on TAF trends and reevaluate at 13Z improvement window.”

Action Required (D1): Implement GS → GDP

Step A: Ground Stop (D1 0850Z)
Instructor: “What scope and duration?”  
Expected Student:  
Issue SAN GS (start immediate, duration through ~1200Z with next review at 0930Z), scope sized to prevent short-haul churn while not over-stopping long-haul already airborne (student may choose 1st-tier + select 2nd-tier depending on demand and airborne list).

Step B: GDP Proposal (D1 0910Z)  
Weather rationale: TEMPO FG/OVC002 through 12Z; slow improvement around 13Z but lingering low ceilings possible through 17Z.

Instructor: “Build a GDP that protects against holding and limits diversions. What rate profile do you propose?”  
Expected Student: A stepped rate profile: very low during worst period (08–12Z), gradual increase after 13Z, with a contingency if 13–17Z stays IFR.

Telcon #1 (D1 0920Z): GS Continuation + GDP Start Time

Participants: ATCSCC (student), SoCal TRACON, SAN Tower, ZLA TMU, LAX TMU, Airlines (WN/UAL/ASA/DAL optional), ATCSCC Weather.

Scripted Dialogue

ATCSCC Student (Chair):  
“SAN marine layer has dropped to 1/2SM FG OVC002. We’ve initiated a Ground Stop to prevent holding and diversions. Objective now is a structured GDP out of the stop. I’ll go around the room—state constraints, then we’ll align on rate and start time.”

ATCSCC Weather:  
“Marine layer is entrenched. Confidence is moderate that improvement begins around 13Z, but ceilings may hover 400–600 with intermittent vis restrictions through 17Z. A second deterioration is possible near 03–06Z tonight.”

SoCal TRACON:  
“We support coming out of the stop into a GDP. Even if we get marginal improvement at 13Z, it’ll be unstable. We need a conservative rate until we’re clearly above 1,000 and 3.”

SAN Tower:  
“Agree. We can’t run normal acceptance in OVC002–006. Keep it controlled to prevent surface saturation.”

ZLA TMU:  
“From a Center perspective, we want a clean program so we can stop building holding stacks. Also, please coordinate alternates—LAX is not going to be a relief valve.”

LAX TMU:  
“Correct. We’re constrained on gates and taxi. We can take some diversions but not a wave. Strong preference that SAN runs a program and uses inland/desert alternates.”

Airline (Southwest/United):  
“We want lower rates early to limit diversions and keep the recovery predictable. We also need clarity on when you’ll reassess.”

Expected Student (Close/Decision):  
Confirm: GS remains until GDP in effect.  
Announce: GDP start time aligned to worst conditions; set review cadence (every 60 min) with clear triggers (e.g., ceiling ≥010 and vis ≥3 for sustained period).  
Note alternates strategy and coordination with LAX/ONT/PSP.

D1 Recovery Phase (1700–2200Z): Partial Improvement, Backlog, and Gate Saturation

D1 METAR (1805Z):  
KSAN 1805Z 28009KT 6SM BR SCT012 BKN025 18/16 A3004  
SAN begins to accept more arrivals, but the morning diversions and late departures have created imbalanced banks, crews out of position, and inbound “recovery push” from PHX/LAS/ONT.

Instructor Inject (D1 1830Z):  
SAN calls: “We’re improving, but gates are tight because several diverted aircraft are repositioning back simultaneously.”  
Airline calls: “We want priority for recovery flights.”  
ZLA warns: “If you release too fast, you’ll re-create holding if BR returns.”

Expected Student:  
Use GDP revision strategy: incremental increases rather than a jump to normal.  
Coordinate with SAN Tower/TRACON on arrival acceptance vs gate availability.  
Emphasize staged releases and EDCT compliance.

DAY 2 (D2): Repeat Marine Layer + Diversion Recovery Complication

Background / Scenario Setup (D2)

D2 Shift Start: 0800 UTC (following morning).  
Carryover: aircraft and crews are displaced due to D1 diversions. Many airlines filed conservatively; alternates include LAX/ONT/PSP/LAS/PHX. Some aircraft remained overnight away from SAN.

METAR KSAN 0800Z: KSAN 0800Z 24005KT 2SM BR OVC005 16/16 A3003  
METAR KSAN 0900Z: KSAN 0900Z 24004KT 1SM BR OVC003 16/16 A3002

Operational Twist (D2):  
Airlines have fewer spare aircraft/crews.

Assessment Question (D2 Instructor Prompt)

Instructor: “Given the D2 forecast repeats the D1 marine layer timing, what’s your plan to avoid a second day of uncontrolled diversions while still enabling recovery flying?”

Expected Response:  
Proactive GDP early (before conditions crater) to shape demand.

Decision Point 2 (D2 0915Z): Lowest Vis + Diversions Already Happening

Update METAR (D2 0915Z):  
KSAN 0915Z 23004KT 3/4SM FG OVC002 16/16 A3002

Stakeholder Dialogue

“Second day in a row. We cannot do a repeat of yesterday’s holding. We want a GDP now, and if it drops further we’ll need an immediate stop.”

ZLA TMU:  
“Also, we’re getting calls from adjacent sectors—yesterday’s recovery pushed traffic late. Don’t over-release when it starts improving.”

Airline Dispatch:  
“We cannot afford diversions today—crew and gate constraints are already maxed. We request a conservative arrival rate early. We’d rather take ground delay than divert.”

Expected Student:  
Announce GDP start time promptly, with conservative early hours and a step-up plan at the 19Z improvement window.  
Establish clear “do not exceed” release guidance tied to gate availability.

Telcon #2 (D2 0930Z): Program Alignment + Diversion Recovery Controls

ATCSCC Student (Chair):  
“Objective: prevent holding/diversions and manage recovery constraints. We are shaping demand with a GDP now rather than chasing weather. I’ll take SAN acceptance first, then ZLA constraints, then airlines.”

SAN Tower:

SoCal TRACON:  
“We need steady, predictable demand. A step-up is fine once we see stable improvement, not before.”

ZLA TMU:  
“We’ll support with meter fixes, but don’t plan on tactical fixes saving the day if the marine layer oscillates.”

Airlines:  
“Request lower early rate and a controlled recovery window. If improvement is later than 19Z, don’t jump the rate.”

Expected Student (Close/Decision):  
Confirm: GDP in effect with conservative early rate, staged recovery, explicit review times.  
Set trigger language: “If KSAN remains at or below OVC004 with vis ≤1SM, we maintain low rate; if sustained improvement, we revise upward.”

Diversion Recovery Inject (D2 1500–1900Z): Alternate Saturation + Return-to-Field Surge

Instructor Inject:  
ONT reports ramp congestion: “We’ve got multiple SAN diversions still on the ground; turn times extended.”  
LAX TMU: “We’re no longer accepting additional diversions without coordination.”  
Airlines request preferential handling for “diverted aircraft returning to SAN” causing a potential inbound surge.

Expected Student:  
Coordinate a controlled “diversion return” flow: hold returns on EDCTs, avoid stacking SAN.

Instructor Evaluation Points (Both Days)

Look for the trainee to demonstrate:
Early recognition that marine layer fog/low ceilings can produce sudden drops below mins and rapid missed approaches.  
Selection of appropriate TMI sequencing (GS to stop immediate risk; GDP to structure longer impacts).  
Clear telcon control: summarize weather/ops, go-around the room, restate decision and review time.  
Diversion mitigation: early alternate coordination, avoiding reliance on LAX, using inland/desert alternates.  
Recovery discipline: no “rate whiplash”; revisions tied to sustained METAR improvement and gate capacity, not optimism.

End-State / Completion Criteria

Simulation ends when the student:
Successfully issues a D1 GS → GDP transition without creating extended holding.  
Manages D1 recovery with controlled rate increases and gate awareness.  
Proactively runs D2 GDP early, minimizing diversions and preventing an uncontrolled return-to-field surge.  
Conducts two effective telcons with facilities/airlines, articulating weather-driven triggers and recovery strategy.

Prompt: Generate a severe convective weather scenario for DFW involving high unpredictability and significant diversions.
Scenario:
Severe Convective Weather Training Scenario — DFW (High Unpredictability / Diversions)

Background / Scenario Setup (Shift Start 0800Z–1200Z Window, Exercise Begins 1000Z)
Position: ATCSCC — Severe Weather / Strategic Traffic Management (DFW Focus)  
Start Time: 1000Z (student assumes position)  
Tools Available: TSD, FSM, NTML/SVRWX log, CEDAR, Advisory DB, ASD/ASPM views, reroute monitor, playbook library, airline chat lines, TFMS demand tables.

Operational Context:  
DFW is in a pre-convective environment with a nocturnal MCS remnant boundary draped west-to-east across North Texas.  
Multiple models disagree on convective initiation timing and coverage; confidence is low on exact placement but high on disruption potential.  
The primary training objective is managing rapidly changing capacity, diversions, and initiative escalation under uncertainty (tactical → strategic), while maintaining consistent stakeholder comms.

Initial METAR/TAF packet provided at position relief:  
METAR KDFW 101953Z 16012KT 10SM FEW060 SCT250 30/22 A2994 RMK AO2 SLP132 T03000217  
*TAF KDFW 101720Z 1018/1124 17012KT P6SM SCT060 SCT250  
  TEMPO 1020/1023 VRB20G35KT 3SM TSRA BKN030CB  
  FM102300 18010KT P6SM VCTS SCT040CB BKN080  
  PROB30 1102/1106 2SM +TSRA OVC015CB  
  FM110600 19008KT P6SM SCT040 BKN080  
  TEMPO 1110/1115 3SM TSRA BKN025CB  
  FM111600 21012G18KT P6SM SCT050*

D10 / DFW ATIS trend summary (briefed by instructor):  
Convection is not yet on the field, but lightning is expected within 30–90 minutes.  
Ramp closures are likely; airlines are already asking about diversion recovery gates at AUS/IAH/OKC/ICT.

Assessment Question (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “Based on what you see at 1000Z, which NAS elements are most likely to become capacity-limiting in the next 4–6 hours: the airport, the terminal airspace, the enroute gates, or downstream diversion airports? Walk me through what you’d watch first and why.”

Expected trainee answer (key points):
DFW airport acceptance rate will become volatile due to TS onset/ramp closures (not just ceilings/vis).  
D10/TRACON constraints likely before full field shutdown: arrival gate compression and departure gate deviations.  
ZFW/ZHU flow constraints and playbook needs as storms expand/translate.  
Diversion airports and gate/ramp capacity become limiting once ground stops start and arrivals are trapped airborne.

Scenario Timeline & Injects
Instructor should maintain uncertainty: storms “build, split, and redevelop” and do not follow a single clean line.

Inject 1 — 1030Z: Early Development / First Deviations
Weather update (ATCSCC met brief over phone):  
“Cells are firing west of the Metroplex earlier than expected. Tops increasing fast. Outflow boundaries could trigger secondary development closer to the field.”

METAR update:  
METAR KDFW 102030Z 17014G22KT 8SM VCTS SCT050CB BKN120 29/22 A2992 RMK AO2 LTG DSNT W-NW SLP123 T02940222

Instructor Prompt: “What’s your posture: do nothing, issue a CCFP-based advisory, start coordinating a GDP, or prepare for a tactical ground stop?”

Decision Options (student chooses):
Tactical posture: no program yet; begin coordination calls with D10/ZFW; monitor airborne holding potential.  
Preemptive GDP modeling: build a draft GDP with an uncertain start and a conservative low-rate window (but do not publish yet).  
Advisory + reroutes: coordinate preferred routes/playbooks for west/south departures to avoid developing cells.

Inject 2 — 1105Z: Ramp Risk / Airlines Start Positioning Diversions
DFW Tower (role-play): “Lightning within 10 miles expected. If we go ramp closed, departures will stop moving. We can land for a while but can’t guarantee gates.”

Airline Dispatch (UAL/AAL/SWA combined line):  
“We’re seeing significant inbound banks. If you don’t slow this down, we’ll be forced into mass diversions. We want an early rate reduction and we want you to protect AUS and IAH capacity for recovery.”

Instructor Task: student must decide whether to:  
Implement a GDP,  
Issue a targeted Ground Stop (tiered or full scope), or  
Wait and manage tactically with MIT/swap/fix balancing.

Inject 3 — 1130Z: First Capacity Drop / Gate Saturation Begins
D10 TRACON:  
“We’re compressing. Deviations are increasing on the west side. We can still run, but not at normal rates. If ramp closes, arrivals will stack and then divert.”

ZAU-style conflict not used here; instead introduce a forecast conflict:  
ZFW Weather Unit: “Expect growth into a broken line; coverage will increase through 13Z–16Z. Confidence medium-high.”  
ATCSCC Meteorologist: “I’m not convinced it fills in. We may see cells merge briefly, then gaps. Confidence medium on diminishing coverage after 14Z.”

Instructor Prompt: “How do you communicate uncertainty without sounding indecisive, and what triggers do you set for escalation?”

Expected trainee behaviors:
State operational triggers (e.g., ramp closure, arrival holding > X minutes, diversions exceeding Y per 30 min, loss of specific gates/routes).  
Propose staged initiatives: advisory → GDP proposal → GS if ramp closure/field impacts.

Stakeholder Dialogue — GDP/GS Planning Telcon (1200Z)
Student runs the conference. Instructor evaluates concise control, sequencing, and decision framing.

Student “Controlling the Conference” Techniques (must practice)
Open with: purpose + time box + decision required.  
Provide: weather summary, demand picture, current constraints, proposed plan, then solicit concerns.  
Use: “I’ll go facility-first, then users. Hold questions until the end unless safety-critical.”  
Close with: decision, actions, next update time, and confirm who is responsible for what.

Telcon Script
Student (Chair):  
“ATCSCC severe weather, this is [Name]. This is the DFW weather telcon. Objective is to decide whether we need a program starting in the next 60–120 minutes to reduce holding and diversions. I’ll go D10/DFW/ZFW first, then users.”

DFW Tower:  
“We’re still landing and departing, but lightning is trending toward the field. If ramp closes, gate turns stop. We cannot support an arrival push into a ramp closure.”

D10 TRACON:  
“Right now we’re workable but deviations are increasing and compression is building. If the convective core moves over the final or the departure gates, we’ll drop hard. We want to stay as open as possible, but we need protection from an arrival surge.”

ZFW Center (TMU):  
“Our biggest issue is gate integrity. We’re already seeing reroutes and tactical deviations. If this grows into a broken line, we lose throughput and can’t feed D10 consistently. I’m concerned we’ll be holding aircraft in ZFW airspace with no gate availability at DFW.”

ATCSCC Meteorologist (role-play):  
“Storms are initiating earlier, but I still see potential for gaps later. Confidence is not high on a continuous line. There’s a real chance this becomes pulsing cells with temporary improvements.”

ZFW Weather Unit (role-play):  
“We expect area growth with more coverage through the mid period. Outflow interactions favor redevelopment closer to the field.”

AAL Dispatch:  
“We’re asking for lower rates early to prevent diversions. If we keep sending arrivals and then ramp closes, we’ll divert dozens. We can accept delay on the ground; we cannot absorb diversions.”

UAL Dispatch:  
“Agree. Give us a conservative plan. We request you plan as if the airport will be unusable for a period. If you don’t, we’ll be diverting to already saturated alternates.”

SWA Dispatch:  
“We need predictability. If you keep changing the plan every 30 minutes, we can’t recover. We support an early controlled rate to protect system recovery.”

Student (Chair — summarize and propose):  
“Here’s what I’m hearing: facility concern is gate blockage and holding, users are prioritizing diversion prevention. Given forecast uncertainty, I’m proposing a staged approach:  
1) GDP with a reduced rate window covering peak convective risk,  
2) clear trigger for a Ground Stop if ramp closure or field impacts occur,  
3) scheduled update in 30 minutes with a weather refresh.  
Any safety-critical objections before I finalize the proposal?”

ZFW Center:  
“No objection, but we want the start early enough to avoid airborne holding.”

D10 TRACON:  
“Support, but don’t lock us into an overly low rate if gaps open—be ready to revise quickly.”

AAL/UAL/SWA:  
“Support. Lower is better early; we can recover later.”

Implementation Pathways (Instructor Chooses Based on Student Decisions)

Path A — Tactical First (Minimal TMI → Escalate)
Trigger: student elects to wait; weather worsens faster than expected.

Inject (1235Z):  
METAR KDFW 102235Z 21018G32KT 2SM +TSRA BKN015CB OVC030 25/22 A2988 RMK AO2 PK WND 22035/2232 LTGICCG OHD SLP115 T02500222

D10 TRACON: “We just lost the west gate. Finals are compromised. We’re about to stop arrivals.”  
DFW Tower: “Ramp closed. No gate turns.”

Expected student actions:
Implement arrival Ground Stop (tiered first if workable; expand if diversions begin).  
Immediately model GDP out of GS with cautious recovery rates.  
Coordinate diversion management messaging (AUS/IAH/OKC/ICT) and protect alternates from overload.

Path B — GDP Early (Controlled Rate → Field Shutdown)
Trigger: student issues GDP earlier to keep demand manageable.

Inject (1245Z): storms still shut down the field; GDP must convert to GS.

Expected student actions:
Convert posture to GS when acceptance drops to near zero.  
Communicate clearly: GDP remains for recovery; GS is for the immediate zero-capacity period.  
Plan for revision cycles as storm pulses create brief reopenings.

Diversions & Recovery Management Injects
Inject (1315Z): Diversions Accelerate
ZFW Center: “We have multiple in-trail holding with minimum fuel concerns developing. Diversions to OKC and AUS increasing.”  
AAL Dispatch: “AUS is nearing gate saturation; we need you to stop feeding AUS as an alternate.”  
Instructor Prompt: “What do you do to avoid ‘diversion gridlock’?”

Expected trainee actions:
Re-coordinate alternate strategy: distribute diversions (IAH/ACT/SAT/OKC/ICT) based on capability.  
Communicate to facilities/users: which alternates are constrained; recommend refile targets.  
Use tactical advisories/reroutes to reduce airborne arrivals to the constrained alternates.

Key Instructor Evaluation Points
Does the student set triggers tied to measurable operational impacts (ramp closure, holding minutes, diversion count, gate loss)?  
Does the student keep the telcon structured (facility → weather → demand → proposal → objections → decision)?  
Does the student avoid “forecast arguing” and instead choose a risk-managed plan with revision points?  
Does the student manage alternates proactively to prevent secondary failures?

End-State / Success Criteria
Simulation ends when the student has:
Implemented and adjusted initiatives as storms evolve from VCTS → TSRA → shutdown.  
Managed a high-diversion environment with alternate protection and clear comms.  
Conducted at least one controlled telcon and delivered a clear decision with triggers and next-update time.  

Instructor Closeout Question:  
“After the event, what would you brief to the next shift about: (1) what’s driving capacity, (2) diversion status and alternate health, (3) what initiatives are active/queued, and (4) what your next trigger is for de-escalation?”

Controlling-the-Conference Technique (Student Script)
Student (expected):  
“Alright, I’m going to run this call in a tight format. I’ll start with weather, then facility constraints, then user impacts, then I’ll state a proposed path forward and take questions. Please hold comments until I call your facility or airline.”

Some airlines want lower rates early; others want to “run it until it breaks.”

Airline Requests (Script)

Student Decision Point (Instructor Prompt)
Instructor: “Based on what you have at 1245Z, what initiative(s) do you implement, if any?”  
Allowed Options:
No TMI yet; remain tactical with reroutes/MIT.  
GDP for MCO only (most sensitive to interior storms + holiday load).  
GDP for MIA/FLL (east coast sea-breeze impacts expected).  
AFP concept for ZMA/ZJX constrained flows (advanced option; high workload).  
Tiered approach: tactical now + pre-built GDPs “parked” for quick issue.

Instructor Expected Outcome: Student chooses a defensible strategy with triggers and communicates it clearly.

11) Instructor Notes / Evaluation Points

Critical Skills to Observe
Uses tools to quantify risk (demand vs acceptance) rather than intuition only.  
Establishes triggers and communicates them before the crisis point.  
Controls the telcon: structure, time discipline, clear decision statement.  
Distinguishes between: “weather at the field” vs “airspace/gate impacts.”  
Manages saturation: prevents airborne holding/diversions by selecting appropriate TMIs.  
Plans for stability: avoids excessive revision churn unless data compels it.

Common Failure Modes (Instructor Should Probe)
Waiting too long because METAR is VFR while demand is already saturated.  
Over-programming too early without acknowledging uncertainty and triggers.  
Allowing telcon to become a debate instead of a decision forum.  
Not coordinating diversion risk (JAX/RSW/PIE/MLB) and downstream impacts.
